chapters.title: Introduction
paragraphs: February 14, 2024
(Amanda Evans)

There I sat at the bar of Toro Ardiente, Love City’s finest Spanish skybar, wearing that low-backed white dress I had bought the previous week specifically for Anton's sake. To this day, I still have a vivid memory of the evening of our first shared birthday, when I poured a little more into my glass than a lady probably should have, whispering things into Anton's ear that I deny to this day whenever he brings them up. Naturally, this is because I am absolutely certain he is just making them up—always something different; somehow, those yearly-recalled phrases never quite match, despite his constant insistence that those were indeed the words that escaped my lips.
"Take me to Paris, and do with me what you will," I breathed soundlessly,

at least according to him. Then, also according to him, I fell asleep for half an hour, only for him to wake me saying we had arrived. Obviously, we hadn't reached Paris in thirty minutes, but it was also true that we had a place of our own that we called just that. Of course, it wasn't because it bore any resemblance to Paris, but because we planned to buy it and build our own Eiffel Tower on it—a petty revenge of sorts on the original for not being able to go, even though back then, we wanted to so desperately.

I was wearing a similar white dress back then, and I could practically see him trembling with excitement—that this slightly underdressed girl was sitting in this car just for him, resting her head on his shoulder as she slept, waiting only for him to undress her from the gown he so admired as a man, yet had absolutely no idea how to unwrap from someone he so desperately wanted to make his own. In those days, we didn't sleep together; we only wound up in long, wild kissing sessions, sitting in the car for hours until we ended up with our necks covered in black-and-blue marks, or whatever else we found bare from the cover of our clothes. No one could have accused us of restraint. The big event didn't happen then either, but I still remember those eyes that devoured me completely, shining like the sun, even though I clearly recall it was pitch black.

What year was that? I don't know, and I won't bother calculating; the result would only depress me. Yet, the woman sitting at the bar now was far more attractive than ever before—though believe me, there were never any complaints about my appeal before Anton, either.

I was on my second Martini, fiddling with the ring on my right hand, wondering what character Anton would choose today for our shared roleplay. He had already dressed up as a misunderstood poet, a successful programmer with visions, a university professor—granted, I knew more than he did about his chosen field of expertise (why on earth did he choose law?)—a CEO, and of course, anyone in whose skin he felt he could make a truly positive impression on me. And let’s face it, he played every role well, succeeding in seducing me with each, though less because of the character itself and more due to the clear eyes peering from behind every mask—eyes in which I always saw that genuine love that swept me off my feet in an instant.

There I sat at the bar, the softly bitter taste of the dry drink lingering in my mouth, when in the low cut of my dress, I suddenly felt the touch of a hand on my back, sliding deeper and deeper. Like a sudden electric shock, an old memory flashed through my body, and the dry taste of the Martini was replaced by the lightning-sharp shiver of remembrance.
"Are you cold?" Anton asked. "You are beautiful! This dress is incredible... or rather, you are..."

Anton loved to compliment me his whole life, which I really adored, and like most men prone to compliments, he occasionally overdid it, while at other times he wasn't careful enough. At the beginning of our relationship, I told him several times that I was glad if my dress achieved its purpose, but if he couldn't see the woman underneath and wanted to get aroused by the fabric, he could easily solve that in a boutique—I wouldn't stand in his way. On such occasions, he usually tried to correct the praise, which only made things worse.

I didn't play this game with him out of self-indulgence. On my part, it wasn't even a game. I truly believed there had to be real substance behind the words, and complimenting the clothes alone wouldn't win me over. This attitude was deeply coded into me, but believe me, I had my reasons for being critical of a compliment. It took years before I could take Anton's words at face value. He knew this too, yet he possessed that—for me, rather fortunate—trait of trying to understand what I meant, why I said what I did, and why I reacted to things the way I did. At the start of our relationship, although everything with him was divine, I didn't believe a word he said, even though he truly did everything for me.

He, of course, absolutely loathed the idea that I thought he loved me for anything other than myself. Obviously, I had hit the mark with the white dress, and it felt wonderful that he made it known—exceedingly gently, yet firmly—that he found me irresistible. I felt a bit sorry that he corrected himself, though it was entirely my fault; I had spoiled him, and now he was probably thinking that I took the praise of the dress as superficiality—but then again, how long ago were those times? They faded faster than a person can even remember, leaving only faint fragments, fleeting mental snapshots of the events back then, when I rested my head on his shoulder as I slept.
"No need to apologize," I replied. "I would only be offended if you hadn't complimented this dress. It's exactly as I wanted it to be; I wanted to please you, and perhaps turn your head a little."
"There was never any doubt you could do that," he answered. "You know, Amanda, you would have had just as much success without the dress as with it."
So there it is. My earlier nitpicking with his compliments led to the birth of such gems.
"So you like me?" I asked again, just to keep the conversation going.
"Very much so!" he answered firmly. "In fact, I like you so much that I'd love to take you right now to that place we always wanted to go."
"Surely not Paris?" I asked back, my voice laced with excitement.
"We go to Paris every year," he replied. "You've seen the Eiffel Tower at least five times by now."
"But that's not the one I meant, Anton."
"Oh," he said, surprised. "You mean *our* Paris."
"What did you mean?" I asked.
"The Corinthia Royal on Napoleon Boulevard."
"Not bad either."
"Do you think so? I wouldn't want to offend you with it."
"Anton, if you're trying to say that on Valentine's Day your only desire is to be with me in some little nook of the Corinthia Royal..."
"Actually, I booked a room, but a nook sounds good too."
"Excuse me, sir! What a crucial difference. So, if you want to make me yours in a cozy little room at the Corinthia Royal..."
"It's actually a suite."
"And excuse me once again! Apologies for not immediately pegging this price bracket. So, in one of the suites at the Corinthia Royal..."
"In the presidential suite, Amy" he specified conclusively.
"Where on earth?" I asked, my voice cracking with surprise.

We had indeed always wanted to go to the Corinthia Royal, even though it was right here in the city. Aside from the fact that spending even a single night within its walls seemed to cost thousands, that was the other factor holding us back. Who on earth rents a room in the city where they live? True, I was always drawn to the idea of not having to travel anywhere, but just *being* there at last. Much as I wanted to explore the countries of the world, the mere thought of the exhausting travel required made me instantly feel unwell.
"You rented the presidential suite at the Corinthia Royal?" I asked.
"Yes."
"Then tell me, what the hell are we still waiting for, Anton?"

The question was obviously rhetorical. I left my Martini on the table, grabbed Anton's arm, and began dragging him toward the exit. That was when it hit me that I hadn't paid for the drinks, but turning back, I saw from Anton's raised eyebrow that he had already treated this breathtakingly attractive lady to two drinks at the bartender's when he arrived. So be it, I thought to myself, because if not, I would certainly be running off without paying now. I didn't want to waste another thought on the drinks, and Anton didn't add words to his suggestive facial expression; he was so proud of himself for getting me this worked up. He even lifted my coat from the hanger—I didn't even put my arms in; he just draped it over me in the elevator as the two doors slid open on the ground floor amidst passionate kisses. Gilded ornaments covered the elegant walls.
When we stepped out of the building, I looked for Anton's car, but couldn't find it.
"Where are you parked?" I questioned with feigned authority.
"I didn't park today, Amy" he replied with an annoying grin on his face.
"You didn't come by car?" I was surprised, starting to worry that my enthusiasm might fade.
"Of course" he replied, pointing to a sleek white limousine parked in front of the skybar.
"Are you serious?" escaped my lips—but it wasn't the uniqueness of the situation that captured me, rather the precise organization that characterized Anton on such occasions.
"Step inside, milady!" he opened the door, then took my hand to help me in.
"By the way, what did you dress up as today?" I asked, changing the subject.
"A president's husband," he smiled, and that wasn't too far from the truth.

The exclusivity of the evening left nothing to complain about. The mere listing of the skybar, the dress, the limousine and the Corinthia Royal presidential suite gave me the feeling that we were special characters on a special day, and when Anton himself said the word, the window separating the limousine driver magically rolled up. Anton was sitting on my right, holding his left arm around my waist, and gently turned me towards him, stroking it with his right hand. The dress was very short, barely reaching the middle of my thighs, and I watched with pleasure as Anton measured it inch by inch so that he could fully take in my view, which was also a gift. The dress was also accompanied by a pair of white high-heeled shoes, the laces of which seductively crawled up almost to my knees. I knew what set it off. Anton loved my legs, so I was happy to draw his attention to them on similar occasions, and when he firmly grabbed my left ankle and pulled it over his right shoulder, I knew that white would checkmate quite easily today.

Anton was wonderfully quick at switching from one position to another, and I took pleasure in how he found the way to me through the openings of various clothes. Now too—since he didn't want to undress me, lest he put me in an awkward position before the chauffeur when the latter politely opened the door and I still had to dress—he simply pushed the dress up above my bottom with his right hand, then slid his fingers between my legs, drawing a sigh heavier than a breath from me. He began to caress me sensually. He knew exactly where to touch to make me feel in heaven, and barely half a minute later, uncontrolled moans escaped my throat. The chauffeur undoubtedly heard our action, yet when he appeared at the rear door about ten minutes later, the same polite discretion showed on his face that one expects from someone performing such a service in similar situations.
The Corinthia Royal shone like a thousand stars among the elegant buildings of Napoleon Boulevard...

chapters.title: Chapter One
paragraphs: (Anton Martin)
January 11, 2022
"Did she file the complaint?" I asked, as if it were the most natural thing in the world.
"She did."
"Do you want to talk about it?"
"No."
"And are you okay?"

She didn't answer that, but I didn't question her further either. I already knew the answer; the question itself was completely foolish when I thought about what they had to go through up until the day they decided they would indeed file a complaint.

No matter how much Amy had been driven for years by the hope of finally putting an end to this whole affair, when she faced the fact that the time had come and everything was set, the weight of the entire world's uncertainty suddenly crashed down upon her. I sympathized with her for this, and I understood why they had been putting off the complaint for months. It must be the hardest thing—to bring yourself to leave your entire past life behind, to settle scores with everything you once were, with everything that actually made you who you are today. It’s like settling scores with yourself. In Amanda's case, however, this reckoning had taken place long ago—or perhaps it never even had to, because she was never the person they believed her to be. There was always someone inside her who knew that the situation she was in was not the same as the person experiencing it. Amanda was rather returning to her former self, and this finding-her-way-back had already begun when we first met.
"On Thursday, Jess is going to the police," she said. "And I want to meet her."

It wouldn't have been surprising if I hadn't known that they hadn't seen each other in quite a few years. There was a good reason they avoided the other, though it wasn't easy for either of them. That Amy wanted to meet Jessica didn't surprise me, but that she would meet her even more so.
January 13, 2022
She got home in the early afternoon.
"We were at Starbucks. They have a new chocolate cookie."
"You could have brought one," I replied. "I haven't even had breakfast yet."
"Whose fault is that?"
"Mine, obviously," I admitted. "Now what?"
"You're not eating a chocolate cookie at noon," she teased.
"I mean with the complaint."
"What about it?" she asked back. I absolutely loathed when she did that.
"Amy, I want to know what's going to happen. I don't know how these things work. I'm a writer, not a lawyer. Should I be worried?"
"Why should you be?" she asked curtly, as if she didn't understand what my problem was.
"Could you get into trouble?"
"No. Why would we? It's just a police complaint. A lot of complaints are filed in a day. Do you even know how many?"

Obviously, I didn't. Amanda sometimes didn't take my worries seriously. After a few hours or days, she would then bring them up and be willing to talk about them. It was then that I realized she understood my concern very well, but she just didn't know what to do with it. The whole thing was consuming her just as much as it was me, and she was finding the answers just as little as I was. Of course, she would never admit this; instead, she considered it useless worrying, something to be pitied, if not laughed at. At first, I used to get angry at her for this, but over time, I learned that this is just how she works, and that was that.
"A complaint doesn't mean anything yet," she continued. "This is the very beginning of everything."
"I'm just worried," I replied.
"There's no reason to be. I'm a prosecutor. The entire police force is behind me. You know that's the way to get the bad guys behind bars."
"I'm still getting used to you chasing bad guys" I smiled. "What you're doing is really cool, Amy."

Cool. What a stupid word that is. As if Amanda ever wanted to be a prosecutor so her fiance would think it was cool. Not. Amy already decided that it would be when, against her will, they did things to her for the first time that she didn't even know existed.

Who was Jessica? In the most superficial way, I could say that she is Amanda's best friend. But in this case, I should tell you when they first met, how long they've been good, why they decided to be friends, etc. However, I could not answer any of them. Amy always referred to Jess as her sister to me, even though they were most definitely not related. But in an orphanage, those who get there at a very young age are likely to form a bond as close as that between real siblings. So Jess was Amanda's sister, only more. They were inseparable not only in the home, but also outside of it. They could count on each other forever.

Even before they were born, clouds had begun to gather over both of their lives, and by ending up in a children's home, they might even have considered themselves lucky. Neither knew anything about their parents, though it was hardly their fault that things weren't exactly cozy in the home either. The years spent there, despite representing stability, gave no hope—at least not the kind of hope both of them could have longed for.

Unsurprisingly, once they were mature enough to believe they were capable of making good decisions, an organization that preyed on children's home residents took them under its wing on a promising day in May. Finally, they no longer had to deal with the house warden, who had made both of their lives increasingly unbearable for years, and they could stay together with Jessica too.

The work really didn't seem difficult at first. A certain part was invented for them. In the home, they tried to find some kind of special occupation for everyone, so that they could live an even fuller life, and everyone could find what they were closest to, and for some of them, these separate hours could even be an opportunity to break out. There were those who became professional soccer players, but there were also actors and musicians among them. The Amandas have been studying hip-hop since they were young, which has worked out very well for them. Although Amy never thought of becoming a professional dancer, she loved the classes and loved that in those few hours, she could feel completely free. She felt that she could fulfill herself in dance and show a side of herself to others that she would otherwise have difficulty talking about.

The person who recruited them showed a great interest in dance, and after several meetings, suggested that in the job he was offering, a background in dance was not only an advantage but a prerequisite, so their further development would also serve their interests. This somehow banished the gray bird of uncertainty even more, and trust began to blossom in them, even though from the very beginning they strongly suspected that in dancing, they would probably be disturbed by various pieces of clothing less and less, and that their greatest success would not be achieved as backup dancers in a theater.

Regardless, the outlined job description seemed strictly bounded, and as soon as they matured the moral dilemma within themselves with Jess—because they really had to convince themselves—they embarked on the adventure. They felt that the corruption of the children's home had irreparably started to kill something in them that they considered valuable enough to try and preserve a piece of. They believed they had nothing to lose, and that they had to risk if they wanted to win...

The plan now was for Jessica to file the complaint and Amy to prosecute it. However, this would have been impossible in this form, since Amanda could not represent the prosecution in a case in which she was also involved as a victim. The solution was a lie. They terminated all previous contact with each other, they only carried on encrypted correspondence, and that too rarely, they only knew what they were up to. Amy's job was to finish college, Jessica's job was to follow up on everything. Now is the time to put their plan into action.

Evening was falling. We were lying on the sofa with Amy, watching something; she snuggled up to me, and I held her. We spent many evenings like this. While she was going to university, almost all of our days ended this way. It felt truly good to hold her in my arms. Her scent meant home to me, and that was worth more than anything. Often I just gazed at her in admiration. As our fingers interlocked, her touch flooded me with a vibrant excitement and an infinite peace all at once. I loved this. I always felt the universe had made us for each other, and this connection was coded somewhere deep inside our bodies, because if I closed my eyes, a perfect, idyllic world opened up where it was just the two of us, and everything else ceased to exist around us. I felt, stronger than anything, that I loved her.

chapters.title: Chapter Two
paragraphs: (Amanda Evans)
Morning of January 13, 2022

Jessica and I agreed to meet at the Starbucks on Sycamore Lane after eleven. I was already there at a quarter to ten, bought coffee and cake, and then sat down at a table upstairs so that as few people as possible could see me with Jess. I didn't know when he was coming. I was afraid that things would drag on at the police station, and I would have to wait for hours. I was fiddling with my phone. I checked the time. He was only eleven. I was scrolling through the news. They denied the threat of war. Then everything will be fine, I thought cynically, even though I've been feeling uncomfortable about this for weeks. Like with the covid epidemic, when I was listening to the radio in the car and after reading the news I knew it would reach us. I knew that this was not news like the others, I felt that the announcer's voice from the loudspeakers began to resonate with the strings of reality. I wouldn't have been able to articulate why, but I had a feeling that this would not remain a simple piece of news that no longer needed to be paid attention to. It was the first time I felt this during a news broadcast. This rebuttal also seems to have stepped out of the ranks and been born into the world.

My mind was racing with everything, including Jess. I hadn't seen her in too long. Suddenly, in a blurry reflection on the polished surface of the table, her figure appeared. She was beautiful. Her red hair fell heavily over her shoulders, and she wore a dress under her long coat. I found her choice of outfit, paired with high heels, rather unwarranted.
"Going somewhere?" I asked, which made her smile.
"I didn't expect you to compliment me right away after all these years," she replied with cynical calmness. I sensed disdain in her voice.
Like in any deep friendship, we were capable of experiencing the widest spectrum of emotions in an instant.
"I'm actually angry," I admitted. "You're too conspicuous. I wanted to meet in secret."
"They don't even know who you are here," she reassured me. "I like to look good." With that, she took possession of the chair opposite me.
"You succeeded."
A minute passed before she spoke again.
"Here," she slid a pink flash drive toward me. "I gathered everything I could. But don't get lost in the details. There's enough material on it for years of trials."
Taking it in my hand, I turned the little device over.
"How interesting," I mused. "Our entire lives are on this little gizmo."
"Don't be so sentimental," she corrected me. "It's just data."
"Of course." Another silence followed. I felt unable to make conversation with Jessica. "How long are you staying?" I finally blurted out.
"We are going home to Jeff's parents today, but when I'm needed, I'll come up."
"All right," I replied. I didn't understand what the need was for this now, but it wasn't my job to figure it out. "When are you leaving?"
"Jeff is already waiting downstairs. I just popped up."
"And does he know?" I asked uncertainty.
"What, Amy? Who are you? Or who I am? He knows what he knows about me, you really don't have to worry about that."
"I don't know Jeff," I excused myself.
"You'll get to know him when you have to," she replied.

Before leaving, she reached for my pastry, broke off a piece, and left me. I stared silently at the remaining part, thinking about how we had divided things between us throughout our lives. She always chose first so that I would get more, and she knew that if I started, I would do the same. I looked at this fairly large pastry, which I had originally wanted to give to Anton as a whole, because I was afraid he wouldn't eat until I got home—lately he was always full of worry—but I couldn't take it to him like this. The rest didn't appeal to me. Outside, the wind was blowing, and rain was starting to drizzle. I opened my umbrella, and reaching the tram, I threw its wrapper into the trash. It was too good to eat alone.

After the children's home, Jesse and I started working in a club where, in addition to striptease, we had to go much further with the clients. This unfolded gradually. They always paid close attention to the girls getting deeper and deeper without being noticed. There was nowhere to run. Processing this was the most difficult.

The organization had a very strong network of relationships based on unwritten rules. In this, everyone had their place. The girls worked as prostitutes, and ten men supervised their work, who were called gentlemen. There were also a few boys who entertained male clients. So, women never visited this place.

The girls here were in smaller groups under a gentleman who had to take care of them. This meant dressing, beautifying, and organizing medical examinations. In addition, they also guaranteed the relative safety of the girls. Other demands did not really arise.

At the bottom of the hierarchy among the men were the helpers/associates. They were as if they weren't even here. These boys, besides quick money, basically wanted to be gentlemen, but the queue was too long for them to succeed. Many left the whole thing before they could even get close to gentleman status. We were recruited by such a guy. He was barely seventeen.

At the top level stood a single man. We saw him often, yet he could not be recognized, and barely a few of the girls spoke to him; he was completely untouchable for us. After a while, however, we knew exactly who he was.
One of the girls was named Katherine. She was the girlfriend of one of the gentlemen, even though this wasn't supposed to happen.

Katherine learned the trade well, the clients loved it while they could, but over time, Katherine could only deal with Justice, who did not organize any other clients for her. Many of us thought the gentleman fell in love with the girl, until he shot her in the head right before our eyes. The incident shocked us all. We understood that there is no position in this system where we should not feel like prostitutes at every moment. This realization liberated us in a strange way. It no longer occurred to us for a moment that if we improve, everything will turn for the better with time.

At the time of Katherine's death, an overzealous gentleman called the police, as it was forbidden to try to kill girls. However, the matter was quickly settled, because the boss took care to prevent the breakdown. He set an example for those who would have thought about making independent decisions. There was only one record of Katherine's death, and none of that of the gentleman.
The club was called Neon Nights.
January 14, 2022

Katherine's file was just one of many on Jessica's flash drive. I sat in my dark office, the light of the laptop reflected in my eyes and browsed through the materials that Jess had collected. There were quite a few folders crowded together, several of them had interesting names, but I noticed that most of the folders were password protected. Why? I asked myself the question. What is the need for this? Then I remembered what Jess had said in the coffee shop: Don't get lost in the details. He made sure it was that way.

Jessica summarized very briefly what was on the drive. She wrote a readme file grandiosely, which said only: Mortenstein materials. This was the name of the president she filed a complaint against, who was previously the head of this club.

The first collection contained invoices. Utility bills and debts, all made out in the name of Neon Nights. I found it strange. How could such things accumulate? Were they too lazy to pay? I was puzzled. We earned quite enough for them to have no maintenance problems. By the definition of a sign, this certainly carried a meaning pointing beyond itself, and I could hardly wait to investigate it.

On one folder, the name of a certain Mike Alioglu could be read. I found it an interesting name. Its sound suggested Turkish origin. I read into the materials, and it turned out he was killed. He was set on fire in the park while negotiating with the people of Neon Nights.

Mike was the child of immigrants and died at the age of twenty. He grew up in a family of seven children, the second in line. His father was a bus driver, and his mother managed the family's affairs. They surely moved from Turkey in the hope of a better life, but they soon had to realize that life wasn't easy here either, even if one had a job. Seven kids... I sighed. What drives people to give birth to seven children into poverty? Did they have no other choice, or were there religious reasons behind it? Who knows. I knew I would never have seven children, and I would be happy if even one came along.

Although the boy was a complete stranger to me, I had a feeling as if I knew him. Of course, this wasn't about him personally, but about the situation he had gotten involved in. Based on the documents, Mike was a good guy, helpful, wanted to take care of his siblings, and surely wanted his parents to be proud of him—and when he took home his first paycheck, they probably were. His mother might have feared for him, but... Jesus, how much I speculate, how easily my imagination wanders. I couldn't know what Mike felt, especially not what his mother felt; I only knew he was set on fire for something he did or wanted to do.

I felt sorry for the boy. He had to die too soon, I thought—it's possible he was a decent kid who just saw his family's future as hopeless, so he tried to seize the lucrative opportunity. I know exactly how Jess and I were recruited, and if we, who from the beginning calculated with what awaited us later, said yes to entering, then a helper probably resisted the temptation much less. He too was a victim of this organization, and recognizing this, I couldn't hate him like the others. By his death, he washed himself clean—at least, in this sentimental moment of mine, that is what I felt.

Mike Alioglu was killed in the Liberty district, which was one of the worst neighborhoods in the city. The district, once deserving of a better fate, today rather indicated by its name that you could freely do anything here that you couldn't do elsewhere. Thus, many illegal enterprises cropped up here, and here you could most easily stumble upon a job that wasn't legal. Many poor families lived here, representing a suitable workforce for dubious employers.

It wasn't surprising at all that Mike met with trouble in this district. The way he died, however, was. Even having worked in the profession for years, it was still a rarity to hear of someone being set on fire. I could imagine few more brutal ways to kill a person, and those I could already rightfully raised the charge of sadism. I couldn't imagine what this boy could have done to have such vengeance taken on him.

Anyway, the witness testimony was completely irrelevant to the murder. The only thing that emerged from it was that the witness and the victim were just about to meet in a park when, as they approached the agreed place, Mike's screams filled the grove, and by the time his friend found him, he could only gaze at the burned body. It was dusk, and he saw no one until the cops arrived. He was suspected first, but despite the circumstances, he was able to clear himself. The killer was not found, though there was no chance of that anyway.

I definitely wanted to speak with the witness, even if I was much more interested in what he knew about the Neon Nights than what he experienced in the park. Though the witness's testimony did not help in the precise reconstruction of Mike Alioglu's killing, it did make it clear that Mike was in contact with the Neon Nights network. And why didn't the truth come out in time? Because the investigation was terminated two weeks after Mike's death. I probably don't need to explain how this could have happened. I was sure the club had an insider within the force.

In another folder, I found only a single article. This was accompanied by a note file, but I didn't learn much from it about what this was. A name was in it, and contact info, and as encouragement, a message: there's more where this came from.
Edgar Mortenstein is the modern Maecenas
July 8, 2010
I couldn't read this title without cynicism.

"The great men of our time do not pay enough attention to the arts," we often hear. Yet it would be important for culture to receive the support it deserves. Who knows, perhaps President Mortenstein will be the exception, remembered decades from now as the man who brought Mozart back into the mainstream.
"I also remembered Mozart, even if he was wild with a little progressive touch."

Because it is not in vain that this rather progressive initiative is reaping huge successes, whose goal is nothing other than to lead our world, which is drifting far from traditional values, back to its roots.
"Since when is it progress to return to roots? Who wrote this?"

And how does he do it all? Evoking the fashion of the Age of Enlightenment, the social elite can once again meet at fancy balls. Who wouldn't want to fly back centuries to the past to take part in these magnificent balls, evoking the splendor of the palaces of Versailles, where the worlds of wealth and high culture meet.
As a classical music-loving patron, Edgar Mortenstein has spent years making this time travel possible, named for its fusion with modern times: Neon Nights.
My eyebrow shot up uncontrollably.

How many celebrities, how many low-profile billionaires are now lining up to participate in these wonderful galas, which focus not only on entertainment but also on business connections, thus promoting long-term economic recovery, traces of which can perhaps already be detected.

And art will undoubtedly profit from this new fashion as well. Just think of the prestige it represents for a musician to perform at the Neon Nights! What a boost it can give to the fine arts to have their exhibitions held before the curious eyes of the spending elite.
This is Edgar Mortenstein's modern day dream...
"What the fuck?! Nausea was literally washing over me. What is this shit? What is happening here? I asked myself the desperate question."

I thought I was misreading. What is this man talking about? What is he writing? What happened in the world that I had absolutely no idea about? The president, as our country's greatest culture warrior? And what are these balls? I didn't remember any balls, only steamy erotic dances and dark private rooms. There was no place for culture there, nor any pomp. I began to cry. I felt humiliated all over again. How could I have been so foolish? While I spent years preparing to lock up those responsible, who took all hope from the girls they held captive, a much larger plan was taking shape over my head, of which I had seen nothing until now.

Then, as I dared to think, I began to calm down. This whole thing must be a lie. It has to be, I sighed. After all, no one has ever heard of the dazzling, nauseating balls of the Neon Nights. Only this article claims their existence.
I needed a clear head.
I didn't want to spend my whole day in the office. I decided the best thing would be to go home and calm down.

Anton was cooking. Of course, he always worked from home, hated unfamiliar environments, and needed peace to write. He had set up a proper study for himself, which I envied, because my desk was in the middle of the living room, and I felt it was only a makeshift solution. I wanted my own place.

He was frying hamburger patties. He had been experimenting with this lately. As soon as I entered the kitchen, I turned off the stove immediately, grabbed Anton's arm, and pushed him against the fridge.
"'Are you home already?' he asked, surprised."
"'Don't talk!' I told him firmly."
With that, I undid his belt, unbuttoned his pants, and knelt before him.
"'This is new,' he said. His eyes shone with excitement."
"'I told you not to talk!'"

Although I loved it when Anton softly whispered sweet things to me during lovemaking, today I wasn't in the mood to crave that. He felt immediately that I wasn't approaching him in the usual way, which is why he was so surprised. It wasn't what I was doing that was new to him, but the way I was doing it.

I freed him with such quick movements that he didn't even stand up completely for him, this rarely happened, and this also showed that I managed to surprise him. I didn't mind it being so. On the way home I fantasized about what I was going to do with it, and the fact that I had to work a little for the pleasure didn't bother me at all. First I grabbed her with my right hand and pulled a few on her, then I planted a kiss on her glans, which was getting brighter and brighter, which I wanted to deal with first. The humidity only made it all the more exciting. When he was in full force in front of me, I gently licked him around like a lollipop and watched how he reacted. Anton enjoyed my performance with his eyes closed, which at first he tried to tolerate in silence, then he could barely control himself. Each of my touches became more and more on target, and I could see her stomach tighten. Now I started working with both hands at the same time, during which I slid my tongue along the base for a moment and took it into my mouth, going deeper with each movement. I knew that Anton's most sensitive point was at the junction of the glans and the penis, so I tried to focus on that part as much as possible. I lingered here with pleasure, pampering her with my tongue and lips at the same time. To drag it on, I started to play with my voice and admitted myself more and more to what I was doing, while loud gasps signaled my attempt to suck out of him what he was already having difficulty keeping inside. As she leaned forward in love, she ran both hands deep into my hair, and I pounced on my prey like a hunting dog. I surprised Anton so much that he became feverish, so it was suddenly more difficult for him to give himself. I didn't buy socks at all. I was glad to be able to continue my instinct game and just put more energy into it. I felt like a predator that has dropped its prey and is not going to release it from its clutches at any cost. I kept her in constant excitement with circular tongue movements, while my hand moved up and down like a piston, and I waited for the moment when she would finally give in. Anton suddenly leaned forward, and with a loud moan the nectar burst out of him, of which I tried to absorb every drop. I looked at Anton, who was staring in front of me with open pupils and open mouth panting, his relaxed hands felt my head. I kissed her. I executed the poor thing. However, my brain felt sharper than ever.
I lay down on the sofa and explained to him from there.

"'I stumbled upon an article that talks about how much of a patron of culture the president is, and that the Neon Nights is a wonderful series of events where stars, magnates, politicians, and the upper thousands dance to rearranged Mozart in ballroom gowns.'"
"'What article is this? I've never heard of such a thing in my life,' he replied, wonder still in his voice."

"'Right? Neither have I. What is this nonsense? Some fraud must have written it.' Anton hummed something without a word as he buttoned his pants back up. 'Would you do something for me?' I continued, wanting him to."
"'What is it?'"
"'It won't be too exciting,' I apologized."
"'You don't have to compare everything to what we just did.'"
"You should go to the library tomorrow and look for the July 8, 2010 issue of LC Rumors, which supposedly has this article."
"'Is that all?' he was puzzled."
"'That's all.'"
"'And what should we be happy about? If it's in there, or if it's not?'"
"'I'll decide once it turns out.'"

chapters.title: Chapter Three
paragraphs: January 15, 2022
I summoned two detectives, Wilbur and Matt, to my office and handed them Katherine and Mike's files.
"Don't speak to anyone about this investigation for now!" I urged them. "For now, handle this case as if it didn't even exist."
"What is this about?" asked Matt, whose shimmering sky-blue eyes completely mesmerized the unwary.
"Now you're coming with me to a private address, where we'll have a chat with someone. There you'll find out what's worth knowing about the case," I replied.

Wilbur was a large, bearded detective with a deep voice who might have seemed simple-minded at first glance; yet, anyone who underestimated his abilities was sorely mistaken. And Matt, despite being one of our most reliable men, was someone I had previously only seen types like in illegal gaming halls.
"Amanda Evans!" I introduced myself at the intercom.

They lived in a newly built residential area in Gemini park, where it was extremely difficult to get into. The whole thing was listed under a single house number, while there were four different buildings with several staircases. I would have preferred to cut this maze right at his head as soon as he let us in, but it would have been strange in front of the police, so I preferred to remain silent.

The two police officers didn't really find their footing in the apartment. Wilbur stood still in the living room next to the coffee table with a notepad in hand, while Matt stared at the family photos hanging on the walls.
"Please, have a seat!" Jessica asked them, but they just wouldn't obey her.
"Miss Marsh!" I began my spiel. "These two colleagues are detectives. They will be working on the case under my direction."
"That's great, thank you," Jess said, slightly flustered.

"In your complaint, you state that President Edgar Mortenstein held you captive for years and forced you into sex work at the nightclub called Neon Nights. And that an entire organization assisted him in this. Is that correct?"
"Yes," Jess answered briefly.

"For now, we are only uncovering the crime. I ask you to tell us everything that might be important for exposing this case during the investigation. Please keep in mind that with your testimony, you can facilitate or obstruct the work of the authorities, which can itself qualify as a crime. Do you understand this?"
"Yes, I'm aware of that."
"You should know that your testimony can, of course, be incomplete; you don't have to remember everything, and you can make additions at any time. Do you have a lawyer?"
"Not yet, but I understand what you're saying."
"Get a lawyer," I advised Jess.

Jess was very taciturn. She was visibly intimidated. I put on my official tone, but knowing Jess, this shouldn't have flustered her. Perhaps the presence of Matt and Wilbur caused it, though I would have found that strange too.
(Jessica Marsh)

Please forgive me if I speak in a muddle. I can't recall every detail—it's not even possible. I ended up at the Neon Nights at a very young age, together with my best friend. Around the age of sixteen. Of course, you might think that girls of that age are almost adults nowadays, but we couldn't have been prepared at all for what awaited us there, especially mentally.

We both grew up in a children's home. We lived in the same room, did everything together. The children's home was a mixed place, but I don't want to talk about that—it doesn't belong here. After a while, we wanted to escape from there, and that was how Neon Nights came into the picture. A guy named Jake Cooper recruited us. I'll never forget, he had beautiful brown eyes and curly hair. For a boy, he had a girlish face, but he was a very strong, muscular boy. I liked him right away, and this attraction probably contributed to my saying yes to his offer.
"Did both of you say yes with your friend?" I asked Jess.

Yes, Alice went along with it too. We discussed it. We were like, if one of us doesn't want to, we won't go. But we didn't think all that much about whether to take it on. We figured we'd seen it all, it couldn't get worse. In this, as it turned out later, we were wrong.

Neon Nights was an elite club. This was evident from its clientele, but I'm not saying it from that perspective now. It was operated by a highly hierarchical organization at the top of which stood the president, and he placed demands on the incoming girls that, if not met, would quickly get someone kicked out.
"Kicked out? How do you mean? Does that mean you stayed there of your own free will?"

Apologies!" Jess continued. "I phrased it poorly. Kicked out of the club, of course. After entering, there was no real chance to escape from the Neon Nights network. I don't have precise information about this, but the chain continued outside the club, and I'm quite sure it extended abroad, starting from the clients, all the way to the Middle East, and from there even to Southeast Asia.
"That would mean a very large network," I interjected.
I doubt you'd be that surprised by the existence of such organizations. Some threads led straight into the depths of the dark web.
"What exactly was your task?" I asked.

Different things at different times. I suppose you can imagine. We had to dance and undress. Then sex. Mostly sex. This had all kinds of arrangements. Whatever the client needed. With him, or with each other, or in groups. There weren't really things we didn't have to do.
"So you were forced to do this."
"What do you think?" Jess asked back cynically. She was relaxing more and more, surrendering to the character—which, in her case, wasn't a role, but her true self. I was the one playing a role.

Believe me, I wouldn't have wanted to frolic with three stocky guys of my own accord. If it had been the three Chrises of the Avengers there, maybe I'd have thought about it. But a person had no other choice.
"Did you resist?"

Everyone resisted at first. But the reaction was always different. The clients naturally wanted to get what they paid for. There were those you sympathized with, and those you hated from the bottom of your soul. Some attracted you, others made your skin crawl. After a while, it didn't really matter who wanted what; we broke, and we did what we had to. But that's just how it has always been. We weren't the first whores in the world. You know yourself that there are girls in much worse situations. Yet no one cares about them.

So after a while, we didn't resist. There was no other choice; we couldn't do anything else. We even had to appreciate that we worked in the club. In fact, it was one of the best things that could have happened to us. None of us wanted to end up in uncertain places. There were those who dropped out. There were rumors about them. We could sometimes squeeze out of the gentlemen who went where. And after such news, everyone knew they had it good here, even if they had thrown up in the sink ten minutes ago.
"Who are the gentlemen?"
The ones who watched over us and organized our lives.
"And where did those girls end up?"
What I already said. Say, on the dark web. Of course, surely in many other places too.
"And where there?"
Is that so extremely important? Are there a lot of sick people in the world? They can all get gratification there. If I say they were tortured or mutilated, will my testimony be more complete?
"You're right," excused prosecutor Dr. Amanda Evans, though she knew exactly what kind of stories there were.

Nobody cared about their fate anymore. What could be done? We couldn't even save ourselves. We were glad to be alive and to be able to go shopping once a week, for example. They knew very well how to take away a person's dignity. Do you know how happy we were for that half-day when we could shop to our heart's content in a mall? Anyone who saw us envied us. Those beautiful clothes, shoes! We went to the best shops. Maybe someone tore it apart that very night, or we ripped it off each other, but we didn't care. We imagined ourselves as women, and we hated the world around us. We got everything we wanted. Nothing had a price. Thus, neither did our lives.
"Why did you report Edgar Mortenstein?" I asked Jess, who fell silent. She stared ahead of herself, then slowly raised her head and looked into my eyes.
Because he's a piece of shit who belongs in prison.
"And why did you wait so long?"
How long did I wait? Your question is why I didn't report him six years ago?
"Yes. I would like to get an answer to that."

It's not easy to conquer the demons that hold a person captive. Don't think that after that nightmare ended, any of us wanted to go to the police. I just wanted to hide. Then get my life back together. A lot of time passed before I felt capable of going through a legal procedure. Later, it was like, if I've put my life back together, why risk it anyway? I had to convince myself not to let it slide.
"Why are you doing it now?"

When the elections were on, suddenly the phone rang. It was a robocall, Mortenstein speaking. He launched into his patriotic slogans, and it made me sick. I hung up the phone, shaken. I realized I would never close this chapter in my life if I didn't stand up for my truth and this man didn't get what he deserved.
"How did you manage to get out?"
By bringing down the system and then blackmailing him for a severance package. That PC bastard was afraid of me. He remembers me, believe me, and to this day I am sure he is terrified.
In Matt's eyes, I saw that he believed what Jess said.

chapters.title: Chapter Four
paragraphs: She did well to believe her, because Jess wasn't lying, nor was she even distorting reality. I had never seen Mortenstein, but I knew all the stories, and I was sure he was afraid of Jessica. There was something about Jessica that had a great effect on him, something he couldn't resist, which shriveled him to a tiny size in Jess's presence. I knew my friend well enough to know what she was capable of.
"What do you mean by bringing down the system?" I asked about this obvious anomaly.
"By putting an end to the whole thing."
"But how?" asked the prosecutor Dr. Amanda Evans within me.

"I was the only one who went to bed with the president. He wanted me, even though he was afraid of me. Maybe that was exactly why he needed me. This opened up an opportunity for me. And I took advantage of it."
"Yet he doesn't seem like someone to be feared," I noted. "How did you catch Edgar Mortenstein anyway?"
"I don't want to talk about that yet. Within a month after that, the place closed down, and all the girls went home."

Jess said these sentences with such confidence, as if she had really been some supernatural being in those times. Of course, in a way, there was some truth to that. As soon as Mortenstein showed weakness, Jess took advantage of it immediately.
"Interesting story," Wilbur chimed in. "Could you tell us names of people we could contact so they could tell us about these things too?"
"Of course. I know many. But I'd be surprised if they hadn't vanished and changed their names since then."
"Mass name changes can also raise suspicion," Matt noted.
"Perhaps some of the girls?" Wilbur asked.
"I figured you'd bring it up, so I wrote a list. Maybe you should look into them." Jess handed a very small piece of paper to the detectives. "Can I help you with anything else?" she asked.
"Not for now," I answered. "Just stay reachable."
"You can reach me by phone, is that enough?"

I would have loved to tell Jess that it wasn't enough, that it was much easier to handle things in person, but knowing she was in the middle of some family crisis, I excused her for seeking an escape.
"Perhaps it would be best," Jess began, "if you detectives set to work on the list right away. There are a few things I'd like to discuss with the prosecutor in private."
"The gentlemen are the detectives," I said, rising to their defense. "They need to know everything that might be important for the investigation."
"I assume you will tell them everything they need anyway, won't you?"
"Boys," I turned to Matt and Wilbur, "get to it!"
Jessica waited about half a minute after the detectives closed the door. We heard their firm steps in the stairwell, and finally, all fell silent.
"Look," she began, and pressed a slip of paper into my hand.
Cold sweat broke out over me.
"Dammit, Jess, where on earth did this come from?!"
"It was in my mailbox."
"Who could have written this?"
"I have no idea, Amy. If I knew, I would tell you."
"How did they find out?!" I snapped, kicking Jess's coffee table a foot away.
"Should I be afraid of the one who threatened me, or the one who destroys my furniture?" she teased.
"Jess! How did they find out about the complaint?"
"I don't know, but at the police station, several people could have found out, couldn't they?"
"At the very start, besides us, only two people know about it, the ones with whom you filed the complaint."
"Maybe they told someone earlier. They were quite surprised when I stated that I blame the president for everything."
"All right, let's assume they told someone. But how did it reach Mortenstein immediately?"
"Are you absolutely sure he sent this threat?"
"He certainly didn't himself. He has enough henchmen for this task. But who else would have done it than them?"
"I don't know."
"Who are you thinking of?" I asked Jessica.
"I'm not thinking of anyone. It would indeed be quite strange if they already knew. Of course, nothing can be ruled out."
"The point is, someone threatened you, and they know what we are doing."

I was indescribably angry. This was the last thing I wanted: for the president to catch wind of what we were planning ahead of time. This whole thing should have remained secret for a long time, but it seemed it was only a vain illusion. I could hardly believe that two days after the complaint was made, it had already leaked. It was a stroke of luck that it didn't leak to the public, although that might have been less of a catastrophe than this, where in all this secrecy, only we stood against Neon Nights.

We wanted to keep the media out of things until the trial so they wouldn't place extra pressure on us. In a highly publicized case, other expectations from public opinion always appear. Suddenly, people have opinions on things that hadn't even crossed their minds before, or if they knew about them, they certainly didn't want to think about them. The crossfire of public opinion can easily confuse a person; we didn't want to make heroes of ourselves, and of course, not of Mortenstein either. The last thing we needed was to turn it all around and have the president come out of it better. Not a single nerve of ours wanted any publicity. Of course, we would have gladly witnessed a sweating question or two cornering that prick on live broadcast, but we didn't chase dreams. He hardly gave interviews to anyone, and the accusation would have seemed like an insidious joke to those who sympathized with him. And it was, until we could support it with sufficient evidence.
Now that the news of the fact of the complaint had reached them, it was on the cards that we would have to change strategy.
"What should we do?" I asked Jessica.
"Nothing. Let's continue as discussed."
"You were threatened, Jess! Someone dropped a note in your mailbox. They know where you live."
"I know. But I'm traveling to Jeff's anyway."
"And you think they won't find you there?" I asked, puzzled.
"So what should I do, Amy? I have already filed the complaint, I will not withdraw it!"
"Aren't you afraid of them?"
"They are afraid of me."
"Let's involve the media!" I brainstormed.

"Why? How do they influence public opinion? Do you want everyone to track you down? They want to know who you are. There will be enough stress in the negotiation phase. Don't fall apart, Amy! I made it all up. You just have to go through with it. We never thought it would be a walking tour."
"Then I'll assign a cop to you. You'll get personal protection."
"You have super ideas," she said with cynicism in her voice.
"I don't care, Jess!" I snorted. "This letter is serious! Don't think they'd shrink from getting you out of the way. You know them. You know exactly what they are capable of."
"All right," she agreed finally. "As you wish."
"Believe me, I would be the first to wish this wasn't necessary."
"Fine!" Jessica cut it short. "I get it. Bring on the cop! Let's hope he'll be scary enough for the bad guys to scare them away."

I left. I was very nervous. Suddenly I didn't know what to do. I needed to clear my head. On the way, I went into a tobacconist and asked for a tea and a single cigarette. They looked at me as if I was completely crazy.
"I don't smoke," I told the clerk. "I just need one."
"You don't have to prove it, I believe you. Do you want it with lots of sugar?"
"Yes!"

Leaning against my car, I tried to weigh the options. I had no idea what could have happened. Everything came to light too quickly. At least it seemed so, but I could do nothing other than believe in appearances. Of course, if this was an appearance at all.

I felt a bit as if they had taken my first piece off the board. I didn't know yet if it was a pawn or a officer, but it didn't feel good. They had the nerve to threaten me. This was what went through my head. It wasn't even the danger itself, but the fact that bothered me. Was this their first reaction? Firing back immediately? But then, what did I expect? They were always like this. Culturedness was only a guise; underneath, very simple dynamics operated. The stronger won.

Indeed, I didn't smoke. Occasionally. I stared at the smoke as it danced delicately from my mouth toward the upper layers of the atmosphere. I tried to blow it out through my nose too, but it felt as if my brain had been dipped in vinegar; my mucous membrane contracted, and I grimaced. No showing off, I thought. It would be best to enjoy the bragging peace of the cigarette in a relaxed way.
It was afternoon. Almost two o'clock. I hadn't eaten anything all day. I was hungry, just didn't know for what.

chapters.title: Chapter Five
paragraphs: (ANTON MARTIN)

The Golden State Library was a beautiful classicist building. It had a huge colonnade and a tympanum decorated with friezes. If you didn't know what it was, you would have recognized that the building was home to high culture. For the sake of the overall effect, there was also a restaurant and pastry shop in the institution, so whoever decided to devote himself to reading or research work could do so in really pleasant conditions.

Every time I came here, I started my visit at the confectionery. Fine pastries are my weakness, and the selection was good enough that I never wanted to skip it. Now, however, it felt as if I had more important things to do than throw myself into the pampering embrace of the hotbed of cultural snobbery. I purposefully bypassed the gold-framed glass wall behind which rows of true pastry creations lined up, and chose literature instead—if reading gossip rags could ever qualify as such.

I searched the database for the magazine issue Amy had sent, and within half an hour I realized that there was no article about Neon Nights in it. Too bad, I thought, but how good it would have been. Then it occurred to me that maybe I didn't ask for the right number after all. Then I thought that maybe I don't need the date that Amy said. Maybe they were only going to release it that day, but it ended up slipping into the next issue. When I asked for the current and next week's publications, I was already sure that the article had never appeared in the columns of LC Rumors. But then what could have happened? While reading the pages, I felt uneasiness. I felt strange. I was sure that the solution was right in front of my eyes, but I couldn't see it. One of the pages contained an advertisement for a clothing store. Strange, I thought, yes, that is what is strange, because none of the other publications had ads on that page. Then I found the solution. At the last moment, something was removed from the issue, which there was no time to replace. A shiver ran down my spine. How interesting, I thought. It just starts to mean something that isn't even there. It is this nothingness that makes me certain that something must have happened that Amy will be able to find out. This is not a coincidence, it has evidential force here.
"Hi!" I said into the phone. "Yes, I found it."
"No, it's not in there."
"That's exactly why we have it. I'll tell you later."
"I know, but I can't phrase it right now."
"Yes, I'm a writer."
"No matter, I'll explain it later!" With that, I hung up the phone.

Amy and I didn't always speak the same language. As for me, I was perhaps even more sensitive to my different moods than he was, and today, since I left home, I have been overcome by a floating feeling. By finding the cold location of the article, I felt like a successful detective. What a clever idea, surely no one else would have thought of it, I fooled myself, or at least not in such a short time. I was completely satisfied with myself, and in this situation I put it in a way that Amy found it difficult to understand. Then he called me right back.
"Anton, I don't have time for your theatrical performances," she began. "I need that info now! Just tell me what you found!"

"The issue you requested did not have the article. There was, however, a page of advertising. I looked in other issues and none of them had ads in that spot. This is almost the middle of the page. They removed that article from there, Amy, but it was there."
"You see, you can tell me after all. Nice work! Thanks a lot. I need to find the editor."
"I think I know where to look."
"Are you serious?" she asked, surprised.
"Of course," I paused briefly. "It's in the masthead," I replied calmly.
"Dammit," she blurted out. "Of course."
"If you like, I can visit him," I offered. "I have acquaintances in such circles."
"Seriously?"
"Why does it surprise you?"
"Anton! This is wonderful. And could it be right now?"
"Already?" I was surprised.
"It would be important, yes. Is it okay?"
"I have nothing better to do, so why not? I'll call you when I've spoken to him."

When Amy started the case, I was worried that we would spend less time together because of all the work. I knew that an investigation consumes a lot of time and energy, and when he became a prosecutor, I reckoned with him that I would be somewhat pushed into the background behind his profession. Of course, I supported him in everything, moreover, he managed to manage his work so far so well that nothing came of my fear, but when everything was ready in the Mortenstein case, I thought that it would be impossible to carry it through in the same way. On the second day, I found myself investigating, and instead of drifting apart, I had a chance to get even closer to him. I really liked that idea.

As a result of my work, I was closer to people who were defining characters of mainstream cultural life. Of course, LC Rumors was not a literary newspaper, it was obviously not written for such a purpose, regardless, the editors-in-chief of similar papers were also part of this circle. Since the paper reached many people, many wanted to work for them, and if not full-time, you could be present for them part-time or by writing occasional articles. However, because of this, I was uncertain as to where the person who wrote the article could take his place in the world of tabloid journalism. Because it is easy to imagine that he is an amateur, but a disruptive person who just wanted to say something big, and because of this, his article was not published at the last moment due to political motives, but rather due to his incompetence. In this case, the whole document is a disinterested jumble, which, even if it has real elements, does not help much during the investigation. On the contrary, if the author of the article was a real journalist, everything becomes much more interesting.

After a few minutes, I was already walking down the street with the latest LC Rumors under my arm, which made me sure that the editor-in-chief was the same as he was ten years ago. I decided it would be easiest to go to the editorial office. I knew that the investigation was secret, so I couldn't spoil the house, but maybe it would have caused the least sensation if I got straight to the point. I called a friend of mine who was able to introduce me to the editor-in-chief, and since I am a writer, I even gave him a reason why we should meet. I went for advice.

"Na yes," I interrupted Timothy Jones. "I understand that you have to finish each day's issue by midnight, but what interests me is whether it's possible that, say, you don't get enough articles by deadline, and the empty pages have to be filled somehow."
"This is LC Rumors Mr. Martin. This thought makes me smile. Do you see how much material we receive every day?" he pointed to his laptop.
"I didn't see your emails, Mr. Jones. But surely a lot."
"We have to reject articles from freelance writers by the hundreds," he bragged.
"So then it's inconceivable for an issue not to be complete and, say, to have to insert a picture or an ad as a space filler?" I explained.

"That is inconceivable. All our papers are edited the same way. It may be tabloid, but it's a very high-quality publication. The advertising spaces are precisely designated. We don't scavenge. In every issue, we know in advance what goes where."
"So if, say, the detective in my book found an issue where there is still a picture where there usually isn't, he would be right to be suspicious?"
"Absolutely!" he said firmly. "Especially if it's about LC Rumors."
"Interesting," I feigned. "I just don't know yet why such a case would occur."
"I don't know, say under police or political pressure," the editor-in-chief brainstormed.
"Political?" I played surprised.
"Yes, unfortunately. It happens. Sometimes."
"Has this happened at LC Rumors too?" The editor-in-chief grimaced.
"I don't like to admit it, but yes, as I said, it has occurred."
"How can censorship function in a free country?"
"It doesn't function like that."
"Then how?"
"Look Mr. Martin! I don't really think I can talk to you about this."
"Oh, come on," I waved it off. "We're not going to be shy in front of colleagues!"
"We are not colleagues."
"Oh, of course we are!" I insisted. "Where would the writer go without the editor? We cooperate, we live in symbiosis. We need each other."

"All right, I get it, just stop," he broke. "There is no censorship. But a paper living off the market cannot afford to let its circulation drop. So we have always tried to pay attention to staying within the bounds of common sense in sensitive matters."
"What self-discipline!" I mocked. "So you don't write about every compromising topic?"
"No, not at all! That's not the goal. I see you are not a LC Rumors reader."
"Do you have crosswords?"
"No."
"Then I don't think so either."

"Look, Martin! This newspaper covers the most important happenings in the celebrity world to a high standard, sometimes it gets into someone's way more than it is polite, but that's the purpose of a tabloid somewhere. Regardless, we try to remain professionally on the top shelf in this segment, and for this we have to remain fair."
"Then how does censorship work? Do they check you?"
"Somewhere in between. We have a team that, in the case of borderline articles, contacts the appropriate people."
"A star's manager?"

"No. A star is a star. There we bear the responsibility of what we say and how. But if a foreign politician comes who we know divides public opinion, then the paper coordinates what should go out and what shouldn't. You could say it's self-defense on our part, but politics can come down hard on us if it wants, even if there is media freedom."
"I understand, but if such a restriction occurs, something would still be put in the article's place, wouldn't it? After all, there are hundreds of articles to choose from."

"There could be," Jones answered, "but an article has to be edited. Besides, if it has to be replaced later, it's more work because it has to be cut to the exact size of the one we want to replace. Sometimes it happens, but it's a lot of work."
"So you're saying if under political pressure you had to replace an article shortly before midnight, then..."
"Yes, then I'd choose an advertisement in its place. Advertisers are crowding in too," he admitted.
"Well, that's truly great," I rejoiced.
"What?" Timothy Jones asked, slightly surprised.
I pulled out my phone and showed Amy the photo from the article.
"Is it possible that this article was removed from the July 8, 2010 issue for this reason?"
Jones's attitude changed suddenly.
"All right, who are you really? Where did you get this?" he switched to a more serious tone.
"I'm Anton Martin, you know, the writer. To be honest, I can't really tell where this article is from. Do you remember him?"
"This really shouldn't be with you."
"Why?" I was puzzled. "It's been more than ten years since it was removed from the paper. How can you even remember it?"
"Do you remember your first kiss?"
"Everyone remembers their first... ah."
"I, too, remember the first article I had to pull from the paper a few minutes after midnight."
He fell silent. I could see that besides surprise, something else was weighing on his soul. We sat like this for about half a minute, speechless.
"Jones, I could tell you to forget about this article and that you saw me at all, but I see this case affected you deeply. I admit I'm surprised by how much. What did I just step into?"
"That is a very good question."
"And will you tell me?"
"You know, I've read something of yours, but I still don't know you. Aside from being a stranger, I don't even feel I should trust you."
"I completely agree with that. Obviously, if I need information, it's not because I want to keep it a secret."
"I see you understand."
"At the same time, I also know that if you don't want to talk to me about it, tomorrow three detectives will be sitting at this desk, and they won't be as receptive to human feelings as I am."
"Are you threatening me now?"
"Me?" I was surprised. "No! Or am I? If so, I didn't mean to at all."
"Who sent you?"
"I can't tell you that, but I can tell you it's not the person because of whom you don't want to talk about this."
"And is that better?" he puzzled. "That's not better either. Actually, it doesn't matter at all."
"Better or not, I still must ask: why is this silly article such a big deal?"
"Silly, is it?" he asked back. "It was terribly written. If you had read our paper, you'd know how much higher quality it is than this propagandist abomination."
"I agree with you. It's a tragedy. What if we start from there?"
Timothy Jones nodded. It was clear he had no desire for this conversation, yet strangely I felt he had expected a case like this to happen someday.
"How did such a low-quality article almost end up in the paper?"
"It was recommended," he answered briefly.
"And who recommended it?" I inquired, as if taking a deposition.
"From above."
"Right, but who?"
"One of Mortenstein's people."
"Yes, I suppose I could have guessed that myself. And which one?"
"What does it matter? They're all the same. There's no need to differentiate. One of them, I don't know the exact name."
"You don't know? That's strange. I'd think if he recommended it, he was also the one who held back the article at the last moment."
"Yes, that was how it went."
"Then what's his name?"
"Jane Smith."
"I don't know him," I replied.
"Why would you? These names belong to the president's inner circle. Why would you know who the twentieth-ranking official in the cabinet is?"
"All right, this really makes no sense. Tell me instead how the incident happened."
"You diverged from the subject anyway. You said we would deal with the quality of the article."
"Yes, you're right. Forgive me, I'm not a professional interrogator. Really, I just want to chat."
"Fine," Jones acknowledged what had been said. "I'll tell you, since you've put so much energy into it. At least I'll get it out of my system."
(Timothy Jones)

I still vividly remember everything that happened that day. Now you might be thinking that all of this is not that important, and you would be right. Seen from the outside, it is really nothing, in fact, it can even be explained by some kind of political attitude. You can quickly figure out why the president needed LC Rumors. Actually, any newspaper with a large readership would have been good, for some reason they chose our paper.

At about two in the afternoon I received a call from Jane Smith, mentioned earlier, that there was an article they wanted to put in the paper. I told him that this was impossible, as the daily news cannot be left out of the newspaper, and there is a waiting list of several weeks for other articles. He said he understood that, but that this article had to be in that day's issue, and he offered a very visible sum to enforce it. At first I was indignant: what does he think, who is he to put pressure on me, then he said, of course, he has many tools with which he can convince me. After he mentioned a couple of my colleagues by name and made an unmistakable hint that the future of the paper is not at all self-evident, I bought the paper and let him have it.
"That's a bit ridiculous, don't you think?" asked Mr. Anton Martin. "What reason would there be to threaten the editor for a wretched article, which is also so lame?"
"I don't know," I admitted contritely.

I had no idea why this was so important to them. I was sure there must be some significant reason, otherwise they wouldn't have started threatening. Actually, I resigned myself to the situation. It hit me so unexpectedly, I felt it was truly below the belt. I was also shocked by how different our concepts of the world were. Apparently, for them, no rejection existed, and they didn't know what to do if someone didn't fulfill their request. No matter, I thought. At least the paper will get a little extra money. That always comes in handy.

"They sent the article over. They said it shouldn't be too hidden, but not showy either. Fine, I said, and was even relieved, because if I had had to put it in place of the editorial in the current issue, I might have hanged myself.

I read it, was horrified, but what was strangest about the whole thing was that I didn't understand a single word of it. I've been involved in tabloid journalism since I entered university. The world of stars is part of my daily life, but I had never heard of what I read before.
"Isn't it?" puzzled Mr. Martin. "Didn't know about the event?"

"'Not at all,' I answered. 'Yet if anyone knows the celeb world, it's me. I almost felt bad that I wasn't invited. LC Rumors should report firsthand on such an event. And of course, at a much better standard than this article was written.'"
"'And do you know who wrote it?'"
"'No. No one told me. But it doesn't matter.'"

I formatted the text, edited it, put it in place of the previous article, then waited for everyone to finish work, and at the end of the day, reviewed the paper so we could send it to the press. This finally happened shortly before midnight.
"Why do they wait so long to close the page?" asked Anton Martin somewhat amateurishly.
"Because daily current events are also important. LC Rumors is both a tabloid and a daily newspaper, we don't want to give it any less, and we won't as long as I'm the editor-in-chief."
"'Do you read every edition before they send it to the press?'"
"'And what happened after midnight?'"

The colleagues had already gone home, and I was in the parking garage. Suddenly my phone rang. It was Jane Smith. He didn't say hello, he just ordered me to take the article out of the paper immediately. I told him that was impossible, it was already in the press. He didn't care. He said he couldn't go out, that was his order, and if I didn't, he would stop me. Since he probably would have handled the situation in a completely different way, I called the printer. They had already started duplicating it, but I stopped it. I took out my laptop and edited the track. I took an advertisement and put it in place of the article. The printing press waited, the copies were ready. No one ever asked for more about it.
(Anton Martin)
"It was an interesting story, Mr. Jones! I'm inclined to think that there was no sense in this whole thing."
"To this day, I don't understand it."
"And did you try to find out what the article was about?"

"Yes, but no one has heard of it. I think it would have been propaganda, which was ultimately unnecessary. I'm just as confused as you. I have no idea. Obviously, it could have been set up as a private event, which was a zero occasion."
"'And couldn't it be that it really was a dress rehearsal? I mean, one that did happen in the end?'"
"'That's not ruled out either,' the editor-in-chief replied. 'Although it would truly surprise me if that were the case.'"
"I didn't know if we were closer to the solution, but I'm sure it helped a lot."
"'Thinking it over, I don't know why I haven't talked about this to anyone before.'"
"'I understand, it's quite confusing. Did you ever feel as if a small detail of something larger but hidden surfaced for a brief moment?'"

"'Yes. And if it comforts you, that's all I can think about even now. That's probably why I wanted to forget it. I didn't understand any of it, but my gut told me it was better not to know about the whole thing.'"

I thanked Timothy Jones for the information and couldn't wait to report what I found out to Amy as soon as possible. I didn't know what she would do with them, but it wasn't my job to say. Unlike in books, things at the real police force ran smoothly, and the most insignificant piece of information could be transformed into important evidence.

chapters.title: Chapter Six
paragraphs: January 22, 2022

It was Saturday, and Anton and I were celebrating our anniversary. Early in the morning, he showed up with a huge bouquet. It contained an odd number of red roses. I always got those from him. Suddenly, I even forgot how many years we had been together. In theory, one could have calculated it from the bouquet, but in eternity, a year here or there doesn't matter.

He brought the flowers straight to my bed. I don't know when he went to get them, because I don't think a single florist is open before eight, though with the amount of flowers he's bought me, I wouldn't have been surprised if they had been at his disposal with personalized opening hours.

Before we lived together, it was simpler. Toward evening, he picked me up and took me out to dinner. Now he had to spend the whole day with me, and on holidays, I liked to feel special. Not that he ever seemed bothered by this. He might even have liked organizing the programs.
"Today we're going to do something a little different than usual," he said.
"What do you mean?" I asked. "Aren't we going to a restaurant?"
"Of course we are."
"Then it will be quite similar to usual."
"No, because before that, we are going to a place you don't really like."
"Hmm, you're a go-getter, I like it! But you know that when I'm disappointed, I'm less good company."
"You are always excellent company," he fibbed. "Disappointed you might be."
"Where are we going?"
"To the baths."
"Well, yes. I'm not a fan," I replied.
"It will be special."
"Why on earth?"
"Because it's the most beautiful baths in the city, and they finished the renovation a few weeks ago. It's become quite a trendy place lately."
"I sound quite shallow when you put it like that. But the truth is, I actually like it better because of that."

It wouldn't have mattered where we went today, because what I really wanted was to be able to think through, with a calm head, the wealth of information we had managed to acquire in recent days.
