Matt and Wilbur were still going over Jess's list, and I was trying to sort out my head. In the meantime, Jessica went back to the countryside. I arranged police protection for her, and in theory, they were doing their job completely invisibly. This could have worried me, but the captain reassured me that they work excellently and will notice everything possible. I had to believe what he said; I couldn't do otherwise anyway.

My main problem with the baths was that I didn't like crowds and the thought of sharing the water with so many people. Of course, it was still much better than going to some dirty lake, though on January 22, that obviously wasn't an option. I hoped there would at least be some secluded spot in the baths where we could talk a little more intimately.

It usually helped a lot if I could talk through the things swirling in my head with Anton. He had quite good questions about cases and could think logically, which, let's face it, was to be expected of him after writing so many crime novels.

After the renovation, small cabins were created where two people could comfortably retreat from the crowd, and in the pool, massage jets helped those wishing to rest to relax. It took us a long time to catch one, but afterward, we wouldn't have given it up for any money, no matter how much the pushy ones tried to make a nuisance of themselves.
"You said you'd tell me this weekend what you've come up with so far," Anton noted while I lay enclosed in his arms in the shallow water.
"Yes, I tried to wait a little for it to settle," I replied. "There's quite a lot of things I need answers to."
"Tell me!" he encouraged, kissing my neck.
"I don't even know where to begin. I thought a lot about whether to tell you."
"Oh dear!" he chimed in.
"You'll see in a moment. I wasn't tense for no reason in recent days."
"I didn't even notice."
"Of course," I countered. "Jess was threatened."
"Already?" Anton was puzzled.
"Does that seriously surprise you?"
"Don't tell me you didn't expect it. How did it come out so soon?"
"That's what I don't know the answer to."
"How did it happen?"
"A note was dropped in the mailbox."
"Doesn't sound very serious."
"Except what they wrote. That she dies if she doesn't withdraw the complaint."
"Then let her withdraw it," Anton declared.
"Of course."
"Why? It's completely logical."
"We worked on this for years! You're saying we should just leave it at that?"
"No," he answered briefly.
"Then I don't understand you."
"I'm just saying that you should withdraw the complaint. You can still investigate."
"Yes, but that won't lead to a trial."
"If there's enough evidence, the authorities will do it instead of Jess," he said, as if it were the most obvious thing. What really hurt was that it actually was.
"Yes, that seems like a viable path."
"Should I solve another of your problems?" he asked contentedly.
"You haven't solved this one yet, but it's a good idea," I replied. "I have to discuss it with Jess to see what she says. She definitely wanted to continue the whole thing."
"I can understand that. But in this situation, it's better to be cautious."
"I assigned a cop to her. In theory, he will watch her every step."
"Every day?"
"Yes."
"I mean, a cop every single moment of every day?"
"Obviously, several people will perform the task."
"I don't trust such things," he tried, unwittingly, to wake my worry.
"Aren't you supposed to be helping me relax?" I asked.
"Of course. You're the expert; I can only think at the level of books."
"And you wouldn't trust them there?"
"In stories, the police are usually excluded from the investigation."
"Of course, because it would be too simple if they solved the case ahead of time."
"Yes, that's probably what's behind it."
"So then? Why is it a bad idea?"
"It's not that it's bad. I just said I don't trust this solution, but that's not the police's fault. Between the two of us, you're the rational one; besides, this is truly your domain."
"Okay, but you've put a bug in my ear, so it would be better if we moved on from this somehow."
"What else is there?" Anton asked.
"That depends on what you want to hear about."
"About everything."
"All right, all right, obviously. But one step at a time."
"How did Jess handle the interrogation?" he inquired.
"At first she was embarrassed, then she got into it," I replied. "By the end, she spoke quite convincingly."
"When you tell the truth, it's not so hard to be convincing. Did she tell everything?"
"More or less. Obviously not everything. A little less than what I suggested."
"What didn't she talk about?"
"How she had the Neon Nights closed."
"That was pretty hardcore."
"Well, yes. It must have been awesome. Too bad I didn't see it... How are you?" I changed the subject, because Anton finally noticed that I had been longingly caressing his thigh for minutes.
"I think you know exactly," he smiled.
"Aha!" I feigned, and continued. "Well, yes. As it happens, this place could be quite exciting."
"Quite exciting indeed."
"Is it the people or the pool that makes it so?" I philosophized.
"Mostly you."
"Yes. That's the right answer," I added, and kissed him.

These private spots weren't very deep. One could practically be seen out of them even if just lying down, so it required greater courage to want to get more out of the situation. But then, it was our anniversary. It was obvious that we both wanted to spice up the day with a little adventure, and a proper balance was established between risk and reward.

I slid my hand slightly higher up Anton's thigh, so that through the leg of his swim trunks, I could reach all the way to his seat. I tried to cover this movement with my leg, drawing my thigh close to the bottom of his trunks. This allowed him to reach me too, and looking into each other's eyes with loving gazes, we began to entertain each other.

The water indeed proved to be very shallow. At first, I touched Anton in the usual way, but it soon became clear that doing so would immediately expose a quite unmistakable shape through the trunks, which wouldn't have been easy to hide. Since we tried to remain in the most natural position possible so that anyone looking at us would think we were just resting, I turned his stiffly swelling member toward the leg of his trunks and began to move it gently back and forth. We didn't want to engage in wild lovemaking—that would have been too much—but we didn't want to let this special occasion go to waste either.

Anton also touched me very gently. First he touched my thigh; he didn't rush in. In the water, everything felt completely different anyway. A touch slipped much less, and it took him longer to find the entrance. He caressed and circled persistently, which filled me with a very pleasant feeling even before he got further in. I felt wonderful. I tried to relax as much as possible and focus only on the slow, fine movements, which seemed to switch on another sense in me. We didn't rush; we proceeded patiently on the path to pleasure, paying much more attention to each other than at other times when we are suddenly carried away by longing. We didn't speak. We lay on each other with closed eyes, imagining connected neural pathways before me, and I could almost see the stimulus triggering the impulse. How different it is this way, I thought. How many sexual acts I had in my life, and yet for years, there wasn't a single one that was even slightly comparable to the most cliché, boring occasion among those I experienced with Anton.

I had a good hand for it; I proved this to Anton quite a few times. Sometimes he couldn't resist my touch for a single minute. Not even when I was doing practically nothing. This time too, I was on the right path. I heard by his breathing which step of the ladder I was on, but I didn't want it to end yet, so I returned to caressing his thigh. He did just the opposite. I didn't mind, because I loved to feel truly good, and between the two of us, he was demonstrably the more impatient one. If I withheld something from him, he wanted to make up for it with me, and pushed his fingers deeper inside me than before. I didn't really want to control my reactions. We behaved completely normally—or at least I reassured myself of this—and the deeper breaths that welled up from me could have been signs of sleepy relaxation as well.

When I grabbed Anton again, he didn't say it, but I felt he didn't want to experience me leaving him again; he was longing for fulfillment. I tried to please him, all the more because he had really outdone himself in the last few minutes and didn't deserve to be kept waiting even a minute. So I began to move my hand more firmly, extending my pampering to the entire apparatus. I didn't think through exactly where he would release, because I hadn't cared about this detail at all. In the shallow water, there was a drain where a whirlpool swirled more strongly, so when we reached the end, I tried to guide the fun-flowing white fluid there. We both solved it in a civilized manner. Anton's smile revealed everything, and when he opened his eyes, he saw me letting him know with rapid batting of my eyelashes how crazy I still was about him after all these years.
We lay on each other for a few minutes. It was a very reassuring feeling. Then, as if another self had gotten the upper hand, we returned to the open questions.
"And what else is there?" he inquired.
"A man set on fire."
"A what?"
"His name was Mike Alioglu. He wanted to get into the Neon Nights."
"Chilling," Anton noted. Such things always upset him.
"I don't really want to think about it either. I have no idea what he did, but I'm sure he didn't deserve such a death."
"And how do you know this happened?"
"From a report."
"Whose?"
"I don't know yet. The name was crossed out in the testimony. They probably didn't want to get him in trouble."
"Lest subsequent investigators find him..."
"It would be obvious, but in the end, they cleared him in the investigation files. He didn't kill Mike. In theory, he was his friend."
"It would be good to ask him about this himself," Anton suggested.
"Believe it or not, that occurred to me too, only we have to find him."
"Maybe Mike's relatives knew him."
"Sometimes completely obvious things lie right in front of us. You're right. Mike's relatives must know him."
"How exciting! Then speak with them as soon as possible!"
"Look how excited you've become," I laughed. "I'll find out who this witness was, and if we have him, we'll talk to him."
"We'll talk? Meaning, you and me?" his eyes lit up.
"The detectives and me. Yes."
He was slightly disappointed by the answer.
"The water's gone cold, let's go!" he suggested suddenly.
Since I felt similarly, I didn't object at all. At the changing cabins, however, Anton continued.
"Amy, wait! Was the note threatening Jess handwritten?"
"Yes," I replied, then thought about it. "Are you thinking that...?"
"You can find out a lot from even a single sentence."
"But we have nothing to compare it to. We don't know who could have written it."
"We don't need to. It can still help narrow the circle. Ask Jess for the note."
"I don't know, Anton. We are looking for a complete stranger."
"And what if he's not?"
"What do you mean?"
"Maybe he's not a stranger."
"Maybe we know him from way back, but even if we found out who wrote it, what would it matter? We know it's someone from Mortenstein's people."
"Yes. That's quite certain."
"So? What do you want with this?" I inquired.
"I don't know, Amy. It just occurred to me."
"I'll ask her to send it, then you can dig into it."
"Super!" A smile sat on Anton's face.
"You enjoy this that much?"
"Yes, I need a little more action," he winked.
"By that, do you mean...?"
"I mean it both ways."
With that, he grabbed me and practically shoved me into the nearest changing cabin.
"We just finished..." I began, though I didn't know why myself.
"You don't want to?" he whispered in my ear, already tearing my swimsuit off me.
"Of course, that's why you're not wearing pants anymore," I countered, and indeed my hand was doing something completely different from what my words would have suggested.
In an instant, I jumped into his lap, and we began kissing wildly. He penetrated me immediately, which surprised me because I didn't understand how he could have gotten aroused so quickly.
"I'm always aroused for you," he replied.
I hadn't noticed that I said my thoughts out loud.

Making love in the changing cabin was indeed very exciting. I was sure people could hear my bottom rhythmically hitting Anton's groin. Besides, I wasn't sure I paid attention to the volume. To tell the truth, I pretty much lost my sense of reality. As hard as I could, I locked my legs around Anton's waist, holding him close to mine while he tossed me around as if I were some basketball. I was quite surprised by his suddenness, but all the more excited. He went very deep inside me; unlike the previous time, our animal instincts took the upper hand now, which was also shown when he suddenly threw me off himself, turned me with my back to him, and I was already leaning forward to rest my elbows on the changing bench. Anton didn't hesitate; he fell upon me immediately, grabbing my breasts tightly, which made everything even more enjoyable. With one hand he reached for my clitoris, so three of my zones were under attack now. Suddenly, I let out a helpless shriek because I couldn't easily control the stimulation of my clitoris, then suddenly swallowed the sound, but by then it was too late. I heard some people making outraged remarks that some people were screwing in one of the cabins. I laughed involuntarily. I didn't care if there would be consequences. This little interlude distracted me, but then I quickly returned to reality because Anton simply pulled me back to him.
"How do you feel?" he whispered.
"Damn good!" I replied. "If only we'd never stop."

This, say, didn't entirely cover the truth, because I was waiting for the moment he would release inside me. I wanted to take him in completely at last. By this time, a small crowd was beginning to gather in front of our cabin, and it would have been hard for us to deny we were making love. We didn't want to, either. I didn't even know where we were anymore. We didn't perceive reality, we just wanted to surrender completely to the pleasure.

At the moment someone finally spoke the obvious—that we should stop this indecent barbarism—Anton reached his goal, and a pleasant warmth spread through me. I turned to press my lips to his, pulling his face to mine with my hand.
"I haven't been taking the pill for three weeks," I breathed in his ear, but he realized I was only joking.
"You're a complete idiot!" he laughed, and held me very tightly.
At the baths, they said the ban isn't permanent, but we can safely take it as such.

chapters.title: Chapter Seven
paragraphs: January 25, 2022

Three days had already passed since Anton tried to convince me to withdraw the complaint. I completely understood why he argued for this; to tell the truth, I didn't take the threat lightly either, but I also knew that the first time they only wanted to scare Jess. However, Jessica wouldn't hear of it. No matter how much I explained to her that the investigation could still continue, she didn't want to accept it. She said she would look weak, and that they only wanted to make her feel vulnerable. In this, let's face it, she was entirely right. She knew them well; she knew exactly how they function and what methods they work with.

So my persuasion did not succeed. The police continued their supervision of Jessica and her partner, sending summaries every day. From these, it was evident that they indeed paid attention to every detail, as well as the fact that so far nothing noteworthy had happened in Montaukville. This didn't surprise me. I didn't expect anyone to hover around Jess's home daily, nor to do so conspicuously. The police's task was primarily to thwart the threat's content; the observation of the protected individuals belonged to the protection only subsidiarily. Thus, from the perspective of the investigation, we didn't gain extra information.

At my request, Jess sent a photo of the note containing the threat, with which Anton set to work immediately to find out more about its writer. He called the writers' union, from where he got the contact info of a recognized graphologist; because of this, he had high hopes for the analysis. I could have looked for a suitable expert myself, but I promised him he could investigate this lead, so I didn't want to interfere in his work.

He was right that we must contact Mike Alioglu's relatives, but this was no easy task. Quite a few Alioglus lived in the city, and the investigation files did not contain the relatives' contact details.
Then Wilbur suddenly walked into my office in an unexpected moment.
"We have several people from the list."
"Fantastic!" I got excited. "Nice work, Wilbur. Who are they?"
"One of them is a man. His name is David King. The other is a certain Hermione."
"Hermione?" I asked back. "Like the witch girl from Harry Potter?"
"I don't know where she got her name, but that's what they call her."
"And what's her last name?"
"She has no last name, Miss Evans. It might be a stage name."
"I fear she's still working. Is there anyone else?"
"Yes, there is. Jake Cooper. The guy who recruited Jessica Marsh and her friend."

I froze completely. The mention of the boy's name who recruited us into the network was like a small electric shock. I hadn't expected at all that we could find Jake Cooper, yet now his address lay before me. I almost regretted that Jessica couldn't come with me to see him. I was sure she could have squeezed much more out of him than I could. But that was why I had detectives, so I wouldn't have to be the tough one. If one had to ask nicely, keeping to all legal norms, Amanda Evans was perfectly suited; but if someone felt like being stubborn, they could talk to Wilbur or Matt, who only seemed like a charmingly cheerful gentleman until the end of their first sentence.
"When could you visit them?" I inquired.
"Today already, if you like."
"Of course I would, Wilbur. Let's just discuss which address belongs to whom. We should all three go to Jake Cooper. He seems the most interesting among them."
"Choose from the other two then," the bear suggested.
"You go to Hermione, and I'll visit David King."
"Won't you be in trouble alone, Miss Evans?"
"I can take care of myself," I replied, pointing toward my service weapon. "Wilbur, would you find out something else for me?"
"What would you like, Miss Evans?"
"Get the address of the Alioglu family from somewhere. You know, the family whose son was set on fire."
"All right," he answered, closing the door.

I didn't remember David King or Hermione from the times I was there. I would have been surprised if Jessica had only given me a list of people who ended up at Neon Nights after I was taken away. I remembered the faces of all the gentlemen who worked there at the time, but I couldn't remember any of them by the name of David King. He must have changed his personality too, I thought. It wouldn't surprise me, most of us did.

Poor little Alice. How scared she was when she got into the Neon Nights. How many terrible mistakes she made. How much she held herself as nothing. I couldn't blame her. She couldn't do otherwise than pile mistake upon mistake. She had no idea how she should take up the fight with life and acted completely instinctively. A few years later, however, she was no longer alive. She ceased to exist. She stopped being Alice forever. Alice, who in her surname didn't even know whose genes she was carrying forward. Alice Bucks died prematurely. But as the years passed, from Alice's ashes, Amanda Evans was born—the woman who was ready to whitewash Alice's memory.

David King lived in a narrow street that had advanced into a public space from a row of alleys forming at the back of several old tenement houses; yet it didn't exude the bad vibe that such places usually do in movies. God only knows how any vegetation settled in this dark place; from wherever the sun shone, it was always in shadow. Anyone visiting here for the first time would know they wouldn't meet wealthy people. It was depressing to see this, since the gentlemen earned plenty of money to easily buy apartments even in the city's prestigious districts.

David King's name was on the intercom, which meant he didn't rent the apartment he lived in. At least he managed that much in life, I thought. I dreaded the meeting, even though I had never known him. I recoiled from talking to a person who was part of the exploitation of the girls, even if, as it seemed, all he got out of it was to buy an apartment in this pathetic neighborhood. I couldn't feel sorry for him. He could have lost much more, and even that wouldn't have been enough.

Many of us became drug addicts because they couldn't stand wanting to throw up every morning, so much did they disgust themselves with whom they saw in the mirror. However, this wasn't only the case for the girls. Some gentlemen also couldn't process what they did day in and day out. David King must have been among them.
A thin man's voice spoke in the intercom. He let me in.

The apartment was on the third floor. In the stairwell and the hallway, the plaster was peeling, while the walls exuded a slight smell of damp. Knowing myself, I would have committed suicide if I had had to live here. To my surprise, however, a polite stranger greeted me. He came out into the hallway to make sure I found my way.
"Thank you!" I greeted him, trying to remain firm.
"Come in! Forgive me, but I didn't catch your name well on the intercom," he said. "It static crackles so much, you can hear almost nothing."
"I'm Amanda Evans. Prosecutor," I continued the introduction.
"Prosecutor?" King was surprised. "Prosecutors don't visit my apartment very often. In fact, you're the first."
Unlike his apartment, there was nothing strange in this. It took me a while to realize what made the room look that way. The whole place was full of colorful accessories and clothing items.
"Please, have a seat!" he then pointed to the sofa on the side of the room.
"Very kind."
"Can I get you something?" he inquired. "A hot tea?"
"No, thank you."
It was quite chilly in those days, and a cup would have felt good, but I wouldn't have drunk uncertain drinks in a strange place even if I had been completely frozen.
"Do you live here alone?" I asked.
"Yes," he replied. "Are you asking because of the skirts?" he tried to guess the real reason for my question, since in half of the room, quite a few skirts indeed lay scattered.
"Actually, yes," I answered, slightly embarrassed.
"Those are mine," he smiled. "But I don't go out on the street in them," he excused himself.
"Oh, I just..." I would have begun to explain when he instead started.
"Those are costumes. I'm a performing artist. Though not a very successful one. I perform in several places, at the Molière Rúzs, for instance. I perform in drag shows."
"Wow," escaped my surprise, though I had seen enough similar things at the Neon Nights back then. "And how long have you worked there?"
"Well, there is no such thing as there. Always elsewhere. Of course, I perform in one place multiple times, like the Molière Rúzs, but I don't have one specific place."
"When did you start?"
"I wish I could say recently," he replied. "But surely ten years ago. Is this why you came?" I failed to detect any fear in his voice.
"No, not exactly. But I came because of you, obviously."
"Because of me?" he was surprised. "I'm not aware of any proceedings against me."
"There aren't any," I replied. "I'd like to hear you as a witness."
"As a witness?" he was puzzled. "But what was I a witness to?"
"I'd be interested in a club where you supposedly worked. The Neon Nights."
"Aha, that one," his tone dropped suddenly. I felt disappointment in his voice.
"My information is correct, isn't it?"
"Yes, of course. That's why I fell silent."
"Do you have such a bad opinion of the place?" I played ignorant.
"Opinion?" he asked back. "No, it's not my opinion that is bad. I don't have an opinion of it."
"Then what?"
"Phew," he blew. "I just woke up, I didn't think I'd have to answer such a question today... I don't know. I have feelings. Mostly painful feelings."
"Why?" I tried to probe, but David King seemed to only now begin to realize what an unusual situation he was in. He decided he wanted to restart the conversation instead.
"What did you say your name was?"
"Amanda Evans," I repeated my name again, this time without my profession.
"How did you get my apartment address?"
"As I said, I'm a prosecutor. My detectives tracked it down."
"I see," King answered shortly. "Then how did you find out about me?"
"You see, that is a question deserving a very different answer."
"I'm really glad of that," he replied. "Because I'd like to know."
"From Jessica Marsh."
David King pulled his lips into a smile.
"Then you are on the good side too?" he asked, almost whispering.
"That depends on how you look at it. But how do you mean, if I'm on the good side?"
"Jessica Marsh was a special girl. So if you are her person too, then yes, you are on the good side."
"I see what you mean. Then my answer will reassure you. She specifically asked me to visit you."
"But why?"
"She said you could help her."
"Me help her? Is she in trouble?"
"No," I replied shortly. "You can help by telling us everything."
"That will take quite a lot of time," he answered.
"Then only what I ask. What did you do at the Neon Nights?"
"At first, what everyone else did. I was a paid sex worker."
"Paid?" I was surprised by his answer.
"Yes, paid."
"Jessica Marsh claimed that the girls were worked almost as slaves."
"Sure, that was true. The girls didn't get paid. But we did."
"Who is we?"
"The men who did the same work as the girls."
"But why?" I was outraged, but soon realized that Amanda Evans had no reason to be outraged by something a former sex worker was telling her.
"I can't tell you, but that's how it was. Regardless, I think that I and those like me were at the very bottom of the ladder."
"At the very bottom? With pay in your pockets?" I threw back at him. What he said hurt.
"Yes. But a man in that environment usually had a harder time." I really couldn't listen to what he said with a straight face. My outrage sat on my face.

"Don't get me wrong," he interrupted himself. "The question of money was only one of many. But no matter how vulnerable the girls were in the Neon Nights, they still received more protection than we did. We had no leader to deal with us. We belonged to the club, but we did the most pitiful work. We had no say in the shaping of our own fate. The girls' leaders behind the scenes tried to fight for the best opportunities for their own. We, however, had no choice at all if a group of clients arrived who wanted our company. There were few of us boys.
I see," I seemingly accepted his answer, much as I would have liked to detail at length the things in which I had no say either.

Then I realized that this man did the same work as I. He was not in a single bit better position, and I didn't even know how he would have had the chance to spend his money, because even visiting malls on a weekly basis in the company of my gentleman, I wouldn't have been able to buy even a pack of gum without him. And he, according to this, couldn't go out. There was no one to go with. It was useless to debate which of us was in a more hopeless position. If he really worked as a prostitute, he deserved this little apartment; I didn't begrudge him it.
"You couldn't go out?" I inquired.
"As I said, there were few of us. There was no day off at the club. I worked through those years without a rest day. Except if I got sick. But there was hardly an example of that."
"How long did Matt work there?"
"For six years?" I disbelieved, especially since I should have known him.
"Almost six, yes."

I was surprised. I couldn't place David King's face. It wasn't familiar. I could have blamed it on it happening long ago, but that would have been no excuse. I thought I'd recognize everyone from those times. King, according to this, was an exception.
"Forgive me," he continued. "I don't want to intrude, and it might seem like a cliché, but don't we know each other? You seem familiar."
"I'm sorry, but I don't think so," I denied, though I wasn't lying to him either; I really didn't remember him. "I'm quite introverted, I don't really go out."
"Just a feeling," he replied, "it doesn't matter."
"You said you only worked as a prostitute at first. Later what was your job, did you become a supervisor?"
"Gentlemen? No way! Nobody became a gentleman who came from the bottom. There was a separate procedure for that."
"Then?"
"Look around. What do you think? I gave transvestite performances."

Then it suddenly clicked who he was. I recalled how once on stage he gave a truly captivating performance from La Cage aux Folles. His face did look similar. Back then, however, he was about thirty kilos thinner, completely fragile, not muscular like now.
"Was that all your job?"
"Not only. My previous job description naturally remained. But a show of my own was definitely considered an advancement. And I liked doing those. It helped me survive."
"How would you describe your six years spent at the Neon Nights?"
"You really don't stop with the hard questions, do you?"
"Due to the case, unfortunately, I can't stop," I excused myself, though I didn't consider it such a hard question.
"It's not important how I experienced it. You see, to this day, I deal with what I learned there."
"Then what is important?" I inquired.

"That not everyone was as lucky as I. Three of my companions disappeared; to this day I know nothing about them. They left the club before it closed. Countless girls were ruined. Many were taken, passed on. How could this happen?"
"Look, because of the investigation, I can't say anything for now. But how do you feel, would you be willing to report on your experiences there in court?"

"I have to think about that," King answered. "Although the Neon Nights officially ceased to exist, the organization certainly remained. Besides, I don't know if you know who directed the whole thing."
"Do you mean the president?"

"I mean the president. Obviously Jessica didn't keep it a secret." He compressed his thin lips and thought for a moment. "So you too can understand if I weigh what I can win or lose with such a testimony."
"Of course I understand. But we're going to need witnesses. Do you know anyone else who could testify?"
"To tell the truth, no. I haven't bumped into anyone since I got away from there. I didn't want to, either. I bought this apartment with my saved money and tried to do my thing quietly."

"Then I hope you'll say yes in the end," I answered. I didn't even want to hear that he wanted to protect even this insignificant existence of his. "If something should occur to you, you can call me at this number anytime. Anytime." I emphasized the flexibility of my availability, while handing a business card to David King.
"It's okay" he answered. "Maybe I'll call you. Maybe I'll confess, maybe not" he answered uncertainly.
"Contact me!" I turned back from the door. "There are few people who can come into play. You are needed."
"Goodbye!" he said, closing the door.

chapters.title: Chapter Eight
paragraphs: So David King was no gentleman. He was a simple prostitute who later turned to trance shows. I couldn't quite take in what he said. My meditation was interrupted by the honking of some cars. People scrambling to catch all kinds of buses. It was foggy and it looked like spray was falling from the sky. My coat was slowly soaking wet as I argued with myself why I hadn't picked up the umbrella from the door in the morning. I headed for the subway. And my thoughts revolved around the case again.

I didn't understand why he was glad that I was on the good side if he didn't want to help. In fact, how foolish it sounds: the good side. We weren't divided into camps of good and bad. None of us was sinless. At least I didn't feel that way, and as far as I knew, neither did Jessica. We were victims, but not sinless. In reality, there was no good side on which we could stand. When did we become guilty? Who knows. But someone who is good doesn't decide to give up themselves and start stripteasing. We weren't good, but we didn't know what it meant to be, either. We were obedient for a long time, and although they labeled us as good in return, we knew this wasn't true. For someone who is good, a male organ doesn't hang out of her mouth at the age of nine, nor from anywhere else.
Jess and I learned early that being a "good girl" actually meant bad, because real good girls didn't do what the "good girls" did. Not even, perhaps, if they had no other choice.

Regardless, I hoped for David King's testimony. His story was authentic. It would have looked really good in a trial, especially when he spoke of his ruined acquaintances. But then he realized he was afraid, realized he was over it, and already had something to lose. I was angry at this man because of that. Jess's presence might convince him, I thought. He hadn't given her name for nothing.

We planned to go to the address belonging to Jake Cooper with the detectives in the afternoon. I didn't know how things were going with Hermione, but I thought they could get important information. I didn't know Hermione, though after my meeting with David King I was no longer so sure of that. At any rate, I didn't remember her under such a name.

What was I expecting? Unlike other investigations, I no longer had to satisfy myself that the accused was indeed guilty. Skipping past that, I already had to work on gathering the evidence. You might think the two are the same, but somehow they aren't. Every time I started an investigation, there was always a moment when I felt I already believed the person was guilty. Until that happened, however, I focused much more on convincing myself. I was interested not only in legally valid evidence, but in everything that could strengthen suspicion and thereby convince me.

Bias to believe someone is guilty without proper evidence? Perhaps. But I couldn't work otherwise. I didn't dispense the truth. My job was to search out all evidence that could support the charge. I just wanted to get closer to the truth.

Now, however, I didn't have to convince myself. From the start, I had to look at the case through completely different glasses. If I didn't feel something could serve as evidence, I didn't bother with it. David King's story was interesting, but he said nothing that fundamentally reshaped my image of Neon Nights. It didn't help me. But it could have helped the court if he honestly recounted how he experienced his years spent there.

I reckoned with Hermione in a similar way. If she wants to talk, and knows what to talk about, that's great. If, on the other hand, she doesn't want to stand before the general public, I won't be able to use her for anything, just as if she didn't exist.

Precisely because of this, I had to trust Jessica that she had given good names. All my hope lay in Jessica to connect me with those who could meaningfully advance our case. Unfortunately, there weren't many, as I also told David King.

I definitely wanted to have lunch before the meeting. It was past noon by the time my train pulled into François Étudiant Square. There was a bistro near the prosecutor's office, where I always found something I liked from the daily menu. To my surprise, Anton also called me about this.
"I'm almost there," I replied.
"Five minutes for me, but then your jaw is going to drop."

Anton knew how to exaggerate. As a writer, I felt he sometimes saw the world in more colorful tones than it actually was. My jaw hadn't dropped from anything in quite a long time, and it wasn't on the agenda that something would happen to cause this accident after all.
I ordered two chickens; we both liked it, besides, I could be sure of what we were getting. When he arrived, he sat opposite me with almost stormy speed.
"You won't believe this," he began.
"I can't wait not to believe it. I haven't ordered a drink yet, what should I get?"
"The graphologist said that Jessica must know the person who wrote the threat."
"Interesting..."
"You don't believe him?"

"It doesn't really matter what I believe, Anton," I phrased it somewhat bluntly, though I didn't want to offend him; I was hungry. "The question is whether we can use it for anything. For now, I don't see how, for several reasons. Let's assume Jess really knows who wrote this. Would that surprise us? Not really, since she knew the whole Neon Nights."
"But it's still strange that a woman wrote it."
"A woman?" I asked back. "That indeed makes the whole thing more interesting."
"What kind of day did you have?" he inquired.
"The idiot witness, I fear, won't play ball," then I told him what we talked about with David King.
"You really didn't remember him?" he asked.
"Why, did you?"
"It was the first trance show I saw."
"And how do you think I should convince him?"
"You don't have to convince him," he replied. "Jess will. Just give her time." Vivacity shone in his blue eyes, then after he felt he had reassured me, he waved to a server.
"Two lemonades, please," I finally said to the waiter.

chapters.title: Chapter Nine
paragraphs: We met Matt and Wilbur at 14:45 in the parking lot in front of the prosecutor's office. When I inquired on the phone about what they had learned from Hermione, they admitted, somewhat uncomfortably, that not much, because she refused to speak with them. It did turn out, however, that Hermione cannot see.

I only knew one girl who was blind in the Neon Nights, and she didn't get there that way either. She lost her sight as a result of an accident, or to be completely honest, a client beat her in the face with a champagne bottle so badly that her optic nerve was damaged. Her name was Mary Morales. She was a very beautiful Latina girl; many adored her. Even after the incident, her popularity did not wane. She continued everything just the same. Maybe it even helped her that she could no longer see who she was with. It would have helped me, but they never allowed the lights to be turned off when we were with a client.

I was inclined to believe that Mary Morales was Hermione, only that she lived on under a stage name after the Neon Nights. This suspicion of mine was also supported by the detectives' physical description. We weren't any further along with this, of course, because I had to get her to talk somehow.
"She will talk," Matt said. "She just needs a big shot she'll associate with."
"Is that what she said?" I asked back. "What did she say exactly?"
"That she won't talk to detectives, especially not if Neon Nights is the subject. She wanted someone she trusts to look for her."
"And who could it be? Jess...?" I inquired, then pretended that I had suddenly thought of something else, and only then added that:. "...yikes" The two detectives were not disturbed by it.
"She wants to speak with the prosecutor."
"Interesting..." I cut it short. "Why is it better for her if she speaks with me?"

Jake Cooper lived in the worst neighborhood of the city. The roads leading there were often bordered by regular mountains of garbage. One had the feeling as if the waste collected in the other half of the city was dumped here. It was more likely, however, that even the garbage collectors avoided this area from afar. I was surprised that Jake Cooper's address was in this place.
"Where did you find this address?" I asked the detectives somewhat accusingly.
"A source of ours whispered it," they answered.
"And is that source directly messing with you?"
"Don't worry, the address is correct," Matt answered.

"We understand that you're worried," Wilbur continued. "You don't need to be afraid next to us. There are neighborhoods like this, people live here too. They are more afraid of you than the other way around."

Jake Cooper's house was a single-story lightweight building. At some point there might have been a small garden in front of it; now rusty barrels stood askew, surrounded by all kinds of metal junk. In front of the house stood a sun-bleached yellowish sun lounger; the umbrella must have been blown away by time long ago, if there ever was one. From the outside, it didn't look like anyone lived here.

Wilbur got out of the car and looked around the house. Matt stayed with me in the meantime. I really was afraid. I wasn't used to such neighborhoods; besides, several people ventured out onto the street. When they saw the detectives' car, they stared for minutes. We caused no small stir; they knew exactly who didn't belong here. Wilbur went up to one and another.
"They live here," he said as he got back into the car.
"It doesn't look like it," I replied.
"They haven't seen the person for weeks."
"And is that Jake Cooper?" I inquired.
"They didn't reveal his name, but I saw from them that they know him."
"And do you believe them?"
"They don't lie about such things. Even if their mouths say something else, it's clearly discernable what the truth is. They are always protecting someone."

Unfortunate people. Are they truly more afraid of us than the other way around? The detectives were certainly right. A scary exterior can stem from many things: illnesses, addictions. It is not the repulsive person who becomes poor, but the other way around. The one living in misery becomes someone everyone despises. What can someone do who is born here? This will be home for them, and they won't understand the other world around them. Everything will be foreign to them; only this misery, this will be familiar. What can take a person out of here? What kind of dreams must one believe in to get away from here? Jake Cooper had a dream. And to free himself, he had to ensnare us.
"We must find this man," I said, though I hated the very thought of speaking to him.
"We'll watch the place," Wilbur promised. "If he shows up, we'll bring him in."
"I fear he'll take flight if we aren't careful enough."
"Or he already did," Matt suggested.
"No!" I replied. "How could he have known we'd look for him? He will return if he lives here."
Suddenly, a text message interrupted the conversation.
"Miss Evans!" Matt continued. "Our man just sent over the Alioglu family's address."
"Already?" I was surprised.
"It's quite close to here, shall we check it?"
"By all means!" I replied. My heart was suddenly in my throat.

I felt this lead was more alive than the others. I didn't know how thoroughly they had investigated Mike's case, but if they brought up the Neon Nights in connection with it, I wouldn't have been surprised if they had wrapped up the investigation prematurely. I hoped the family might still share some information that the police hadn't taken seriously enough, which could bring us closer to who Mike was in contact with in the organization.

The Alioglu family's house was ten minutes from Jake Cooper's assumed home, albeit in the same district. This already counted as a better neighborhood; the feeling of fear also subsided in me, and I had to realize that my weapon only promised false security, since I didn't want to use it at all. Here, however, it didn't even cross my mind that it was on me; I got out of the car immediately as we stopped. The street was lined with sycamores, wrapping me in a suburban atmosphere.

In front of the house, a clothesline was stretched across, but in the damp, wet weather, only water droplets dried on it. The light was already on in the window; it was slowly getting dark. I knocked, while the two detectives served as backup behind me. A stocky woman of about fifty opened the door.
"Good day!" I greeted her. "Is this the Alioglu house?"
The woman stared at us with narrowed eyes, nodding uncertainly.
"I'm prosecutor Amanda Evans. These gentlemen behind me are detectives. We'd like to ask a few questions."
"About what?" the lady was puzzled.
"Regarding the death of Mike Alioglu."
Her large body shuddered slightly; terror sat in her eyes, she was not in the mood to open the door any wider.
"Mike died a long time ago. Why should we still talk about him?"
"His name came up in another investigation," I replied.
"I don't think so. He was a good boy. He wouldn't hurt a fly. You're looking for the wrong person."
I understood the mother's reaction, and indeed I was flooded with the sympathy I had already felt when reading Mike's file.
"Then tell me who we should look for instead."
The woman looked at the ground, then decided she would receive us after all.
"Come in! Just don't touch anything!"

The prohibition might have seemed an exaggeration, but looking around the room, I understood better why she said it. A huge number of items were tastefully arranged on the shelves. It must have taken hours to tidy up, which she might do daily. Three young people sat on the living room sofa, and who knows how many more lived here. She said something loudly in Turkish to them, and upon this, they moved off to another room.

"Have a seat!" she offered, and we all three sat on the sofa next to each other. "Coffee!" she ordered whoever heard her word, and immediately two jumped to bring us coffee. None of us objected. A family-like atmosphere prevailed; it almost felt good.
"Thank you!" I said on behalf of the others too. "Please don't be angry that we burst in on you like this, but it would be good to know more about Mike's death."
"Why?" she asked back.
"Because we need to see clearly what kind of company he got mixed up with."
"I see," she acknowledged. "Then I will tell the police for the last time that Mike was not the kind of person they tried to portray him as."
"What kind of person was he, and what kind of person did they try to portray him as?" I inquired.
"You didn't handle his case, did you?" she looked at Wilbur.
"No. We wandered here from somewhere else entirely."

The youth put the coffee down in front of us, then moved away. Their mother took a deep breath; it was palpable how much she did not want to speak of Mike's death again, besides she might also have feared that she would uselessly reopen the wounds, achieving nothing by it anyway.

"Mike was a good boy. He helped a very great deal. Of course, no matter how much I told everyone this, they only kept repeating that he got mixed up in bad company, that's why he died. But that wasn't true."
"Why are you so sure of that?" I put the question in a somewhat provocative way, to which in a mother's eyes I certainly had no right.
"Because I knew him," she replied concisely and firmly.
"A teenager has secrets from his parents."
I didn't push the woman out of her patience. She spoke to one of her sons. When he came closer, she asked him.
"Where were you yesterday at six in the evening?"
The boy answered with a grin on his face.
"With Zake and Melissa in the parking lot."
"And what did you do?"
"We smoked, talked. We ate hot dogs. The vendor raised the price."
"How much?" the mother asked.
"50 cents."
"Who else was there?"
"A lot of people. The Brighton Twins."
"Is Sue still busy with you?"
"If it turns out otherwise, I'll let you know," he smiled.
"You may go."
The boy went into another room. He wasn't embarrassed by his mother's questions; it seemed their relationship with each other was completely normal.
"That was Daniel. He's a big swindler, but he's honest. I may not always know where he is, but if I ask, he will tell me. I have a good relationship with my children."
"I see," I cut in.

"Mike was not like that. He didn't need to be questioned. He was like an angel. Even when he was little, if someone hurt a bug, the poor boy was completely beside himself. A more sensitive person did not live on this earth than him."
"I believe you," I replied. "But for some reason he died. What do you think could have happened?"
"I don't know for sure, but I know who could know."
"And who?"
"The witness whom the police questioned. He was there, so I'm sure he knows what happened, he just told it differently."
"Why did he do that?" I asked.
"Because he was afraid. First they said he might have killed him, obviously he shit himself. He started talking about all sorts of things."
"Do you think he lied?" I inquired.
"He just didn't tell the truth, that he was the one who wanted to be a member of that organization."
"Which organization?" I asked back. I wanted to hear the obvious from her.
"The Neon Nights. You know, that prostitute place."
It felt good to hear these words from another mouth too. I felt there was at least one person who knew what a terrible place the Neon Nights was without ever having been there.
"But ask around the neighborhood. Everyone knows that organization was fishing around here."
"How do you mean?"
"Isn't it clear?" she countered. "There were many young boys here who wanted money. They were constantly coaxing them to work for them."
"Do you know what kind of work they were recruiting them for?"
"No. That I don't know. But surely for something dark. Quite a few fell for it. Mike feared for them too."
"Mike feared for them?"
"I told you before how sensitive he was. Of course he worried. He grew up with many of them. I think on that night too, he wanted to act the hero."

Based on the mother's account, a completely different picture of the boy emerged before me, and I couldn't see why Mike Alioglu couldn't have been as his mother described. Why couldn't he have been a decent, sensitive guy who, unlike the others, understood that there could be no well-paying easy work, especially not in this neighborhood? I believed his mother that Mike feared for his companions.
"What was Mike like? Physically," I inquired.
"Beautiful," the mother replied. "He was strong and had a beautiful face. I'm sure many girls liked him."
"Did the others look up to him?"
"He was a popular boy. Yes, I think they listened to him."
"What happened on that night, in your opinion? How do you mean he wanted to act the hero?"
I wanted to reconstruct this scene somehow, and in the mother's mind, I thought, a much more precise picture of that night lived than what the police outlined during the interrogation.

"There was this friend of his. He worried about him a lot. He told me the boy spoke to these people. He didn't say what they lured him with, only that they offered him a very lucrative job. He knew Mike wanted to work too, to help out here at home. I didn't insist on it; we got by. The boy, however, forced that it would be good. Mike saw through the screen and wanted to talk him out of it at all costs, but couldn't, so he thought he'd approach it from elsewhere."
"Is that why Mike met with the Neon Nights people?"

"Apparently, yes." The mother stared at the ground for seconds. She fell silent. I could almost see her conjuring up the image of her son. "He shouldn't have been there. I have no idea what he was thinking. Surely he spoke to them; I think he wanted to influence them to leave this neighborhood. I don't think he thought he could be harmed. The whole thing made no sense. Mike couldn't represent a threat to them. He was a simple local boy who liked living here and loved his friends. They couldn't understand that, because they were barbarians."
I didn't even know what to say. I felt the mother's pain. Her son's death was senseless. And the way they killed him, brutal. I only hoped that in time, it would come to light who did it.
"Are you sure it happened that way?" I asked, because she presented it very convincingly.
"No," the woman snapped immediately. "I'm not sure. But the story is a thousand times more believable this way than how the police presented it."
"Then where do you get all this?"
"Mike's friend told it this way at the funeral."
"And why to the police differently?"
"Because he was afraid, Miss Evans. He was barely sixteen. He was terrified of being harmed."
"Yet he spoke to them about the Neon Nights."
"Yes, he spoke. He had every reason to."
"Wasn't he afraid they would retaliate?"

"No. He didn't fear that. He knew exactly that if the Neon Nights was mentioned, the police would close the case as quickly as possible, and since they had no evidence against him, they'd leave him alone."
"It was a clever move," Matt noted unexpectedly. The mother just stared ahead.
"And you let the matter rest?" I was puzzled.

"I didn't blame him for it. Mike too would have wanted him not to be harmed. People stick together here. He didn't want to hurt Mike. And where would I have gone if I pushed? I too only knew what Jake told me."
"Who is he?" I asked back. In the blink of an eye, the power went out of me. Mrs. Alioglu immediately noticed my excitement.
"Is everything all right with you, prosecutor?"
"Mike's friend was Jake Cooper?" I put the question to her nervously.
"Yes, Miss Evans," she replied calmly. "As I see, then he is the one you are looking for."

My emotions were swirling inside me. I got excited. Jake Cooper was the witness from whose point of view I got to know the events, and as it turned out, he revealed a completely skewed truth to me. Now I really wanted to meet him, I wanted to look into his eyes, and I wanted to know if he recognized me. After he hired us, we never, not once spoke. He didn't become a gentleman. I didn't know where he went, what happened to him. I didn't really know anything about it, but it changed my whole life.

At first, I didn't feel he was an important person, yet wherever I stepped, I stumbled into him. He was like a phantom. I had no idea what he actually did, didn't know what his role was in how things shaped up; he was simply there.

In parallel with this, however, I also calmed down. I felt a circle closed, which could have led even into infinity, yet rather took mercy and decided it doesn't lead into the depths of labyrinths. I had to speak with him. I had to find out what role he had in all that happened to us.

If Mike had remained alive, perhaps everything would have shaped up differently. Maybe Jess and I would never have ended up in the Neon Nights, but a different, easier life would have awaited us. Of course, I didn't want to deceive myself. In our despair, someone else would have come who found us and, like Jake, got us in there, since in that situation we saw no other solution than to escape from the home.
"Mike would have just turned thirty" Mrs. Alioglu spoke after a short pause. "what age are you" nailed the question to me.
From her tone, I sensed she perhaps wanted to ask more by this than what she said.
"Twenty-five," I replied.
"Very young," she noted. "Two years younger than the boys too."

"I don't feel like it," I picketed. "I've already experienced a few things in life. The fact that I'm not yet thirty, unfortunately, means nothing. I haven't seen the world in rosy colors for a long time."
"Oh, of course you don't," she replied. "You seem like a determined woman. You know exactly what you want, and also why."
"Why are you telling me this?" I was puzzled.
She didn't answer.
"When did you last meet Jake?" I searched.
"At the funeral or not long after."
"That long ago?"
"Yes, then for the last time."
"But do you know that Jake still lives here in the area?"
"Sure, though to tell you the truth, I haven't heard anything about him for quite a while. I'd rather say I know he lived here."
"Do you know where?"
"Somewhere by the numbered streets."
"Have you been there?"
"In the neighborhood, yes. At their place, no. I didn't know his parents. Because of Mike, he was here multiple times."
"How is it that you still remember him?"
"I don't think you're seriously asking that" he answered, and he was right. I actually wanted to make sure he was telling the truth. Mrs. Alioglu stood up and went to the closet.
"Could I ask you for something?" she asked.
"What would it be?"
She pulled out a photo of Mike.
"If you find Jake, would you give him this picture?"
"Why?" I asked back, because I found it strange.
"Just give it to him, please."

There was something captivating in her voice. I had no idea what was in her head, but I didn't want to question her about it either. I looked at the picture. A very handsome, dark-eyed, cheerful boy looked back at me. He was barely sixteen.

chapters.title: Chapter Ten
paragraphs: January 26, 2022
In the morning, a message was waiting on my phone. Jessica sent it. It was just a picture and a sentence: "Fuck them!". I zoomed in on the picture and immediately handed it to Anton.
"Look!"
"Is this another threat?" he asked, as if he had sensed it.
"That's what it looks like."
"Then withdraw the complaint!" he said immediately.
"Ugh," I protested. "Fuck them!"
I called Jess immediately.
"Where did you find it?" I asked.
"In the mailbox, this one too."
"Aren't they tired of it yet?"
In the background, I heard a man's voice arguing, then he took the phone from Jessica.
"Miss Evans?!"
"Yes."
Jefferson had been dating Jess for years, but obviously I had never seen him. I had no idea who he was. Now he was distraught, speaking almost in a shout.
"I don't know how long you want to play with fire, stop it! Let this whole thing end! We are moving somewhere far away. We are not going to live like this!"
"I understand," I replied calmly, surprising even myself by how much. "Please give the phone back to Jessica."
"Jessica!" I said. "We have the opportunity for you to withdraw the complaint."
"But then I can't do it again," she replied.
"That's true, but we can. You can still testify just the same."
"I will only discredit the whole thing with that, Miss Evans."
"Withdraw it under the influence of threats. This might even strengthen our positions."
"I'm not afraid of them!" she insisted. "I'm not going to back down. It's like former colleagues want to silence me."
Jessica paused.
"I'll think about it," she answered finally. "My fiancé is truly unbearable when he worries. I swear, that will be my biggest reason for it."
"Do it, Jessica, leave the rest to us!" I reassured her. "Also, since we're speaking, could you perhaps talk to David King?"
"Why me?" she asked back.
"I feel he is uncertain. I fear he won't want to testify."
"It's ironic that you ask me this precisely when you want me to withdraw the complaint."
"But I don't want to drop the case, Jessica!" I defended myself.
"It doesn't matter anyway..."
Anton suddenly reached for my phone and hung up.
"What are you doing?!" I yelled at him.
"There's no point in you two fighting now."
"We fight when we want to," I replied, but I didn't call Jessica back; I just left Anton standing there and went into the bathroom.
Jessica behaved completely unreasonably. I hated when someone tried to paint me as selfish. I knew Jess didn't think that, yet the sarcasm with which she emphasized her last sentence bit into me.

Jeff might have overreacted to the situation, though his fear could be understood. He only knew Neon Nights from stories, so he could expect anything from them. To tell the truth, I didn't really believe Jessica's life was in danger either, but it was possible that we underestimated their baseness.

The fact of the threat, however, didn't fit in my head. Every day I received the reports, and after a quick query, it turned out that the police ordered for surveillance didn't notice any movement around Jessica's house during the night. Thinking it over, however, several methods presented themselves for someone to deliver the threatening message to Jessica's mailbox, and it wasn't important to catch a courier, but that Jess wouldn't be harmed.

So I still found the police presence around the house satisfactory, and I still wanted to maintain it. If I only make things more difficult for the Neon Nights by them being there, it was worth it. Jess could take care of herself anyway.

Regardless, I really hoped that she would finally agree and do what was necessary. I felt everything would become easier from it, especially since we were just beginning to dig deeper into the leads unfolding before us.
Anton timidly tried to approach me, but I rejected him.
"Do you think this is the right moment for this?" I asked condescendingly.

He slinked away as if nothing else mattered in life besides sex. I didn't understand how it even occurred to him after this morning. Before the message, of course, he would still have had the opportunity, but so much had happened since! The inspiration dissolved in the morning fog.
"I'm going to Hermione's," I said.
"Okay, good luck!" he replied with excessive kindness. I felt he wasn't sincere, but he restrained himself.

I realized that was my problem with his approach too. He was spineless, like an idiot. If he had really wanted it, he would have taken it. Yet now it would have been really good to feel wanted. Jessica's guy was whimpering, and mine had the chance to prove himself, but he screwed up. If he had been able to make me his own, at least I would have felt safe... but men can be really stupid sometimes.

Hermione lived in a place where I could imagine myself. I wouldn't have traded our suburban house for an apartment, but I could have gotten used to the doorman at our gate. Even despite him being annoyingly busybody, so I had to quiet him down by showing my badge. After this morning, say, I wasn't sure I didn't have a greater need for this.
I wasn't wrong about Hermione. I did know her. Standing in the doorway opposite me was Mary Morales.
"Come in!" she said in an extremely direct style.
"I'm prosecutor Amanda Evans," I greeted her, ignoring the obvious fact that she already knew who I was.
"Amanda Evans..." she laughed. "You're Alice. Alice Bucks. You don't think I forgot your voice, do you?" She practically jumped at me to hug me.

It was hard for me to react to what happened. For a moment I grew even angrier at Jessica, because I thought she might have told her that I was the one who would look for her. During the conversation, however, it turned out that Jess hadn't spoken to Mary for years.
"How did you know it was me?" I asked finally, because I saw no point in my denial.

"The two detectives you sent here tried to persuade me to talk. They brought up the prosecutor leading the investigation, and from a few of their words it became clear that it could only be you. You always said you'd have these locked up one day. Of course, many people make irresponsible promises, but when I went blind, I remember you were the first to come to me. Blood was pouring from my head. I wasn't completely conscious, but it burned into me how you said it. I knew you meant it seriously."

I was almost embarrassed by this warm welcome, of course it was good that Mary remembered this moment and took it seriously. I really got there first. Poor Mary fell against the glass table, which completely shattered. His back and arm were also shattered by shrapnel. Although one of the occasions was often so intense that even a horse race would have envied the pace, it was still unusual for the equipment to suffer at the end. We knew it wasn't an accident. Mary's face was covered in blood. The fat bastard was sweating and panting over her with a broken champagne bottle in his hand. He wasn't wearing pants, his tail was still hard, and all I could think about was putting that sharp glass on him instead of the most special beauty of Neon Nights. The client was led out by the gentleman, and then somehow they settled the matter between themselves. Of course, Mary had seen nothing of it. Nor from the extra jatt they paid for it as compensation.
"Look Mary, no one can find out who I am."
"Don't worry about that" he answered. "I certainly won't tell anyone. I didn't talk to the detectives either."
"Do you know why I came?"
"Actually, no. But if you introduced yourself as prosecutor Amanda Evans, you can only want to fulfill your promise."
"Yes, that's true," I replied.
"But why do you need me for this?" she asked.
"You know why I need you. To testify."
"Sure, with a thousand pleasures," she snapped, as if there were no tomorrow.
I was slightly surprised. Mary hadn't even thought about the matter.
"You say yes already?" I disbelieved.
"Yes. Why wouldn't I?"
"Don't you want to think it through?"
"What should I think through? You want to punish those who abused and exploited us for so many years. Do you know how long I've been waiting for this?"
I didn't know.
"Since when?"
"What kind of question is that? Since you first said it. I believed you. And lo and behold, now you stand here, calling me to testify."

I felt strange, as if this whole thing embarrassed me. When I came here, we were still at the point where I had to convince someone named Hermione to speak at all. And now, a moment later, I already had a witness.
"You must know what you are undertaking," I would have begun, because I felt Mary didn't see the situation completely realistically, but she interrupted.
"Alice! Or rather Amanda... I better practice this name of yours."
"Yes, let it be Amanda for the sake of order," I replied, then heard how condescending it sounded. I wanted to clarify: "Alice is dead."
"You died?" Mary wondered.
"Yes," I replied shortly, but far from determination flowed from my voice, rather some deep sadness. I felt sorry for Alice. "Alice died, more precisely."
"Amanda. I know what you are asking. You don't have to worry about me."
"Jessica was threatened twice," I emphasized the validity of my fear.
"We all know what they are like," she snapped. "And I hate them!" Sparks flew from her blind eyes. "I hate them, do you understand?!"
"I understand," I replied shortly, because how could I not.
I understood, though I could never know what she must have experienced. Even though she went blind, she worked just the same. No one cared what happened.

"I know what you believe now," she explained. "But I felt only hatred for them even before they did this to me. This really didn't change anything. That I went blind? What does it matter. What did it matter there whether you see or not? Sometimes you might even have envied me for it. All those disgusting pieces of shit we were with. They wanted slaves so they could do everything."
"They knew we were."

"And that turned them on. That dick, do you remember him? You were there too. We didn't start the profession then. Was I supposed to apologize for not choking on him? That it wasn't big enough to strangle me? He took offense at being laughed at. 'Live happily with it, man,' I said, 'but you don't have to think you're Rocco.' He couldn't have been used to someone speaking to him as if he were mortal too, so he hit me on the head. I wasn't scared. I had no plans back then."
"I remember," I replied, "and you're saying even now you have nothing to risk?"
"There is nothing I'm afraid of losing."
"What do you do, Mary?" I inquired. "Who is Hermione?"
"The witch from Harry Potter."
"Do you perform magic?"
"According to many, yes. Though I don't even meet them."
An interesting thought struck my head.
"Mary, do I guess right that you, on the net..."
"Spot on!" she cut into my words, as she had done several times already, then grabbed my arm and led me toward her bedroom.

In the middle of the room was a huge white bed covered with a pink blanket, decorated with puffy throw pillows. On the wall, pink textile leather inserts enhanced the atmosphere, which was made truly complete by the white canopy surrounding the bed. Opposite the bed, the unsuspecting visitor could inspect a professional camera system, and if that hadn't blinded them completely, the two studio spotlights certainly achieved it.
"Holy shit, Mary, you work here?!" I was surprised, and a laugh indicating fascination mixed into my voice.
"Do you like it?" she asked back with a proud smile on her lips.
"Well, I don't even know what to say," I replied.
"Amanda, you haven't become a prude, have you?" she complained teasingly. "What is a person supposed to live on? I'm a star," she admitted.
"I'm sure of that," I replied honestly. If there was one thing the girl understood, it was sex.
"And do guys come here?" I inquired.
"What a boomer you are! It should be! They are in the machine. I don't need them here. I'm just having fun. I enjoy it. I love it all."

For minutes I just walked around the room with a confused smile. It's lucky he didn't see it, because he really could have thought I was a prude. Mary was still beautiful and I could believe she was casting a spell. There were at least thirty different shield versions of costumes hanging in the closets, from Harley Quinn to Snow White. The schoolgirl set had an almost ordinary effect.
"Mary, it's good to see you," I commended. "You look good, and you know what to do with yourself."

"One does what they are best at. Morals, I think, we stripped off quite a long time ago, and if one can live this well off it, it certainly doesn't bother me. Besides, I do it the way it's good for me."
"And really, no one comes here?"
"A few good friends," she replied. "But they are truly good friends. And they only give what's needed."

Mary's eyes sparkled with life, despite clearly staring into nothingness. The blow hit her temple. It wasn't her eyes that were damaged, but the optic nerve. They looked as if they were made of dark chocolate.
"And are you well?" she asked somewhat unexpectedly, though if I had thought about it at that moment, I could have known it was my turn.
"Yes," I replied shortly. "You see, I'm handling the case of my life."
"That's truly impressive."
"How do you mean?"
"I have no doubt that this is your biggest case. But that is exactly why I'm sure it didn't start today. Not even a few weeks ago, nor even a few months ago."
"To tell the truth, on the 13th."
"No. That's not how I meant it."
"Yes, I know," I replied. "But alone it wouldn't have gone. I owe a lot to Anton."
"Who is he?" she was curious.
"Anton is the opposite of Neon Nights in everything. It's ironic that I still met him there."
"You got together with a client?" she was surprised.
"Client? No way. At least not in that sense. I don't even know how he ended up there anymore. I didn't even sleep with him. He was simply there and fell in love with me."

It happened so long ago, and before so much else, that it became completely irrelevant how we met. Anton stared there as if he hadn't seen a naked woman before, he was like a little boy, though he was much older than me. It was his first time in a nightclub. "You started in the best place!" I told him. "Still, how did you wander in here, you innocent kid?" That's what I felt. I almost got embarrassed that I had no clothes on. Everyone saw only a naked body that turned them on, and he saw a girl who was afraid.
"Anton is a writer" I continued. "Anton Martin. In the last few years, he has had quite a lot of success. He writes crime stories. He has believed in me since he met me."
"I guess a dream of his came true, dating a prosecutor."
"He never expressed it that way, but I hope so."

I didn't know what to say more. Mary's presence reassured me unusually, and this filled me with a truly good feeling. Meeting an old acquaintance, strangely, didn't evoke the worst moments of our years together, but rather a close bond I had to trust.

chapters.title: Chapter Eleven
paragraphs: February 12, 2022
Days came and went. Jessica withdrew the complaint, and so they could stay in the countryside with Jeff's parents; they didn't have to move to the end of the world.
Matt and Wilbur tried to find Jake Cooper, but as if he had indeed gotten wind of us being there, the earth swallowed him up. It's very possible that they tipped him off.

Before the prosecutor's office could officially launch the investigation, I had to clear up several things. Such was, for instance, the Neon Nights' utility bill debt, and untangling various covered-up police reports. This latter was not progressing at all. The policeman whose name appeared on the report about Katherine's death had also vanished, and I felt this was all too predictable. Obviously, it was no accident that it was so difficult to track down even one person who had to do with Neon Nights, but not finding anyone even after weeks filled me with disappointment. I wanted to believe that despite the Neon Nights' well-organized network and its presidential integration, even they could make mistakes, and my only job was to sniff this out and turn it to my advantage.
This was where we stood on February 12, 2022, two days before Valentine's Day.
(Anton Martin)
Amanda answered the phone.

Suddenly she turned completely pale, and then all I saw was her staring blankly ahead with glassy eyes. It was as if she didn't hear what was being spoken at all. The phone slipped from her hand and clattered loudly on the floor. I jumped up from the armchair. I asked what happened, but she didn't answer. A voice in the phone was saying hello. I held it to my ear.
"Anton Martin" I introduced myself.

The policeman kept saying that it was an accident and that Jefferson survived and Jessica didn't. As if he wanted to make a report. But he really had no such obligation to me. I pressed the call. Amy was still standing in one place, a battle of worlds could be raging behind her expressionless face. I rolled my eyes at the ground, as if I had done something wrong, but I was only overcome by deep shock. I couldn't speak either. At that moment, I wasn't even sure if we would ever meet again.

Amy resorted to the device of silence several times, when all she could have done otherwise was shout. Sometimes a day passed before he spoke to me again, but it was always for a reason that depended on me. I couldn't do anything about Jessica's death. When I recovered a little, I pulled one of the armchairs over to Amy. I sat her down, spread a blanket over her, and went out to the kitchen to make tea. When I returned, Amy was in the same position as I had left her.
I couldn't speak to her. There was nothing that seemed to make sense; I only knew that I had to stand by her, no matter how long it would take to get past this whole thing.

I also knew Jessica well. In Amy's previous life, Jessica and I developed a close friendship, the focal point of which was Amanda. As Amy and I began our free, new life, our relationship with Jesse ended as suddenly as it had begun. We needed the other one because of Amanda. When we got him back, we didn't need to talk to each other anymore. I didn't even know it was missing until today.

Amy sipped her tea. I tried to figure out if he liked it, but it didn't matter. I took the fact that he didn't put it out of his hands as a good sign. Suddenly, a million thoughts flooded my mind, but none of them made any sense. It was like I was fighting the dream. Another one looked really promising and I thought I might be able to do something with them, but it soon proved to be nothing more than a stray glimmer of some past that still made sense to believe in the truth.
Jessica was dead.

After withdrawing the complaint, Amy apparently calmed down, maybe she didn't even think about the threats anymore. However, I was less optimistic. I thought that whoever wrote the threats must have known that the investigation was not going to stop. I felt the messages weren't really about the report and Jessica was still in danger. I didn't tell Amy about that, though. I didn't want to interfere. I was glad that he was finally paying attention to his work. They knew what they were up against better than I did.

Now, however, she was starting Pretty Little Liars for the second time without having gone in to work. It was painful to see how much she was suffering. For a week I had been racking my brain on how I could help her, until I had the idea to surprise her with something that could shake her out of this state.
I couldn't hide the huge fluffy cat I got for her behind my back.
"Amy!" I told him in a voice full of enthusiasm. "I brought you someone."
She didn't seem particularly interested in the matter.
"It's a cat, Amy!"
"You're crazy!" she replied, without taking her eyes off the TV.

I named the new arrival Mr. Fuzzy. No matter how much I wanted to involve Amanda in choosing a name, she listlessly rejected all my attempts, and at first glance it might have seemed that the thing had backfired instead of going as planned. Days later, however, an barely visible smile appeared on her face, contorted with pain, with which she was undoubtedly mocking me; yet I chalked it up as the success of my struggle with the cat.

One morning I received a message from the attorney general, Athena Wright. She asked me to go in to see her. I didn't understand why, so I preferred to keep it a secret from Amanda lest I upset her. If something had come to light, they would surely call her.

Amy still barely spoke. We haven't talked in over a week. Every day was the same. Around six, he was overwhelmed by the dream of being up at ten. He turned on the series, pulled the covers over himself and watched in silence. He went to bed at midnight but could not sleep. In the first days, I was still trying to figure out what he would eat, then I realized that I couldn't even try something more pointless than this. Whatever I put in front of it, it only took bites, so I used the well-proven recipe. I made broth every other day and hoped that he would recover from it soon.
Jess's funeral was drawing closer...

The Attorney General was aware that Jessica and Amanda used to be friends, and it wasn't for nothing that Amy was given this case at the age of twenty-five. She looked to Athena Wright, known as one of the most significant figures on violence against women, as her true mentor.
"I don't usually talk to a stranger about cases that are ongoing," she began, "but now I must make an exception with you."
"I don't even know if I have the right to ask," I tried to reassure her of my minimal expectations. "Jessica was not my relative."
I soon understood that the Attorney General wanted to make a concession not only because of me or Amanda. He needed Amy to be ready for deployment and hoped I could help.
"I won't beat around the bush, Anton! Jessica's death was not an accident."
I don't even know what I felt.
"No?" I asked back uncertainty.
"She was killed."
"How do you know?" I inquired.
"According to Jefferson's statement, they were attacked on the road."
"Who?"
"There are only assumptions," she replied. "But one cannot help but think of the Neon Nights."
In this, she was indeed right. Regarding Jessica, only they could come into question.
"And why are you telling me?"
"Evans needs to know about it."
"I think so too," I puzzled. "Which is why I don't understand why you didn't call her?"
"Because you have to notify her."
"That's a truly terrible idea," I defended myself. "You have no idea what you are asking of me."
"You saw what happened when we notified her of her friend's death."

"But that was the natural reaction. She is grieving. Everyone grieves differently," I replied. "Amanda silently. If, however, I stand before her with the news that Jessica was killed, she will ask countless questions about when I wanted to tell her, to which I could only reply that precisely when I felt she was ready to know the truth, but that moment would be much later than when she would actually be ready to know the truth. Besides, she would rightly ask why I knew about this first?!"
"You are overthinking it," Wright reassured.
"I think you are overthinking it, with all due respect," I answered with indignation in my voice, while Wright cast a deprecating glance at me.
"I won't get into this argument," she insisted. "You know what condition your fiancée is in. Believe me, whatever bad time you choose, it will still be better than if she heard it from us."
"Then I guess all opposition on my part is in vain," I concluded with obvious disappointment.
