"Yes. I'm sorry, Anton, but this will be for the best," she confirmed with unbroken optimism.
"And how long do I have to find an opportunity?" I inquired, hoping for a later date.
"As soon as possible," she replied shortly. "The investigation needs Amanda. And with Jessica's death, the trail is even hotter. Every day counts!"
"What could be more natural," I replied, though my words were dripping with pure irony.

I understood that they needed Amy, but I felt that forcing it would get us nowhere. The Attorney General entrusted me with a truly thankless task. I was afraid, there is no good solution. Even though every day counted, I didn't know how Amanda would handle the news that Jessica had been killed despite her best efforts. Although he did his best, I was sure he would blame himself for what had happened, and I was afraid it would lead to another breakdown. If ever, I would have given everything to be wrong now.

chapters.title: Chapter Twelve
paragraphs: February 22, 2022

I had no idea how Amy could be so dependent on something. Even if I knew, he has every reason to be oblivious to the world around him. Another season of the series has passed since yesterday, and no matter how I calculated it, it all seemed impossible. Almost only his eyes were visible from under the blanket. My head would have exploded from watching so much TV, but he still took it. He didn't even seem to have to push himself.
I sat down next to her on the sofa. I put my hand on her back and hugged her as much as I could. She raised her eyes and paused the series.
"Where were you?" she asked.
"At the prosecutor's office."
"For what?" he said this in an accent that was almost close to his usual one. He emphasized the i in the word.

"They asked me to tell you," I faltered slightly, but rather gathered my strength because I wanted to tell the truth, "that Jessica was killed. The accident was no accident. Even though she withdrew the complaint."
I thought Amy would be silent again, but as if she heard me up there that I was going to be wrong, she suddenly covered up.
"Rrgh!" he growled.
I wouldn't say that Amy was quick to squirm out of the couch's swampy attraction, but she definitely overcame all doubt solely by the force acting on her.
"Of course they killed her!" she concluded. "If they easily kill anyone in a miserable series, why wouldn't they do it? We have to go to Jefferson."
"Now?" I puzzled. I was surprised by this sudden change.
"I've lain enough, and they've ticked me off. So I'm changing and we're going."
I almost regretted that it hadn't come to light sooner.
"But it's a three-hour drive to get there!" I demurred.
"I didn't know you had other work besides sitting at home," she roasted me. I didn't take it personally; she was right. I had plenty of time.
"We'll take the Ferrari," I stated.
"I don't care right now," she acknowledged, then disappeared into the bathroom.

I have a Ferrari 308. It was a long-standing desire of mine. Fire-red with a cream leather interior. I loved classic cars, and when I had the chance, I bought one. We didn't use it very often because comfort usually took a back seat to the elegance of the eighties, but now we felt like characters in an old series in which they chased the bad guys in exactly such a car.

The three-hour journey was not exactly relaxing, but that was not the goal either. Amy wrapped herself in sunglasses. I didn't understand why, because it didn't show at all that she hadn't gotten out of bed for a week and a half, but she must have seen something about herself that she had to hide.

Jeff's parents lived in a large house. It had three levels. Two families could comfortably fit in it without even having to meet. I could understand that Jess's mother-in-law was reluctant to live in it alone for weeks. And the fact that the young couple stayed even after the treatments could be attributed to the threatening messages.

Amy stepped out of the car firmly, wrapping her head in a white silk scarf. She looked like a sixties actress. He rang the bell. Jeff opened the door after a few minutes, when Amanda did not let go of the button.
"Jefferson!" Amy said demandingly.
"Prosecutor Evans?" Jefferson asked back. It was the first time they met. "You came here from Love City without checking in?"
"I don't think it's your business to tell me where to go," she threw back.

Amy used to be less blunt, especially not when she was talking to strangers. In time, he managed to shed his half-heartedness and said several times what he was thinking. He didn't know Jefferson personally, but Jessica made him feel like he didn't have to pretend. It seemed to me that he had reservations about Jefferson.
"Are you letting me in at last?"
"I have nothing to say," replied Jeff, who must have had similar feelings for Amy.
"Take it as an official visit," Amanda switched strategies. "They said you were attacked."
"And you're still surprised I don't want to talk to you about it?" he complained. "This whole thing shouldn't have been started."
He drew a pretty severe conclusion from the existing facts, but I couldn't blame him. If the same had happened to Amy, I would have run off the first cliff I saw.
"I understand you are angry and disappointed, but believe me, Jessica was aware of the dangers too."
"Come to the yard!" Jefferson steered us to a secluded spot.
In the area behind the house stood a heated glass pavilion; we went in there. The taste of country luxury hit me.
"Do you want something to drink?" he asked.
"Nothing. Talk instead" Amy replied urgently.
"What are you curious about?"
"Everything! What happened?!"
"I already told the police. Haven't you spoken with them, Miss Evans?"
"I want to hear how it was with my own ears. Why are you stalling? Spill it!"
"I'll tell you, but don't harass me with this anymore! I'm trying with all my strength to forget it."
"Just tell me, and I'll leave you alone."
Deep pain sat on Jefferson's face as he thought back to that day again.

"We were heading to a nearby town. Riverdale. The road is forested and hilly in that direction, lots of switchbacks. It was raining. It must have been five in the afternoon. After a while, I noticed a car was following us."
"What kind of car was it?" Amy asked.
"A black Ford Mustang. New. I don't know the license plate."
"Go on!"

"I told Jessica that I thought we were being followed. She said I was seeing ghosts. But a few turns later, when we were approaching a tunnel, they accelerated next to us and hit us from the side. I tried to hold against it, but the rear of the car broke out and we crashed into the tunnel entrance."
"Just with Jessica's side?" Amy scoffed.
"Yes!" Jefferson shouted. He slammed his fist on the table, tears running down his face. "If I were back there, I'd do everything to crash into the wall on my side. I loved her more than anything!"
His feelings seemed genuine. He was visibly shaken. I couldn't even imagine how hard it must have been for him to survive the last week and a half too.
"Didn't you see the attackers?" I chimed in.
"No. The windows were tinted," Jeff replied.
"I'm sorry," I cut it short.
"Didn't you see anything else?" Amy asked back.
"Nothing. I can't really recall how it happened; I fainted, and only came to later."
"And where is that tunnel?"
"North to Riverdale. There's only one way to get there" Jefferson said.
"We'll check it," Amanda replied resolutely. "I want to see that place."
"You're behaving strangely, Amanda," Jefferson noted. "Why did you come here from Love City without warning?"
"The star witness of my case was killed; obviously I'm interested in what happened."
"Then why didn't you come earlier?"
Amy answered the question with a devastating look.
We left Jefferson the way we came, whirling through Amy's thoughts.
"Why didn't he want to talk to me? In the reverse situation, I wouldn't leave the station until they found your killer," she said.
She was right. Jeff indeed behaved illogically, but who can be called to account for their behavior in such a situation?

The direction, although quite rough, described exactly where the tunnel was located in this case. We had to drive on a winding forest road for almost ten minutes, then several short straights followed, until after a long sweeping left turn, we saw the tunnel. Traffic was extremely sparse. While we were there, maybe two cars passed us.

On the right side of the tunnel entrance, traces of a huge crash were discoverable. The concrete was broken in several places, despite an iron edge being fixed to the corner. It was terrible to see. Jessica didn't have much chance of surviving the impact if this was indeed the wall that caught them.
A few brake marks were still visible on the road, though only two, from the two rear wheels, but that matched what Jefferson said.
"what do you see" I asked Amy.
"I don't know," she replied. "I'm not an expert. I just wanted to see the place. Don't you see anything strange?"
"No," I replied immediately. "Everything is as Jefferson described it."
"Something is still not right here."
"Where do you get that from?"
"I feel it," she replied. I couldn't compete with Amanda's premonitions. They were often correct.
"Then what should we do?"
"Let's go home. Can you drive?" she inquired.
"You know I can," I replied.

The journey home was unanimous. I planned that the adventure we could spend in the Ferrari would be enjoyable, but it didn't have the right atmosphere. Amy couldn't do that. The investigation saddened us both.
"Jess's car is in LC," Amanda announced.
"How come?"
"They brought it up to headquarters. Now I understand why."
"Do you want to see it?" I inquired.
"No, but you must."
"Shall we go now?"
"No way. Tomorrow," she replied. "I'd like to ask you to go and see it."
"Am I allowed to do that?" I was surprised.

"If you have papers for it, yes. Matt or Wilbur will show you. Please take photos! Everything that could be interesting. Even if there isn't anything like that, take photos. We have to find something."
"Are you that sure the wreck of the car will help with something?"
"Either that, or nothing."

A three-hour drive takes a toll on a person, especially if it's six, and in the meantime a three-liter engine is roaring behind their head. I loved driving this car, but I wished we had gone with the other one instead.

Amy's blanket was still lying on the couch, so as soon as we got home, I plopped down there and pulled her over me. In the past week, I might have thought that Amanda's precise schedule of showers and brushing her teeth had been thrown off course, but I would have been wrong. Amy yanked mercilessly out of the couch's soft embrace and pushed me into the shower.
"I'm back, Anton!" she said. "Forget about letting yourself go."

She kissed me on the mouth and took off all my clothes. She didn't even care that I got wet before she undressed me. I didn't know she missed me so much. In the past week and a half, she had cocooned completely in her own world, and I felt there was no place for me there. That might have been true, but now that she was back, to use her words, it was obvious she wanted to get back something from the past days that she had to give up.

He pressed foam on his bath rose and rubbed it from head to toe. This amused me greatly. Her skin was as smooth and shiny as if she had bathed in fresh snow. Not wanting to resist the temptation, I went into the game he wanted to play. We hugged and showered. But instead of toweling off, Amy sat on the bottom of the bathroom cabinet and spread her legs. I stood in front of him and gently penetrated him. A wonderful feeling washed over me. It felt like we hadn't made love in forever and it showed on both of us. Our lips were sealed forever, we didn't want to let go of each other, lest we lose each other. I laid Amy on her back on the dresser and leaned over her. Grabbing her waist, I pushed myself deeper and deeper into her, and it filled me with pleasure as her soft moans between her teeth echoed in my mouth. Amy was a beautiful woman. Her golden blonde hair covered almost her entire upper body. Her shapely breasts rose from under her like two hills, and my gaze was directed to her muscular stomach between her legs, where the sight of her labia continued to burn. I couldn't get enough of them hugging me. Her thighs were high against my chest, I almost lost myself in the sight as she beat my bottom rhythmically against my crotch. We locked hands and I pulled her closer to me. He wrapped his arms around my neck and hung on me like an intoxicated addict. I was completely in control. I wanted to enjoy every moment of this long-awaited meeting. Amy was getting louder and louder and I couldn't keep quiet either. I didn't even want to. I knew he would be turned on too when he heard how much I was enjoying it. I put it back on the cupboard, and he pushed me away with a decisive movement and we switched places. He kissed my whole body starting from my lips. Circling with his tongue, he slid my hard swelling manhood deeper and deeper into his mouth. He grabbed it with his hand and began to suck it with pleasure, making soft movements. Many spheres of beauty came over me, I watched her devotional performance with admiration. Knowing from my voice, he felt that I was close to consummation, abandoning what he had started, he ran into my lap and, making the last movements with his hips, took in my pleasure. We sat hugging each other for minutes. All the happiness in the world swirled inside me.

chapters.title: Chapter Thirteen
paragraphs: February 23, 2022
I complied with Amy's request and the next day I went to the specialist workshop where Jessica's car was examined. Both detectives accompanied me.

It was a 2010 BMW 3 Series. It was a good car, but the right side was so mangled that it was barely recognizable from that view. At the request of the investigators, the specialist conducting the investigation repeated to me what he had already told them earlier. The side airbag did not deploy in the crash, although that was not the cause of Jessica's death. The wall of the entrance pushed the right door all the way to the center console. Jess had no chance of surviving. Amy could have seen the body, but she didn't want to hear about it. Based on the condition of the wreck, he made a good decision. Whatever he imagined instead, it must have been less terrifying than reality.
"Can I take pictures?" I asked. "The prosecutor would need them."
"We will hand over our pictures," the specialist said. "But if she needs them immediately, take photos."
I accepted both options.
"How did the accident happen? Does what Jefferson claims hold water?" I inquired.

"The examination is still ongoing, but they indeed might have been pushed. Do you see that dent on the left door? The marks of the black car are clearly visible. The analysis of the paint supports that they collided with a black Mustang before the impact."
"So everything was as described" I established. I felt disappointment. I wanted Amy's hunch to be spot on and something that turned out to be really unusual.
I looked into the passenger compartment. I didn't know what I was looking for, relying only on my instincts, which I had developed on novels, not on active field duty.
"Could the handbrake have moved because they hit the wall?"
"No, that was certainly used," the expert replied.
"And isn't that strange?" I asked.
"Not really. The driver was fighting another car. Obviously, he tried everything he thought could help."
"So you've seen this in other cars too?"

"In half of the cases in an accident, the driver pulls the handbrake. This is an unexpected situation. No one is prepared for such a thing. People react to it as best they can. Using the handbrake in such a case is obviously foolish. Everything worth thinking over will be in the report."

I thanked the expert for his answer. The specialist's last sentence put a bug in my head. Was Jefferson a practiced driver? Good question. What counts as one? I considered myself a practiced driver, yet I didn't know how I would react in a similar situation.
Matt and Wilbur knew about the black Mustang, but they hadn't found it yet. There were no cameras on that route, and too many roads branched off before a surveillance camera could record it.
"How many cars are there that could come into question?" I asked.
"In that region, none," Matt replied.
"Or at least no one has such a car in those parts," Wilbur continued. "There is no doubt that one was there, though."
"I feel like there is more and more to look into," I grumbled.
"Investigation is like that. Here we always meet only loose ends until we find the central element that unlocks the other mysteries like a key."
"I can't wait until we have it," I replied. "You used a good metaphor."
"Wilbur is a secret poet," Matt teased.
"Bah," Wilbur replied extremely expressively.

The half-applied handbrake was on my mind all the way home. Amy also found this development interesting, but in the end she believed the expert, who said that in half of the cases, everyone pulls it in in such a situation.
Amy was looking at the pictures. Morphed. I felt he wanted to find fault with Jefferson's story at all costs.
"I don't mind at all that the casket will be closed," she stated unexpectedly. "Jess must be in terrible shape."
"Do you know what you are wearing yet?" I inquired. "Don't you want a new dress?"
"I ordered one online one night. It will arrive by then," she replied. She was charmingly far-sighted even in this state.
"How are you?" I asked, although I saw her every day.
Amy sensed that my question was truly sincere, so she didn't hit her with some half-answer.

"I'm not well, but I have to pull myself together," she replied. "The Neon Nights killed her, I just don't know how yet." She cast her soul-piercing sea-blue gaze upon me. "Currently, I'm shifting between blaming myself and blaming the Neon Nights."
"You are not to blame for Jessica's death," I said firmly.
"I know," she replied. "But I feel otherwise, and unfortunately, that's what counts."
It hurt very much to see her like this. I wanted her to receive absolution from Jess's death, and hoped that however things shape up, this one thing at least would come to pass.

chapters.title: Chapter Fourteen
paragraphs: February 26, 2022
(Amanda Evans)

The funeral was in Love City. I could count on both hands how many friends or acquaintances of Jessica's attended. Of course, her death wasn't widely publicized. Practically hardly anyone knew about it. Jessica didn't work for years after the Neon Nights. From the money blackmailed out of Mortenstein, she probably could have lived her whole life, but after a while, she had to find something to occupy herself. She learned photography and started working as an event photographer. After the Neon Nights, she became a reclusive personality. She made few friendships. She hardly kept in touch with her former acquaintances. David King or Hermione were not on her list because she hung out with them monthly. She simply kept track of what they were doing. Others, however, vanished after their release.

The attorney general decided not to notify Hermione and David King of Jessica's death. It would have been completely unnecessary to risk the outcome of the investigation. Regardless, the funeral could easily have become a meeting if they had published Jessica Marsh's obituary in the papers. After the murder of the star witness, however, protecting the other witnesses became even more important.

Jessica and Jefferson met at a wedding where Jess worked as a photographer. They liked each other immediately. Jessica always went for curly brown-haired guys. Those were her cases. They say that cases represent some sort of fixation we couldn't move past. For Jessica, perhaps it was Jake Cooper, because when she saw him, she immediately fell in love with his dark brown eyes. Jess's excitement largely dulled a more precise understanding of the dangers of Neon Nights, and although she later felt guilt toward me because of this, I knew that even without her longing, I would have said yes to the adventure.

With Jefferson, perhaps she finally wanted to live out this relationship. At the end of the wedding, they already left as a couple, and I wasn't surprised at all by how quickly events moved. Jessica, who had bewitched even the president—how could she not have won over Jeff, the only little boy of a country psychiatrist?
This boy now, however, behaved like a beaten dog.

In our meeting a few days ago, I might have overshot the mark. I had a hard time processing Jessica's death, but I was sure Jeff experienced it the same way. Now, a few days later, I felt ready to open a new chapter, but I must have left too deep a mark in Jefferson; he didn't even want to see me. Blaming each other mutually didn't help either of us, and I had to admit we both made mistakes.
After the ceremony, I wanted to express my sympathy to him, but even though we were the most important to Jess, it seems we had a much harder time getting along with each other without her.
"What do you expect from him?" Anton asked, as soon as he noticed that I kept my eye on Jeff.
"We just need to reconcile somehow," I replied.
"Some things shouldn't be forced. It will happen if it's meant to happen."
"I didn't know you were a philosopher," I replied mockingly. In similar situations, this was my game. I tried to distance myself from the truth.
Among the lingering people, suddenly Jeff's mother came up to me.
"You're Amanda, aren't you?" she asked.
"Yes," I replied.
"I am Mrs. Margaret Morris. Jefferson's foster mother. I understand you are investigating Jessica's death."
"We could say that," I let it go. I didn't want to get into details.
"She was a wonderful girl. I couldn't have wished for better for my son."
Mrs. Morriset's introduction caught my ear right away, but I wanted to run the obligatory laps.
"I wanted to speak with Jefferson, but he avoids me. Please tell him that I'm very sorry about the last meeting. I didn't take his feelings into account."
"It's hard for him now. He blames himself for Jessica's death."
Her last sentence surprised me.
"He is just as much a victim as Jessica," I replied. "I hope he manages to get over it as soon as possible."
"And on the very day when that big bang happened" continued Mrs. Morris.
"What crash are you thinking of?" I inquired.

"On the day the children had the accident, in the morning we woke up to a huge crash. I don't know where it happened, but close to the house. Someone was rampaging around the neighborhood with a loud car. For minutes we listened as he speeded, roared the engine like a madman, then crashed. He hit another car but was able to drive on. When the trouble happened, he scurried off."
"There are many idiots on the roads," I answered. "Were you very frightened?"
"I was frightened, yes. I couldn't even fall back asleep. After that, the alarm went off for about fifteen minutes. Jeff had to go out to turn it off. But by then the sleep was gone from my eyes."
What she told didn't seem like a particularly striking incident, but I still found it strange that such a thing happened on the very day Jessica died.
"Was Jefferson adopted?" I asked without beating around the bush.
"Yes, he was sixteen when we took him in."
"Sixteen?" I was surprised.
"Married," she would have started another story, but Jefferson noticed us and snapped at Mrs. Morriset irritably. The woman immediately fell silent.
"Sorry Amanda" freed himself. Jeff will make up. It's too much trauma for him, forgive him, please...
After the incident, I saw fit to leave. On the way home, I called Wilbur.
"Check out footage from Montaukville's public surveillance cameras on the day of the accident from five to seven in the morning!" I instructed.
"What are we looking for?"
"Any car that can be loud. In the morning a driver was rampaging in the village. He hit something. Probably the car was damaged too."
"If I find something, I'll let you know," he answered. "There are two things, however, that I wanted to speak with you about anyway."
It took only a few minutes to hang up the phone.
"No matter how much Jeff doesn't want to, I have to talk to him about that morning. If nothing else, I'll have him brought in for questioning," I threatened.
"You can ask him about the adoption too."
"It's quite strange. Especially at that age. For sure, he had a life before too. But will he tell me anything about it? Who knows."
"What did the detective want?" Anton inquired.
"The 'newsstand' got in touch, saying he'd gladly sell more articles to us, but I don't know where they lead."
"He must be pushing it for some reason."
"Because he wants to see money from them."
"There must have been some reason he collected them."
"Are you getting a commission after the articles?" I asked.
"I think rather my money will suffer."
"Are you seriously going to buy them? You're not normal," I grumbled.

"Just think about it. Why is there more than one of it? I'd understand one. The campaign doesn't come together. But why are there more? This makes no sense. Or did they organize a campaign in advance? With this many articles? Why, if they suddenly put the whole thing on hold?"
"I don't know," I replied. "It really makes no sense. But still I don't know what to do with this thread now."
"Then I'll find out for you."

I was sorry he was going to buy them all. That was how he worked. He never bargained about anything. He liked it when everything had a concrete price. That's how it is. It will cost us fifteen hundred dollars, but it's very possible that we get hold of some information that is worth that much.
Matt called me to the police station in the afternoon to speak with a man who knew the policeman who wrote the report.
"How did you find him?" I asked him.
"We investigated a little within the department."
"And who is it they called in?"
"He volunteered. He says he knows about how the Katherine case was covered up."
Sitting in the interrogation room was a patrolman wearing a uniform.
"Good day! What is your name?"
"Toby McRae."
"Are you currently active in service?" I inquired.
"Yes, I've been working at the 23rd precinct for eight years now."
"My colleague said that he volunteered because he had information about why the Katherine Mayers investigation was closed before it was resolved."
"Yes. But I'd start by saying I had no role in the investigation. I only noticed oddities at the precinct."
"You don't need to worry about that. We are not investigating the Katherine case. What oddities did you notice?"

"There was a detective in the first half of the tens, Ross Wilson he was called. Once, they were called to a club because of a death. I had to go out with Wilson. When I drove him there, he asked me to stay in the car. I found it strange, because if we are called somewhere, we never go alone. In any case, I didn't understand why they called Wilson, since he was a detective, not a patrolman. We usually arrive at a scene first. Then I noticed that after Wilson went in, after a short time two patrolmen left the place. I didn't know them; they belonged to another district."
"Do I understand correctly that in this case too, patrolmen arrived first, then Wilson took over the case?"
"Yes. He didn't stay inside very long, ten minutes at most."
"And why did you have to go if only Wilson went into the club?"
"Because it would have been strange if he went alone," Toby McRae replied.
"Do you know the name of the place, by any chance?"
"Neon Nights. That's how Wilson referred to it."
"Why do you remember these events so precisely?" I inquired.
"As I said, I then noticed several oddities at the precinct, and it burned into me. It was an unusual situation, and I felt Wilson wasn't walking a straight path."
"Did you report this to anyone?"
"This case specifically, no. I had no reason to."
"I don't only mean this case," I completed the question. "But anything from what you found strange."
The patrolman thought.

"Actually, I had nothing to report. Several times I felt Wilson was making phone calls that were suspicious, but I couldn't prove anything. I was sure he was in contact with that club, which was why he had to go in alone. I didn't know that place, but it was known that a girl died. Wilson didn't want to deny this either. He couldn't have, since two policemen had been there before. But that the investigation was closed after barely a week, he obviously had a hand in it."
"Did you look at the investigation file?"
"No," the patrolman replied immediately. "I had no authority to look into it. Obviously, if they had asked me to help in the case, I would have done so, but that didn't happen."
"Can you recall anything else that made Wilson suspicious?"
"Once I caught a phone conversation in which he seemed to want to have a fake death certificate issued for someone."
"Why could he need that?"
"That I don't know, I have no idea," he answered.

Toby McRae's account indeed seemed true, and I couldn't help but think that perhaps he had to hack the death certificate of the gentleman who had filed the complaint. The patrolman's story fit precisely with the facts I knew.
"Was this conversation before or after the mentioned case?"
"After," the patrolman answered clearly. "About a week and a half after that."
"You have an excellent memory," I noted. "You remember events from nearly ten years ago so precisely," I praised.
"I was still a rookie, recently assigned to the precinct. One is more attentive in that period."
"And what became of Ross Wilson?"
"In 2015, he left the force," he replied.
"In which year did this case occur?" I asked to make sure he remembered correctly.
"In 2014," he gave the correct year.
"Do you know, by any chance, where he went or why Wilson departed?"
"To know, I don't. But I'm sure the Neon Nights had something to do with him leaving."
"Why?" I inquired. I was interested in what he had come up with during his private investigation.

"He told everyone that he won the jackpot. Others immediately thought he won the lottery, but I thought he might have received a larger sum from the Neon Nights for some reason. Only that made sense. He said he was going to the Bahamas and finally getting married, though I knew he didn't even have anyone."
"Why do you think he received money from the Neon Nights?"
"There are more corrupt cops than lottery winners," he answered. I couldn't argue with his reasoning.
"Would you say this in court too, if it came to that?"
"When I became a cop, I took an oath to uphold and enforce the law. I feel it is my duty to testify in court if necessary."
"All right, thank you!"
"Nice work, Matt," I said to the detective upon stepping out of the room.

Toby McRae was still a young cop, but no longer a rookie. His mind worked fast; I found it a bit strange that after so many years he was still serving as a patrolman. Of course, not everyone has to climb the ladder, I thought. Where would we be without excellent patrolmen?

chapters.title: Chapter Fifteen
paragraphs: February 28, 2022
Athena Wright called me into her office for Monday. She wanted to know where the investigation stood. She judged that we must soon be ready to officially file the complaint.
"How are you, Amanda?" she asked with tact in her voice.
"I'm well, thank you," I replied. "I'm capable of working. And I want to."
"That's good to hear," she said with a friendly smile on her face. "Then it's time for us to level up. What do we know now? Do you have enough material for the indictment?"
"There are still gaps," I replied immediately. "But we already have a lot of material, we've started on many leads. We've got witnesses. The prosecution can already stand on its own two feet."
"Then we also know for sure that the president ran the Neon Nights?"
"Indirectly, this too can be proven. But they also write in a newspaper article that the Neon Nights was Edgar Mortenstein's idea."
"And is this article authentic?" Athena inquired.
"Yes, provably so. Furthermore, in addition to Jessica's testimony, two other witnesses can support that the Neon Nights was indeed built on the exploitation of the girls."
"And that the network was run by Mortenstein?"
"The article and Jessica," I replied objectively.
"And is there anything else besides these?" the attorney general searched.
"We can also use Jessica's death as evidence."
"Why? Have they found anything indicating that the Neon Nights network is responsible for Jessica Marsh's death?" she asked with complete calm.

I wasn't glad about this question, since we had no such evidence, and the attempt on Jessica's life would only have been usable in the original case if we had found something that tied the case to the Neon Nights beyond all doubt.
"No," I replied. "We cannot prove this for now."
"That's a great pity. And what do you think: will you be able to?"
"We are doing everything we can. But for now, we can't even find the car they forced Jessica's off the road with."
"Then there will still be work with that," the attorney general noted. "The threats and Jessica's death can of course easily be connected, but that would need to be proven."
"Yes," I agreed.

"Furthermore, the threats cannot be used as evidence either, until you can tie them to the Neon Nights beyond all doubt. You may win the court's sympathy, but that doesn't mean Mortenstein will be convicted for everything he committed."
"Yes, we are aware of that too," I replied, and began to feel more and more that there were too few things we could use.
The attorney general continued.
"You said Jessica had incriminating evidence against Mortenstein. What is it?"
"Jessica was with the president during the years of the Neon Nights. She spoke about this in her testimony too, which we recorded."
"That may make her claim credible, but the president will obviously deny it. Can we prove from elsewhere that Jessica was indeed with the president?"
"Yes," I replied. "Both other witnesses can support this claim, since they were allowed to leave the Neon Nights because Jessica convinced the president to dissolve the organization."
"And do the witnesses know how she did it?"
"No, they don't," I replied, and anxiety filled me again.
"According to this, only Jessica spoke about this."
"Officially, not even she did," I confessed. "She said she would later," I replied.
"Well, that's a great pity, because Jessica Marsh is dead."
The attorney general paused. I knew she trusted me, but she tried to do everything to corner me.
"Did we miss out on this evidence?" she asked.
"I know how it happened," I tried to save the situation.
"Yes, I think so," she replied with an edge in her voice. "The only problem is that it is not you who knows, but Alice Bucks. And she is also dead, if I'm not mistaken."

I tried to pull myself together. Only now did I truly realize why it's difficult to investigate when one is aware of eighty percent of the case. One takes for granted what is not. Of course, why wouldn't I have thought that we must also be able to prove that the collapse of the Neon Nights was indeed thanks to Jessica. But then Jessica was alive, and despite being threatened, I didn't believe for a minute that they could actually kill her. And now I stood there with a wealth of information in my head that I couldn't prove. Jessica, as the star witness, was an indispensable element of the proceedings against the president, but to me, Jessica always remained Jess. I surprised even myself by how much I couldn't distance myself from this. Now I truly understood what it means for a case when it loses the person around whom the whole thing was built. I felt the case against the president crumbling like a house of cards in my hands.

"Draft a preliminary indictment!" Athena Wright continued. "It will help think through every eventuality. Calculate how many years the president could get for the crimes he committed. Then put next to it how much you, Jessica, and the other innocent people who fell victim to the Neon Nights would be satisfied with. I'm sure you will find the evidence to make each of them satisfied."
"I understand, I'll write it," I replied, somewhat crushed.

chapters.title: Chapter Sixteen
paragraphs: March 1, 2022
Spring had arrived, but the trees had not yet begun to bud, nor had the birds emerged from their winter hiding places; only the rain poured inconsolably since morning.

It was a sleepy morning. I didn't go into the prosecutor's office, but rather, taking the attorney general's advice, I was doing my best to draft the outline of the indictment. Since I woke up, however, I only kept putting off the task. I had almost cleaned the kitchen too, by the time I finally sat down at my desk.

I was trying with all my might to compile the outline of the indictment. However, since I got up, I had only been putting off the task. I had slowly even cleaned the kitchen before I finally sat down at my table.
"How do you do this?" I asked Anton.
"What?"
"Writing."
"Do you mean a novel or work?"
"Do I look like someone who has changed jobs?"
"I was afraid of that," Anton noted. "If I had to write for work, I'd be in trouble too."
"You're not really helping with that," I grumbled.
"Unfortunately, the two are still different."
"Whatever. What do you do when there's no inspiration?" I asked.
"I sit in front of the computer and look blankly," he answered. "Meanwhile, guilt gnaws at me."
"There we go. I have that already. So is that a good sign?"
Anton ran through a line on the keyboard, which had been preceded by about ten minutes of inactivity, and I saw that he had finally completed something with this.
"Let's do this: we talk things through while you take notes," he suggested.
"Yes, that's exactly the problem. I can't press the keys."
"Uh-oh," he shook his head. "Then we are indeed facing a serious problem."
"I know," I replied. "So it wasn't for nothing that I said I wasn't just imagining there is trouble!"
Anton got up from the armchair and came over to me at my desk.
"By the way, why aren't you writing in your study?" I asked.

"Writing," he corrected me. "Not scribbling. I used to scribble. And since you ask, I don't write there because if I sit here, I can see you, and if I'm inside, I can't see you. It's as simple as that."
Meanwhile, he began to massage my neck.
"Well, yes. And if I may ask, why must you see me?" I provoked him, while throwing my head back to kiss him.
"Because I still fall for your tricks, Amy!"
"What do you mean? In this freezing weather, I'm completely bundled up," I feigned, pulling my t-shirt up to my chest to show what was under it.
"Yes, that's a thong," he replied. "Somehow, the cleaning rag fell out of your hand quite a few times today, darling," he complained.
"I'm sorry for my clumsiness," I kept playing.

"No problem. That's why I'd rather sit here," he replied. "And of course, that's why in the last half hour I've only been able to write a single sentence in the book: 'What days followed!' It doesn't even fit the context."
"Well, I didn't know I was bothering you!" I broke free. "I didn't want to distract the great Anton Martin!"
Anton snatched the laptop out of my hand, slammed it shut, and yanked me up from the chair.
"You didn't know?" he asked. "Then it's time we discuss the rules!"
"All right, sweetheart, I can't wait to discuss them!" I mimicked in a breathless voice.

He grabbed me, threw me onto my desk—which, in line with my inspiration, was completely empty—and with one movement tore my panties off me, then slapped my bottom with his cupped palm. I was completely turned on.
"Yes, Mr. Artist, I won't clean in lingerie in front of you anymore!" I vowed.
"I see you already understand that there is no need for it!" he agreed.
In a blink, he penetrated me. I groaned as if he were doing it for the first time.
"I didn't know today's writers were so hard!" I disbelieved. "We learned it differently in school."
"As if you went!" he snapped. His remark sounded excitingly sharp. I indeed skipped a few grades back then.
He grabbed both my legs with his hands, and holding them tightly, dictated the pace. I couldn't do anything, only slide back and forth on the desk surface with my face turned to the side.
"That's why it's so big" I clarified.
"What?" he asked.
"The desk. Just continue!" I encouraged.

I sometimes closed and sometimes opened my eyes. The room danced interestingly around me, but I wasn't sure if it was dancing or rather I was spinning in it. Anton went deep with every thrust. A sweet numbness flooded my brain; I felt that besides him, there was nothing else inside me. Heat flooded my body; the intoxicating tingle of orgasm shot through me.
"Do it more!" I begged.

Not that he had any intention of stopping. He made me his own with such force that the polished wood tore my skin. I didn't care. One more mark or less really didn't matter. Helplessly I surrendered to Anton's workout. Shortly, he turned me onto my back. I tried to reach his neck to pull him to me, succeeded for a short time, and kissed him.
"I adore you, my everything!" I encouraged him further. "I love when you do this to me!"
"I love doing this to you too!" he panted.

The position remained, only turned one hundred and eighty degrees around my axis. He bit into my breasts and, holding onto them, moved me further. Another exciting wave spread through my body. No matter how tightly he squeezed them, I felt no pain, only a vibrating tingle in my brain. I locked his waist with my legs, but only enough that his hips could still move freely. After a while, I felt my body writhing lifelessly on the desk, but I was completely carried away by this wave, which in the reverse situation he must have experienced when I rode him at a gallop.
"Has anything occurred to you yet of what you need to write?" he asked articulately.
"I don't know what you are talking about," I defended myself. "I've forgotten everything, I only know I want to be fulfilled with you!"
"Good," he concluded contentedly.

He raised my legs to his chest, kissed both my soles, then pulled himself out of me and released on my stomach. For a little while, making slight hip movements, he stood before me, then slowly leaned over me, and I squeezed him to me with my hugging thighs; resting his head on my breasts, he stretched out on me.
"So this is what you usually do when you lack inspiration?" I asked him five minutes later.
"You know exactly that this is what I do," he answered.
"This solution also flashed before me when I started cleaning."
"Next time you don't have to drag it out so long, baby. I can get you over the hump sooner."
We both dressed, I brewed him a coffee, and I sipped hot tea.
"I'll take notes," Anton said, "and you talk."
"Okay." I agreed, since I still had no desire for writing. "Let's divide things in two," I offered.
"All right, sweetheart, as you think."
"First, let's look at what we can already prove, then let's look at what we can't yet, but is definitely connected to the case. And then..."
"Yes, there is always a third option," Anton noted.
"Okay, so let it be three, as I said before, right..."
"And what should that be?" he asked.
"Those cases that arose during the investigation, but we don't know yet if they belong to the big picture."
"Excellent insight," he agreed again. "Then let's take it from the beginning."
"Well, I'll say what we should do, you're here to write," I teased him. "There is David King."
"Will he be in it in the end?" he asked, surprised.

"He won't perform, what do you mean by will he be in it? Now we're only going through who can be used for what," I explained. "You said Jess will convince him. If it didn't work live, then now..." I fell silent. I faltered on whether I could joke with Jessica's death. "Thinking it over, they ran us, Anton!" I got caught up.
"Yes, Amy, I thought you knew that was what they did to you."
"Yes-yes" I approved. "but I didn't see myself as a whore, but as a stripper who provides extra services."
"Obviously, this is also something a person doesn't want to face," Anton explained. "Surely you tried to keep this thought away from yourself."
"Did you just call me a whore?" I snapped teasingly, going slightly beyond the level of banter.
"No!" Anton shot back.
"Don't you dare!" I agreed. "I wasn't a whore!" I didn't want to fight with him, and besides, I was the one who provoked. Of course, he still shouldn't have agreed.

When we talked among ourselves, the girls and I often used this term for ourselves. It seemed simple. Like when gay people refer to themselves as faggots. At the same time, we were aware that we held ourselves higher than that. Thus, when I began to think about what we actually were, it seemed very important to emphasize the dance, since this was what distinguished us from other sex workers. We were performing artists. We couldn't help it that, exploiting our skills, they chased us day by day into satisfying sexual fantasies. This was true for everyone who was in similar shoes.
"He can support that the leader of Neon Nights was Edgar Mortenstein," I continued.
"Written down."
"Then he too knows how the organization was set up. That they took us out once a week, which isn't a crime itself, only of course they didn't let us leave either. But we wrote that earlier."
"And what else does he know?" Anton asked, as if King were some action figure.

"He knows, for instance, where those ended up who dropped out of the club. Credible, since he is specifically grateful to Neon Nights for the drag shows, for example; he highlighted that as a positive. This could be important too. I wouldn't speak a good word of them, that's for sure. Oh, and of course he can prove that they have Jess to thank for the organization being dissolved."
"So that's enough for David King?"
"Yes," I replied. "Let's look at Hermione."
"How she went blind," Anton began impatiently.
"Well, what did I say?" I questioned him. "I say the things, you only confuse."
"True, sorry," he excused himself; he had long known I didn't expect explanations.
"The story of his going blind is definitely quite striking. It's a shame that no gentleman came forward" I was sorry. "Someone should prove the events from the other side as well."
"I'll write this down too, lest we forget."
"That we look for a gentleman? I know this in my sleep, but no matter," I replied. "Hermione actually knows everything I would know, so there is nothing new in this."
"Then I'll write next to it: the narrator."
"Why would you do that?" I was puzzled. "Does this seem like a literary text to you?"
"No, I only thought so it would be clear," he explained.
"No need," I recommended to him. "It's enough if you write: like every girl..."
"Written."

"And there is the article. That is also clear. Edgar Mortenstein, as the father of Neon Nights, the great patron of culture," I retched. "Oh, and of course Jess," it occurred to me. "I almost forgot."
"Well, yes. On Jessica's part, there is the whole prosecution case," Anton summarized.

"It's there, but what is in it?" I asked myself. "This can be used as the backbone of the indictment. Kidnapping, prostitution, violence, rowdiness, murder, what else? That's it?" I was surprised. "Is this why we want to convict this unfortunate man?"
"Committed against minors in a criminal organization," Anton completed my list.

"Well, yes, these are not negligible things either," I agreed. "You're clever. Let's start the second category," I offered. "This includes proving exactly what Jessica blackmailed the president with to close the Neon Nights. I'm thrilled Jess had that stupid idea that she would talk about all this later. I was an idiot to let her."

"It doesn't matter now," Anton replied. "Either you'll be lucky and manage to prove it somehow, or you have to accept that this remains incomplete. The charge must be written in such a way that this cannot weaken it."
"Amen," I agreed with his suggestion. "Let's not leave out the threat either, which is your obsession," I mocked. "But I don't know how we could prove from two notes that the Neon Nights wrote it."
"Let's not leave out the possibility of a miracle!" Anton called to my attention.
"Philosopher!" I poked him.
"A teacher of mine said it, but the compliment feels good," he noted.
"Then Jessica's death. This is perhaps the most important. We must connect this with the president in any case."
"Yes," he agreed. "This is the key."
"Don't forget to list the newspaper articles here, because we don't know what the others are about, or Ross Wilson, in connection with whom we can mention the Katherine Mayers case."
"It would be a big hit if it turned out that he indeed worked for the Neon Nights."

"It will bang, my dear, bang-bang" I imitated boxing with my hands, then thought for a few moments. "Jake Cooper and Mike Alioglu should be mentioned for the third time. For some reason, I have the feeling that this is also a key moment in the case, and if we could get through this door, the president would be suffocated by so much manure juice."
"I love when you think in metaphors," he flattered.
"I love having sex with you," I replied completely out of the blue.
"I don't know how this belongs here, but I think so too," Anton replied.
"And what still interests me very much," I began, "is the utility bill debt. Because let me not be Amanda Evans if some financial trickery isn't behind things."

Anton did a great job listing what was said. I tried to think everything through, and indeed it helped me see the tasks more clearly before me. It wasn't a small amount, but I had known all along that a lot of work still awaited us, and I felt I was full of energy to want to get to the end of everything as soon as possible.

I thought I would write a full indictment based on which the attorney general could see the details, and I'd add a few supplements about what we know and what we are still looking for answers to. Although this wouldn't have been needed for the complaint, I saw fit to give all information to Wright so we could go after the president more calmly. None of us had shot at such big game before, and whatever rodeos I had been in in my life, I was sure that even if I had ridden every bull so far, Mortenstein, unlike others, would be bent on throwing me off as soon as possible.
"Wilbur, tell me you found something on the cameras!" I answered the ringing phone.
I got excited by the big bear's answer.

"The Mustang left the village after six in the morning? And which side is visible? The break will be on the right," I said. "That there's no license plate, only that it's a Mustang? Show-offs. Try to track where it could have gone! Oh, you know this already! Never mind, dig it out even from under the earth!"
I put down the phone with a trembling hand.

"The black Mustang was there in Montaukville that morning, Anton!" I reported, though of course from my answers he had long pieced together what happened. "We have to call Jefferson in and find out what happened that day."

chapters.title: Chapter Seventeen
paragraphs: March 2, 2022

Jeff was still acting like he blamed me for the death of his love. I called him to the station, as he had just spent a few days in Love City for work. Even though I offered him to go to the apartment, he didn't want to hear about it.

According to his account, his mother woke them up after five in the morning because the alarm of one of the cars had already been going off for fifteen minutes, and asked him to check what happened. He didn't hear the crash; obviously, the car had long gone by the time he got down. No one besides him was down there, and he didn't know whose gold Renault SUV it was that had alarmed. Its bumper had shifted, but it didn't seem like a serious case, he said. The wailing stopped after five minutes, and then he went back into the house.

I found the matter slightly strange. I didn't understand how he didn't know the car they ran into if this was a village and it was parked near their house. He said he hadn't seen it earlier, and although he knocked at two places, they didn't know whose it was either. It wasn't important for him to find out. After he returned to the house, they went back to sleep with Jessica.

I accepted his account, but I wanted to share with him what we found out about the car that caused the crash. I hadn't come forward with it earlier because I wanted him to tell what happened without knowing the truth.
Upon hearing of the Mustang, he turned pale, almost frightened.
"Could they have been checking earlier if we were home?" he asked.
"I don't think they went there for that," I replied. "I'm sure they knew where to look for you. Where was your car parked?" I asked.

"I think in front of the house," he answered. "Usually I drive it into the yard, but my mother used it earlier, and she doesn't like to drive in through the gate. Maybe they withdrew the police surveillance too early," Jeff noted.
"Possibly," I let it go. "But I still don't understand why the Mustang was there in the morning, and why it behaved conspicuously. What sense did that make?" I asked more of myself than of him.
"If I knew, I'd tell you," Jefferson said.
"Was its only goal to cause alarm?"
"If it was, it succeeded. My mother was very frightened."

"Yes, her. A kind old woman. While you two slept through the whole thing. I doubt they wanted to harass her. If they wanted to make it known that you are in danger, and wanted to threaten you, they would have given clearer signs."
"I don't know," Jeff replied. "I think they only wanted to know if we were home. And when they saw the car, they left."
"But why did they run into the other car?"

"By accident," he replied. "These are aggressive people. Don't you think that when they completed the task, they wanted to celebrate a little, live out the animal that is in them? A bit of extra gas is enough for such a car and immediately there's trouble."
"Is it hard to drive such a Mustang?" I inquired; I saw he knew what he was talking about.
"It's over 400 horsepower."
"Whatever that means," I noted. "Do you think this tells me anything?"

"Yes, it's hard to drive!" he replied. "It's a strong car and rear-wheel drive. If traction control isn't turned on, it can spin suddenly before one can react. My mother said she heard them revving the engine. I think the driver was trying, but couldn't drive that well."
"Yet in the evening he was able to run you off the road," I chimed in.
"There were two of them. It's not sure the same one drove in the morning too," Jeff replied. I had to agree with him.
I didn't feel I was much wiser from the conversation. At any rate, I noted that Jefferson has some clue about cars. So I asked him.
"How much of this horsepower is in the BMW?"
"A girl asks like that, not a prosecutor," he noted. "420," he replied.
"420!" I was surprised. "So the little BMW is as strong as that Mustang?"
"I said a GT for the Mustang. There are even stronger ones, but yes. Mine is an M series, which is factory-wise the strongest version of the 3 series."
"And where did your mother go with it?" I pried. "To the shop?"
Jeff fell silent for a moment.
"I don't know what you are getting at, Miss Evans. Ask her if she usually uses it," he encouraged, indicating he had nothing to hide.
"Maybe that too will come to pass," I replied. "I don't dare drive my partner's Ferrari. Though that is a Ferrari."
"Yes, I saw it. Nice car!" he noted. "Is there a two- or three-liter engine in it?"
"Red," I cut short my humiliation, then something else occurred to me. "Why did you pull the handbrake on the car?"
"When?" he asked back.
"In the workshop, the car's handbrake was pulled. The specialist said this happens with inexperienced drivers. But you can drive, can't you?"
Jefferson's face remained expressionless for a time, then I saw an uncomfortable smile appear on it.
"You embarrass me, prosecutor. I have no idea why I pulled it. You cannot expect me to explain why I did something in a situation where panic took hold of me."
"Yet I hoped you could tell me. It would have been good if you said: because it has to be done this way!"
"I won't say that, because at first hearing it seems pretty foolish. I don't know when I pulled it. I could have pulled it after the accident too."
"Do you usually when parking?"
"Never," he shot back immediately.
"And while unconscious?" Jefferson didn't even deign to answer. "Then there is no sense to what you say," I summarized.

"It could have been pulled during the accident too," I saw the image of the car running through his mind, in the right front seat of which Jessica is already dead. "Would you like anything else?" he called me to account. I felt he was beginning to have enough of me and my questions.
"Your parents are foster parents, right?"
"Does this have any connection to Jess's death or the Mustang?"
"None," I replied shortly.
"Then I've told you everything."

I saw on Jeff that he didn't really want to open up to me. I didn't blame him. Our relationship didn't start well; besides, I felt he would blame me for Jessica's death his whole life, as if I had driven the car he crashed into the tunnel. I had no need of him; one person linked us, but she was already dead, and I thought it would be quite enough if I bumped into him accidentally in the cemetery once a year. Perhaps even that is too much.

I let him go. His arrogant style was beginning to annoy me, making me feel he looked down on me, and I struggled with myself to believe the version he outlined of the morning of that dark day: The Neon Nights only wanted to know if they were home. What nonsense...

I got home completely wrung out. My brain kept ticking on the conversation with Jeff, but to no avail. I knew I had asked good questions, only the answers weren't satisfactory enough for me. I felt I was slowly going mad from the black Mustang.
"Do you finally have your articles?" I asked as I dropped my bag on the sofa and threw myself on it.
"No," Anton replied. "The informant is now asking double the price for them."
"He doesn't do things in halves. In a month he'll ask four times as much?"
"Undoubtedly," he replied. "I didn't want to decide without you. Three thousand dollars is a psychological limit."
"Nothing to think about, Anton" I moaned into the pillow so he barely heard what I said. "I forbid you to spend so much on them. Get rich on someone else."
"And what's wrong with you?" he inquired.
"I don't want to talk about it," I replied.
"A great pity."
"But I will, don't worry."
Anton burst out laughing.
"Why don't you want to talk about it then?"
"Because I don't want to think anymore; my head hurts from it. And now we can't even have sex, because I have no desire for that either."
Lying on my stomach, I groaned on the bed for a minute. "I'd rather jump out the window."
"It's a one-story house, Amy."
"You ruin everything," I replied, and continued to torture myself.
"Are we suffering?" he asked sympathetically.
"Yes, come suffer too," I pleaded, and he lay on his stomach next to me on the sofa. "Here's a pillow, I'll teach you how to do it."
He couldn't stop laughing.

"Hey! Pay attention, there is no joy when there is suffering" I started education. "Set up the pillow, then place your head where the couch meets the pillow" I didn't check if he was doing it right, that didn't fit into the suffering either, only the whimpering. "Now extend your two hands above your head, hug the pillow, and finally pull it to the top of your head. Make sure the bottom of the pillow doesn't turn out! That's it. And you can only speak squealing like a broken violin."
He was still laughing.
"Did you know that Jeff's car has 420 horsepower?"
"Why is it an M?" he was surprised.
"Why is it an M?" I mimicked affectedly. This guy-language annoyed me. "How you get excited over a simple letter," I grumbled, but Anton didn't take it personally.
"It was so smashed up, I didn't even notice."
"And did you know his mother drives it too?"
"His mother?!" he disbelieved. "I find it hard to believe she even has a license."
"Well, that's it, I don't believe it either, even though I said I did, but I don't."
"And what else did you find out?" he asked.
"I wish I knew..." I began. "If Jeff has such a car, he probably knows how to drive it, doesn't he?"
"Probably."
"And if a car came next to you, wanting to force you off, what would you do?" I pried.
"I'd brake or try to spin it, whichever has more chance. Maybe I'd step on the gas and push it."
"Yes, because you know how to drive, Anton. But do you know what you wouldn't do?"
"I wouldn't pull the handbrake, because pulling it is bullshit when they want to crash into you."
We both sat up.
"At least the expert said so. But neither of us has been in such a situation," Anton parried.
"Still, we can establish that it's strange. Beyond all doubt, there are strange things here."
"What are you going to do now?" he asked.
"Nothing today. Today I only want to enjoy life and forget everything my head is full of," I answered.
Anton, as I saw, deeply agreed with my every word.

chapters.title: Chapter Eighteen
paragraphs: March 7, 2022
(LC News 11 a.m.)

Breaking News: The prosecutor's office has officially filed a complaint against President Edgar Mortenstein for a series of crimes committed in a criminal organization. The president is accused of bribery, organizing prostitution to the detriment of minors, forced labor, human trafficking, and murder. The prosecutor's office has requested a ban on the president leaving his residence, as well as his dismissal from office. For now, he may defend himself at liberty.
Responding to the emergency situation, the presidential office announced a press conference for noon.
We will discuss the expected events with political scientist Dimitris Makrekis after the news.

I muted the TV. Anton stood in the middle of the living room with coffee in his hand, pacing excitedly. A huge tension gathered in the air. Neither of us could speak. We looked into each other's eyes, and I began to weep from the excitement. In the announcer's phrasing, every charge sounded much more serious than it had appeared before. The president is accused of human trafficking, I cited to myself. This term somehow really caught me. Based on the charges, we were facing a man who directed a network using the toughest mafia methods, of which I too was a member, even if, as was just stated, I ended up there as a result of human trafficking.

When Jessica and I entered the walls of Neon Nights at the age of sixteen, terms like forcing into prostitution or forced labor did not swirl in our heads. We only experienced on our own skin what these concepts meant in reality, without ever having heard of them. The members of Neon Nights did not use these words, just as they never spoke of many other things either, so the girls and boys forced into labor there became the best in their trade in ignorance, even if they could hardly have learned this trade under legal circumstances, and of course it was hardly their life's dream to develop in it.

I have thought of myself as many things in my life. I was a sweet little princess (favorite period), I was a girl, I was a good girl and a bad girl, I regarded myself as a dancer, a stripper, a stripper with extras, I felt like a whore, a sex worker, I was a kidnapped princess (strangely confusing period), the luckiest and then the unluckiest person in the world, a fiancée, a university student, a prosecutor, and finally the meaning of Anton's life (most confident period). Yet I felt none of my titles entitled me to stand against such a bastard in a courtroom, although already in the stripper with extras stage I would have had every right to.

I stood in the middle of the living room watching the news; a whole country owed the lead story to me, and I should have felt strong from this, but I wasn't. Not even though I knew the world's sympathy would be behind me, addressed to the girls I stand up for. And among those girls would be Alice Bucks too, to whom I owed not only the truth but the fulfillment of her chief desire to put the guilty behind bars. At that moment, I didn't know which weight pressed heavier on my shoulders: this promise, or the restoration of injustice.
It sounds unbelievable, but Anton understood everything of this immediately as he looked into my eyes.
"You are the most perfect for this task," he said.
I knew he genuinely believed in me, but I didn't feel ready yet. Then my phone rang, and everything changed.
"Are you Amanda Evans?" came a voice on the phone.
"It is," I replied.
"Are you the prosecutor in this dirty campaign shenanigans?"
Sweat broke out on me. I began to tremble.
"What do you mean?" I asked.
"To attack the president. Are you the architect of all this?" insisted.
"I don't know who you are or what campaign stunt you are talking about."
The phone's background noise was filled with a web of loud arguments.
"You will not get away with such a smear campaign" the voice threatened. "Who paid you for this?"
In the meantime, I put the phone on speaker so Anton could hear what was happening too.

"I receive my salary from the state," I replied, "which maintains the institution of justice precisely to convict and imprison criminals who are dangerous to society," I turned the threat back, in a much more sophisticated form than the attacker's slander.
Anton nodded approvingly.
"We warned you! Prepare for the worst!" the voice hung up.
I opened my eyes wide, looking at Anton like a calf, but before he could speak, I said what he was thinking.
"What an arrogant prick!"
"You couldn't have said it better," he praised me.
"Did his staff really call to threaten me?"
"They did indeed," Anton replied.
"I seriously don't believe this. In this one hour, that's what occurred to them? Too bad we didn't record it."
"But I did," he smiled, and played the snippet.
You won't get away with such a smear campaign!
"And I think you won't get away with your actions, you bastard!" I shouted at the phone. "This is evidence, Anton, don't lose it! And let's hope we never have to use it for anything."
"Yes, prosecutor!" he obeyed.
I had barely calmed down when my phone rang again.
"Amanda, are you okay?" Athena Wright worried.
"Someone from the president's staff called just now. Threatened me," I replied.
"I had to give out your number, I couldn't do otherwise."
"I don't hold it against you, Athena. I didn't think for a moment that it would be an easy run. I handled the situation well," I bragged. "And I won't give them the pleasure of losing my head."
"Amanda was very good! Confidence in the prosecution is at stake with this case, but I'm sure we'll win it."
"I try not to think of it as a burden," I replied.

"They have done a good job so far. Now we have even more people working on the case. I have set up a task force for this purpose, and don't worry, I will not allow the President's team to influence the further investigation or the trial in any way."
"Thank you," I replied, although this was also the attorney general's job.
"Stay home today" recommended. "I will introduce it to your people tomorrow. Try to ignore the events. Don't watch TV!" with that he said goodbye.
"How does she expect you not to watch TV?" Anton asked. "It's very hard not to watch."

Dimitris whoever was just saying that as long as the president is in office, the case cannot be investigated, but if he doesn't resign on his own, parliament can strip him of his office. He also added that in the country's hundreds of years of history, it has never occurred that criminal proceedings were launched against a sitting president.
"Well, I didn't even know that," Anton noted, and I felt he was becoming increasingly aware of what a massive tree we had chopped our axe into.

Now opposition politicians were being interviewed, who, due to the disruption of the power order, smelled blood and tried to forge political capital from the president's potential fall. Of course, they all condemned the president's actions and were deeply shocked. A few of them actually seemed credible. Finally, I turned off the TV.

"Wright is right," I said. Anton stared at me in puzzlement. "If we pay attention to what goes on TV, we won't be able to do our work properly. Let the media do what it must, but they cannot confuse us."
"All right," he replied. "But soon you'll be a real star going up against the president. People will recognize you on the street."
"They won't even see me. I'll be in a courtroom, at the prosecutor's office, or at the police station."
"Of course, on TV and in every newspaper. The net will be full of you."
"No way," I demurred.
"Yes way," Anton persuaded. "And you'd do much better to prepare for this."

I indeed worried about this, but I tried to push the very thought of becoming known out of my head. I knew this was a huge case, but I regarded myself as a simple prosecutor doing her job, not seeking fame. I almost laughed at myself because this line of thought was exactly as if I had given an interview to one of the papers. Even this confused me. Of course, I couldn't have released any information about the ongoing investigation, but Anton was right that every media outlet would try to find out who I am. I didn't know how far they would go, but I was beginning to feel already here on day zero as if instead of the hunter, I were the prey. This was too big a burden suddenly. Just to be safe, I even took the batteries out of the remote control.

chapters.title: Chapter Nineteen
paragraphs: March 8, 2022

The president did not resign. Only, as he phrased it, he suspended his office for the duration of the investigation. His place was taken by one of his vice presidents—which one is essentially all the same. This of course was not said by him; I thought of it. His stepping into the background was necessary so that the authorities could investigate the slanders brought against him undisturbed. It was strange to hear this in this form, since the same authority "slandered" him as was dealing with the investigation of the case. Regardless, it sounded good.

The next day at the prosecution briefing, I found myself facing a real team. In the task force there were detectives, prosecutors, experts, communication specialists on whom the task fell to make our work go as undisturbed as possible. A more important topic than the trial against the president could hardly be provided by anyone at the present moment, so we prepared for an intrusive media presence. Wright forbade us from giving statements to anyone in the case; besides her, only communication staff could leak information to the public. In one day, interest took on huge proportions. Everyone spoke of it, the opposition organized demonstrations, a host of political experts gave interviews on various platforms, producing multi-million views instead of previous hundreds of thousands.

It was hard to stay calm. I was constantly scrolling my mobile and trying to assess the consequences of this negotiation, while I felt the pressure increasing. The politics of previous years may be in jeopardy, declared a political scientist, whose justification for the statement I could no longer read, because the title of another article in the news feed also caught my attention. The term presidential pardon takes on a new meaning. And indeed, as clickbait as it seemed, there was truth in it. Although the president had the power to pardon anyone, I knew he didn't deserve the same. The two interpretations began to blur together in my head, but with great difficulty I managed to squeeze out the screen of my phone before I was completely screwed. I took a deep breath. I need a dumb phone, I thought. And I put the mobile as deep as possible in my pocket.

The next moment, I already found myself walking in the hallway of the prosecutor's office. I tried to get out to the yard to freshen up, but a colleague, with an unknown man at his side, addressed me.
"Amanda, attorney for Mr. Jessica Marsh" he said.
"Bill Thompson," the person introduced himself.
I was very surprised. Jessica never said she had her own lawyer.
"Evans" I answered. What can I help you with?
"It's a bit of a weird situation," he started. "I have a message for you from Jessica Marsh."
My eyes opened wide.
"A letter to be exact."
"That indeed is strange," I said, "because Jessica Marsh is dead."
"Of course," he replied with the greatest respect. "I know that too; regardless, the letter comes from her. Can we talk?"
The man was middle-aged. Slightly stocky, wearing a checkered suit. If I had to describe a civil lawyer, I would have thought of similar.
"I just went for a walk in the yard, you can join" I said.
"You can also sign there" he answered.
"Do I have to sign it?"
"Just a formality, obviously."
The lawyer spoke slowly and politely. While we got out, I learned that he wanted to get home for lunch. It was noon. I didn't think we had to talk for too long; after all, it was only about a letter.
"Jessica approached me with a strange request a few months ago" he started
"Since when do you know her?" I asked.
"Quite a long time. It's been five years now," he replied. "We could say I'm her lawyer, as I also dared tell your colleague."
"Can we just say?"
"Well," he sighed, "you know, she paid my monthly retainer constantly, but she didn't ask for any legal help from me at all."
"That's interesting," I noted.

"Now and then he came to my place for tea, but even then it didn't seem like he needed my work. Of course, he was interested in things, such as how much legal support for the purchase of an apartment would cost, but I never once had to represent him in any matter."
"Did you just go for tea then?" I was confused. I knew Jessica well, but nothing would have been further from her than such tea stirrings. The lawyer obviously couldn't know that.

"I thought it would surprise you" he answered. "I'm sorry to talk to you like this, but from what Jessica asked, I was sure that you two knew each other, so I said that because I thought it would surprise you."
The thing was beginning to get confusing.

"Unfortunately, you are mistaken," I denied the situation, because although the lawyer seemed very friendly, this was still one of our biggest secrets with Jessica, especially now that things had turned critical.
"Well, it happens to me too," he replied.
"Still, it's strange that she paid your monthly retainer, yet only went over to have tea."
"We talked a lot."
"don't be angry" I interrupted. "but can we continue the conversation with what he actually came for?"

"Yes, sorry" he released himself again as if it were the most common thing in his life. "On one occasion, it must have been October, he stopped by for more than just a cup of tea. He said that if he were to die for any reason..."
"If she died?" I cut in. "In October she was already talking about what should happen if she died?"

"Yes. That's why I said she turned to me with a strange request," he said. "Of course, it's completely normal if someone wants a will drawn up, but Jessica was not even thirty, and she didn't come because of that."
"Do you think he might have thought he was going to be killed?" I asked because that was the first thing that came to mind.
"Or that she was suffering from some illness. Seeing the facts, however, I see more chance of the first."
"But Jessica had an accident," I explained, since this was the official position. "Didn't you ask her why she was preparing for this eventuality?"

"I reacted similarly to you. Then, with a pitying smile on her face, she made it clear to me that what she was doing was very timely. So she handed me two letters and asked me to hand them over to you if it happened."
"Me specifically?"

"Yes, that's why I brought it here; I must give it to the prosecutor of the case against the president," he replied. "Despite the fact," now he made a small dramatic pause, "that she didn't even know you."
"This indeed is interesting. And why did you only bring one letter?"
"Because I can only hand over the other after, and here I quote Jessica literally: 'when Amanda finally puts that prick in prison.'"
I almost laughed. Bill Thompson also felt how ill-suited this word was to him, which was why he tried to distance himself as much as possible from the quote's style.
"And do you know what is in the letters?" I asked.
"No, by no means! I respected Jessica's request, and these messages are not addressed to me, but to you."
"Thank you so much for bringing it" I said, underlining my name on the dotted line. I couldn't wait to open it. I had no idea what was in it.

"You are very welcome" he answered. "I wouldn't bother you anymore" he said goodbye. "I hope to see you again," he continued his rambling speech. "if so, well, yes, good luck!" We shook hands and he left.
A few moments later, however, he appeared in the doorway again.
"Miss Evans!"
"Yes?"
"Jessica asked me to tell you: cuckoo."
I stared blankly. I didn't understand anything.
"What did she ask?" I asked back.
"To tell you: cuckoo," Bill spread his arms. "I don't understand it either. I hoped you would."
"Well, no," I replied, puzzled.
"If you ever need help, call me!" the lawyer offered his services.
"Thank you," I smiled at him, but didn't understand how he could be of help to me.
I sat down on one of the benches and opened Jessica's message.
Fuck these faggots!
Hi Amy!

"I don't think I could have started the letter otherwise. Believe me, it's not out of my own good will that I'm not here. It would have been better to speak in person, but that's how it is. That's life. However we want to control it, at most we can ride on it.

This letter is a sort of insurance because I don't know when what I expect will happen. I need a letter in which I write down everything about the years spent in the Neon Nights. You know everything, of course, since you were there, and I told you everything about the period when you weren't, too. Take this as a written deposition for the trial. I wanted to report these in person, but apparently things worked out differently..."

Here Jess described at length and in detail on separate sheets everything that happened in the Neon Nights, including how she blackmailed the president into closing the club. To foreign ears it will certainly sound fascinating, I thought. I blessed my luck that the information was not lost after all. All the bad feeling that arose because of Jess's ease—that she didn't speak of the information regarding the closing of Neon Nights before the detectives—vanished in one swoop.
At the end of the letter stood a few more lines.

You can trust Bill. You know we know each other. I told him, but he would never abuse that. He is a very good person. He really is, not just pretending. I'm only saying this so that if you ever need to use it for something, don't be afraid to turn to it.

You should say goodbye. My arm is dying from writing so much. Anyway, I could only write nonsense, to say; I've been missing you for years and I'm afraid we'll never be the same again. And that; too bad I can't be there when you put that bastard in jail!
Don't worry about any of it! The first one really doesn't matter and the second one is your destiny.
i love you
Jess"

I struggled with my tears, but I didn't care; no one saw me anyway. I was glad of the letter; it saved my ass. The strange circumstances, however, I couldn't place. Why did Jessica already know her life was in danger when no one could have known about the complaint yet? Why didn't she speak? Why did she need this lawyer? Why did she go to have tea with him? And what didn't fit in my head in the least: what does it mean: cuckoo?

chapters.title: Chapter Twenty
paragraphs: March 9, 2022

The team worked hard. I contacted both Hermione and David King to tell them Jessica was dead. Both were touched by the news, but King still didn't know if he would be willing to testify.
