Despite my persuasion, he didn't want to be a rope, he just kept saying that he had to think about it some more. However, this was less important than before.

When news of the proceedings against the president spread, several people voluntarily approached the police to tell their own stories. Previously, Jesse and I were afraid of who the media coverage would favor, since the president had very influential acquaintances. However, I hoped that the truth could make better use of the power of the public than manipulation, and this was also indicated by the recent confessions.

However, Mortenstein's was still missing. Even though it has been a week since what he did became public, he still hasn't spoken out, and he couldn't just be taken to the police, because that would have damaged his political reputation. Several media still treated the situation as if the report was based on speculation. The presidential office was obviously trying to communicate this to the public, and the fact that it took a step back even made it sympathetic to many. However, I was not so forgiving. The hatred in me was growing every day, and the interviews with his supporters didn't help. One by one, the accusations made were classified as slander, and I had the impression that all our statements would be subjected to tremendous pressure, which would aim to unsettle the public. Regardless, I trusted the prosecutor's office, knowing that Wright would do everything to make this case run as smoothly as possible, even if the attorney general was known to be one of the president's proteges.

I was reading the new statements in the privacy of my desk when Matt called and asked me to go to his police station because one of the witnesses had a particularly interesting story to tell. It sounded exciting.
While on guard, I tried to think about how they could catch us. I was alert. I knew the president's strategy was to attack, even if he was now feigning retreat.

The girl sitting in the interrogation room was not familiar at all. Of course, that meant nothing. I didn't recognize David King either. Her blonde hair was down to her waist, her nails were studded with stones, and the clothes she wore were worth five thousand. Either he started a very good career after Neon Nights, or he was very happily married, I thought. In any case, there was something classy in his movements, and I got the feeling that he didn't learn all this with us, he could always be like that.

"I consider him my supporter to this day" answered the investigator's question about his relationship with the president. "I can thank Mr. Mortenstein for preparing me for my career abroad. Without the courses at Neon Nights, I certainly wouldn't have made it to New York and worked with the best designers week after week."
"What is your occupation?" asked the detective.
"I am a model."
"Let's clarify then. What is your relationship with President Edgar Mortenstein?"

"Look, I don't know how much I can talk about this, since I signed a non-disclosure agreement that year, which gave the trainings exclusivity, but I think that in this delicate situation, maybe I can still say this or that."
"An indictment is being prepared against the president, so I think if you know anything about the things the complaint is about, don't keep it to yourself!"
"That will be for the best," the girl acknowledged.

I looked at Matt, shaking my leg nervously. Just what kind of training programs is she talking about? He must have thought the same, because incomprehension sat on his face. We had listened through quite a few testimonies together, but none of them spoke of anything similar.
"I'm glad you called me" I thanked him while we watched the witness through the interrogator's spyglass.
The girl continued.

"My father has known Mortenstein for a long time. Even as a child I met him many times at various receptions. He was always very kind; I have a good relationship with one of his sons, we've been friends since then, so to speak, even if life drifted us far apart. He always knew I was interested in fashion, how much the glittering world attracted me. I was fourteen, just starting high school, when Edgar offered my father that I could participate in one of Neon Nights' exclusive training programs, which started specifically for girls like me."
"What do you mean by girls like you?" the detective asked.
"Attractive girls who are, of course, interested in fashion," she summarized how she defined her fourteen-year-old self.
"Did you have anything else in common?"
"What do you mean?"
"Say, that you knew the president."

"No, no. Definitely not," the girl protested. "Many of us didn't meet him at all. I was obviously in a privileged position in this respect, but the president had no say in the training programs whatsoever. He was only the visionary of Neon Nights' glamour."
"Glamour?"
"They always said: shine, and believe in yourselves, because then others will see it too. This was the first thing they drilled into us there."
"So the Neon Nights was a modeling school?"

"Like I said, I signed a nondisclosure agreement, but maybe they'll understand if I talk about it openly" she tossed her blonde hair back, then gathered her thoughts and continued. "I wouldn't call it a model trainer. Rather, a place where they are aware of how to turn a girl into a successful woman, and the focus is on the woman. Everything revolved around that. We had mentors who had already achieved a lot in their profession and were ready to pass on the knowledge they had accumulated to the younger generation. I was taught by real celebrities like Leona St. Claire, and I hardly need a better model than her."
"What else did you learn?" the detective inquired.
"A great many things. Etiquette, dance, acting, conversation. But there were those who learned music, singing, or other knowledge that a cultured and educated woman might need."
"And what was the goal of these training programs?"

"Are you paying attention to me at all?" the girl complained. "I told you earlier. The goal was to become women who direct their own destiny, rather than suffer it. I don't know how I could present this better to you."
"How long did the training at the Neon Nights last?" the cop asked.
"Several years."
"And could you leave the institution in the meantime?"

"What kind of question is that? What do you think? I lived here in the city, had my own apartment; do you think I was in need of some dormitory? But I see you misunderstand. The Neon Nights was not some high school. The lucky ones who were allowed to enter the realm of glamour went there after school and on weekends."
I knew I should have eaten less in the morning, because a bit of vomit came up. I wiped my mouth discreetly. My ears didn't really want to take in what they heard.

"Anyway, they took the studies very seriously. They only allowed a good grade in three subjects; in everything else, you had to perform excellently, otherwise you couldn't continue the training. They motivated us this way to put as much as possible into everything. There was no such thing as something being difficult. They taught us to fight."
"And were there those who dropped out during the training programs?" the colleague pried further.
"There are always such. Of course there were here too."
"And what became of their fate?"
"What would have become? Nothing. Or as I know, they had to repay the scholarship. But that was all."
"And what kind of sum could it be?"
"Ugh," the girl shook herself. "I don't know. It never seemed that this sum interested anyone. I was in no danger of having to return it, and of course neither was my father. I want to imply..."
"We understand what you imply, don't worry," the detective took back the initiative. "What age girls did they deal with there?"
"High schoolers. That's why they admitted me when I started secondary school."
"Do you keep in touch with anyone from the Neon Nights, by any chance?"
"Of course I do. That too was an important point of the training, that we learned to value our connections."
"Would others also like to testify? Or just tell their experiences?"
"Definitely. We would like the truth about Edgar to come out as soon as possible. I don't know on what basis they formulated these charges against him. But I can assure you they are ridiculous."
"So you didn't have bad experiences regarding the Neon Nights?"
"None at all. Of course, there were a few exercises that some might have felt were difficult, but I don't think one should complain because of that."
"What do you mean?"
I waited curiously to see what would come of this, and how the lie this girl came here with would fit the difficult exercises she mentioned.

"As I said, the most successful people taught us in their respective fields, and successful people are sometimes difficult of nature. Here too, someone might have felt they were behaved with humiliatingly."
"What is to be understood by that?"
"Sometimes we were scolded if something didn't go the way it should have."
"Were you verbally abused?"
"Abused?" the girl was puzzled. "What kind of word is that? They just shouted at us, a few cried, that's all that happened. Afterward, we wanted success even more."
"And were you not physically abused?"
"Again this abuse. This is some fetish of yours," the witness established. "There was a time when someone was slapped. But this really fit in."
"You said you were made to sign a non-disclosure. Why was that needed?"

"I think it's pretty clear. To preserve the exclusivity of the training. The girls were admitted to the training courses from a well-defined circle. And this circle was obviously not the song circle of folk cultivators from Pettyville. They didn't want some competitors to notice the training here. This segment of education targets a very small market. Every piece of information is behind the scenes, if I may say so."
"Do you know of any case when someone was sexually exploited or perhaps abused?"
"No. And I can firmly state that nothing of the sort happened either. Despite certain types of training touching upon this area too, of course in a completely discrete environment."
"What do you mean by that?" the detective asked.
"That no, such things didn't happen. How a woman gets to know her body, I think, doesn't belong to the category of sexual abuse. Much rather biology."
"I see, I understood, thank you," the detective closed it. "Is there anything else you would say?"

"No, there isn't," the girl replied. "Perhaps only that I hope this case is closed as soon as possible. What one hears on TV is truly unpleasant. I don't know who it could come from, but every word of it is a lie."

It was good that a glass wall separated me from her, because I would have liked most to tear her apart. I was incredibly angry. Tears were close. In front of Matt, however, I still couldn't afford this behavior. I couldn't place what the girl was talking about. I only felt she breezed in here in her multi-thousand-dollar outfit, and with such ease twisted reality as if it had never existed.

chapters.title: Chapter Twenty-One
paragraphs: March 10, 2022
I wasn't in a good mood. I had no problem with the gloomy weather, but even more so because it reflected my mood.

"She sat there like some kind of prima donna, pretending that it was New York and that she was a good friend of the president. Specifically, I threw up" I explained to Anton, with whom I couldn't talk about what happened last night, because he went alone to a reception, which I told him back in time.
"Do you know what keeps coming to my mind?" he asked with a beginning enthusiasm in his voice.
"I'm afraid I do. Whether we have three thousand dollars?"

"I know we have it," he smiled, "I just don't know if we want to spend it. But I feel more and more that those articles know something. That's where we first read about the Neon Nights in a completely different context. What if they write something about these training programs in them too?"

I couldn't help but agree with Anton, though I tried not to. Not because I too wanted to parade in a multi-thousand-dollar outfit in the lobby of the prosecutor's office—I would have tolerated it, I must add—but because I didn't want to accept that an aspect of this soul-destroying organization existed that facilitated the self-fulfillment of rich girls on the ruins of our dreams.

But life is just like that: it doesn't really pay attention to what we pray for, it is only interested in the rock-hard reality. And in that reality, the Neon Nights could easily have created a front organization with which it erased the traces of our exploitation, and of course sent the beautiful children of well-paying, loving fathers in our stead to where they belonged. To the top of the world.
Whether the articles for which we finally left four thousand dollars were useful? Undoubtedly yes, even if it was hard for me to say even one good word during the entire ride home.
"Four thousand dollars for this spit in the face?!" I grumbled.
"At least we have proof of this too now. By the way, where the hell could these letters have been published? They seem more like e-mails than newspaper articles," Anton established.

"Because they are. Newsletters in printed form. The whole thing is quite a fancy piece of shit. Acting classes, singer training, supermodel course... And do you know what hurts the most in all this?" I asked poetically, if one says it that way. "That it really worked out for these girls. As a little girl, I dreamed of this too. Then reality slapped me in the face."

I was sad. I wanted to cry, which I could have done with Anton, but I felt that the situation would not allow it. I couldn't cry myself, because that would have offended the prosecutor Amanda Evans, and she didn't deserve to have her accomplishments dismissed because of childhood dreams. You're one hell of a tough girl, Amy! I told myself.
"I think so too," Anton agreed. "It seems I spoke out loud again."

The days passed and Liz Bennet's girlfriends also joined the president's praise club with their confessions. In the meantime, it turned out that the girl's name is Liz Bennet. If I want to be precise, it wasn't about finding out, because the police already knew who he was, but I didn't remember his name, I was so angry with him.

One day, I really had to stay late. In response to the new information, we had to work out another strategy, which took a lot of time. The president's team worked to paint the complaint as the accusations of offended, dismissed girls, and the explanations in the articles appearing in the media sometimes supported this quite believably. I got home exhausted and tired. It was already past midnight.

On the street, the lights were unusually dim. I wanted nothing but a shower and to get to bed as soon as possible. The house was not locked. A bad feeling filled me. It was always important to Anton that the door should not remain open. During the day I sometimes argued with him because of this. Nevertheless, it obviously occurred that the door remained so for some reason.

The apartment was quiet; I took off my shoes in the hallway in silence so as not to wake Anton with the knocking. As soon as I entered the living room, the shape of a figure sitting in an armchair emerged in the dim light.
"have you come" Anton asked.
"I am. What is this, some interrogation?" I inquired.
"If I guess correctly, a policeman should be standing out there somewhere, shouldn't he?" How would he have realized?

So I really came home to an interrogation. My whole life I hated such things. I detested if someone called me to report, and since I started working, I tolerated similar situations even less. I found it slightly strange, because Anton never created a situation to make this obvious. This sitting in the armchair in the dark, however, turned out to be quite theatrical.
"Why the hell aren't you sleeping yet?" I retorted, and with that momentum I turned on the light. At that moment, a low scream escapes from me.

In the middle of the living room lay the body of an unknown man facing forward in the direction of the bedroom, with a silenced pistol lying next to him. A pool of blood spread under the man, reflecting the nearby furniture. Between the hall and the living room, a smaller but more visible bloodstain stretched for about two meters until it ended at the edge of the carpet. Anton was sitting in his armchair facing the hall, holding a revolving Colt Python in his right hand and Mr. Fuzzy's lifeless bloody body in his left. There was emptiness in his eyes, disappointment in his voice.
"You could have said" he noted.
"What happened here?" I asked back.

"You are the specialist," he answered. "This beast obviously broke in, which I woke up to; the cat started moving in the meantime, so he shot it, then headed toward our room, from where I shot him. This is self-defense, isn't it?"
"It is."
"My God, Amy! Call it self-defense, right?" his voice trembled.
"To be fair, it's a legitimate defense, but based on what you've said, yes, it was."
"I'll get used to it" he noted.
"What the hell happened here, Anton?" I asked again, but this question meant more; how could this happen?
"I thought you would be the one to give the answer to that," he put the gun down on the floor. He stroked his dead cat with both hands.
"Should I know?" I was confused.
"I was hoping you knew, yes. Say, Amy, wasn't there supposed to be a policeman out there?" he asked again.
"Yes, he should have," I confessed. "And I'm about to ask where the hell he was."
"Why did you have to ask the police to come to our house, Amy? Shouldn't I have known about this? Did you know we were in danger?"
"What does that matter?"
"Be honest with me! Have you been threatened, Amy?" he asked angrily.
"Still after Jess's death," I answered.
"You must be kidding me now. It's been more than a month! Why the hell couldn't you tell me you were threatened?"
"how do you speak" I looked at him. I couldn't stand it when he spoke badly, even if he did it justifiably.
"Damn it, Amy, why didn't you tell me?"
"Don't you dare say that again! Do you think you're tough at times like this?" I shouted at him. "Speak normally or we don't speak!"

I was incredibly angry. Not at Anton. Least of all at him, but I didn't want him to make me angry at him too with his ugly talk. I was most angry at myself. He was completely right. I should have told him, but I didn't want to. I thought I could protect him. A policeman had been guarding us for weeks, but I wanted to keep it secret from him. When I looked at him, I didn't see him worrying, only how much he ate a day. He had no appetite. And I had no strength to pay attention to this besides the investigation. I thought I had everything under control. I was wrong.
"Why couldn't you tell me?" Anton asked. "Shouldn't I have known we were in danger?"
"Do you know how much you've been eating since this all started?" I attacked him. "Even less after Jess's death. I am seriously worried about this."
"This is such nonsense," he grumbled. "I'm not a toddler you have to sit next to constantly."
"But you are very sensitive," I switched to a calmer tone, and sat on the arm of the chair. "I thought I could keep things under control."
"This is so pathetic" he looked in front of him. "Do you really think it's that pathetic, Amy?"
"No way!" I answered. "How can someone who kills an assassin be pathetic?" I looked at the corpse. I was honestly proud of Anton. "I should have booked you."
"Maybe not after all" he continued pausing. "Maybe it was better that way. They say better safe than sorry, but I would have hated to wait months for that to happen."

"You were very brave!" I complimented her, and I really meant it, despite how silly it sounded in this situation. "My God, Anton! They wanted to kill me with an assassin!" the realization hit me. It was as if the adrenaline caused by the initial shock had now allowed me to think more realistically for the first time. Suddenly, a strong fear gripped him. "I have to call the police!"
"Where the hell have you been?!"
"Just not for me...!"
"They attacked my fiance in my own house!"
"Call the scene, one person is dead. I'm calling the supervisor!"

When I managed to think through what had happened, I felt helpless. It was terrible to think that a hitman broke into our house and tried to kill Anton. I thought control had irrecoverably slipped from my hands, and I had to admit that I couldn't take responsibility for those I love.

Jess's death was a huge loss, but I had to draw strength from it and work on. The move against Anton, however, was already aimed at my breaking, in which I felt they had achieved success. Anton wouldn't have been a martyr, but the clear failure of my struggle for justice. A loss I couldn't explain in any other way than that I am unfit for the task.

Anton, however, warded off the attack. From his perspective, only situational legitimate defense mattered, and I hated myself for not being able to stop here, although with all my heart this would have been the only thing that interested me. If I had come home today to find Anton lying in a pool of blood in the middle of the living room instead of the hitman, it's very possible that I would have fired the Colt at my own head. I felt I couldn't be grateful enough to him for saving both our lives.

When he spoke to the forensic police, he behaved as if he were on a movie set. I imagined him choreographing the scene perfectly, then having the actors play it out. The situation was pretty clear. It happened a few minutes after eleven. From Anton's gestures, it came alive before me how the hitman slips in, how the cat gets scared, and how Anton steps out of the bedroom door with his revolver, then shoots. If the guy hadn't shot the cat, Anton would perhaps have thought I had come home. An unthinkable assumption. Mr. Fuzzy's misfortune, however, became our luck.

After the forensics and Anton's statement, I felt I couldn't stay in the house. This also had to do with the police not being done with the cleanup, and I didn't want to sleep in a place where a dead man's blood had coagulated into a pool in the middle of the living room. We decided to spend the night in a hotel.
"We haven't been to a hotel in a long time" Anton commented as we lay on the bed.
"We haven't had time to travel lately."
"We could stay in a hotel in the city sometimes" raised.
"It's not customary to go to a hotel where you live."
"People really could. You don't even have to leave the hotel. It can be solved anywhere. Let's say here and now" Anton asked.

"I think we did it then" I answered. "I don't think I'd want to leave here until morning anyway" We looked at each other. I just realized that we hadn't even looked at each other since I turned on the light and the image of our ravaged living room unfolded before me. He smiled, but there was sadness in his eyes. We were both very tired. "I feel sorry for your cat" I continued.
"If he hadn't misled the hitman," he said, "the situation might be different now."
"I haven't been able to get this possibility out of my head since I saw you telling the police what happened."
"Everything is fine" reassured me. "It was exciting."
"Don't say it!"
"You know, I noticed that he was fiddling with the lock a lot" he continued. "At first I thought maybe you were drinking and couldn't find the key."
"Sorry, but the very assumption is offensive!" I smiled. "So you were up there?"
"I wake up to very small noises too," he answered. "I found it strange anyway. After he came in, I didn't hear him take off his shoes, which you always do."
"Am I that predictable?" I asked.
"You did exactly the same today when you arrived."
"Because I really do, I don't want you to wake up" I caressed his face.
"Always wake up. I cannot sleep deeply until you come home."

He slid gently closer on the bed and gave a kiss on my lips. We put our foreheads together. We slept like this many times, when in the middle of our night conversations we suddenly got sleepy. Both of us closed our eyes, and we only heard each other's breathing. It felt good to lie like this. We held each other's hand, and snuggled very closely. Anton always put one hand on my thigh at times like this, and swallowed hard several times, which we both heard ridiculously loudly in this meditative silence.
"I'm sleeping now" freed himself.
"You don't have to" I whispered, even though I felt very tired.

Of course I knew that despite his pretenses, he actually had no intention of sleeping, which became clear following his next hand movements, as he slowly slid his hand from my thigh to my bottom, from where he guided his hand from behind straight into my trousers, and purposefully, since he knew the path well, pushing aside the useless back partition of the thong, slid his fingers into me from behind. I hissed from the adored feeling. I could never suppress the moment when he touched me like this. When he first did it, I enjoyed it just as much as now. We were completely tuned into each other. From the beginning, we understood each other's bodies. It was always good with him, but when sleepiness came over us, it was as if time stretched out the pleasure even more. First at the back, then at the front he pleasured me, and meanwhile with his other fingers he found the right point outside too, so the overall effect turned out brutally intense. But I didn't leave him be either. With my leg I kicked the trousers off him, and since he never wore underwear at night, what becomes a tame lamb in moments if taken care of was immediately exposed. At first I also used my hand to force the first sounds of pleasure from him, then when I felt we had both caught fire sufficiently, with a stronger movement I turned him onto his back, and pulling my thighs along him, guided into myself that with which Anton could think at the present moment. I loved this simplicity. Sex was always one of the simplest things between us, as if we were both playing from the same sheet music. I knew what Anton needed, and although at first I usually finished quickly with him, this position could draw out the time; besides, I too could enjoy that I can finally dominate, since contrary to appearances, Anton regularly hesitated to give up control in bed. I leaned deeply forward, so much that he could kiss my breasts too, and of course my neck, which I loved more than anything. With his touches he immediately created the feeling of heaven in me. With his two hands he gripped tightly in front and back, but this never caused pain, although sometimes he put everything into it, and although I had always thought this was normal, now I know that it was actually huge luck that there was hardly a place on me where it didn't cause true pleasure if he touched me. Anton, I think, could have done anything he wanted with me, and he tried to expand his scope of interest as long as possible. For him I was a map he knew well, yet could discover newer and newer areas of, and in this wandering only his imagination could set limits. And his imagination, being a writer, seemed infinite to me, and I loved the knowledge that Anton was on and in me, but even more to see how much joy I can cause him by this. Our lovemaking usually started fiercely, and now I tried to slow us back down, so we could feel each other even more after this frightening night. I wanted us to strike chords that only sound in a very intimate moment. We knew that this whole thing could have disappeared forever in a blink, and the realization of how close we came to this formulated the same desire in both of us: just to be as close to each other as possible.

After the initial control, the time arrived for Anton to take over the role of conductor, and after a quick side-change I was already underneath, with closed eyes letting go of the very last receptor of perception too, to receive the tiniest vibration of excitement, not least the signals coming from Anton's increasingly rhythmic movement. Every single thrust sent a new wave across the all-engulfing inner sea of pleasure, and my broken breathing only egged him on to even stronger movements, who now, turning me onto my stomach, bit into my left shoulder, while from inside, targeting the harder front side, he brought me growing pleasures, and with his right hand he raised my pleasure almost to the point of intolerability. Physically too, Anton and I fit together perfectly. What he gave, I took all into myself, and where Anton ended, it was as if a separate garden opened to receive his delegation. Our hands locked, holding himself above me with our arms stretched out to the side, while kisses and bites alternated. I pressed my head firmly into the pillow to moan into it every moment of our shared pleasure. From his halting breathing, I heard he was close to the peak, and accompanied by a strong bite, he surrendered to the stretching feeling, then relieved, he spread out on me, and at that moment the temptation of sleep defeated us both.

chapters.title: Chapter Twenty-Two
paragraphs: March 17, 2022

The fact of the assassination quickly swept through the media. I didn't regret it. The power of publicity, I hoped, might protect us. The president assured us of his sympathy: the woman who represents the state in the trial of the century; and condemned bullying. Of course, I didn't believe a word of it, but he presented it so convincingly that if I hadn't known him, I would have thought this hypocritical charade was genuine concern.

The investigation continued, and as a result of the attack, the patrols surrounded us even more. They offered us a sheltered apartment in the city center, but we didn't accept it. I felt that if I couldn't come home to my usual environment and at least pretend for a little bit that everything was fine, I would go crazy. We lived under the constant supervision of four patrol cars, which had the advantage that no matter where we went, we didn't have to bother with our own car, in fact, if we really wanted to, they even turned on the flashing lights, although to be honest, the patrol got bored of driving around the city earlier than I did.
One day, Matt dropped me off at my office.
"Got it" he said matter-of-factly.
"Who?"
"Ross Wilson is back."
"Just now?" I asked back.
"What do you mean just now?"
"I'll ask another way then. Why now? Where did it come from?"

"Toby McRae called two days ago to say he had tracked her down. Since he's been with us, he's been completely immersed in Wilson's old affairs. He found a lot of oddities. He was not only able to hide things from Neon Nights, but also appeared elsewhere, where something had to be removed in relation to interests connected to the president."
"And is he here in town?"
"According to McRae, yes."
"Do you know where, Matt?"
"You just typed the address" he answered.

I've become as excited as a first-grader when going to school for the first time. This lead came completely unexpectedly, and it was still as hot as if it had just been taken out of the oven. I wanted to see this man. I wanted to meet him and question him about every case in which we assumed the investigation closed prematurely because of him.

In moments we were speeding on the street with a patrol car in our wake, sirens blaring. Wilson was seen this morning in a hotel in the Nouveau Palais district, so we had every reason to assume we could raid him. The informant with whom McRae was in contact hadn't seen Wilson leave the building.

Terror filled the receptionist's eyes when he saw the three uniformed policemen with their hands on their holsters in the hotel lobby. It was a modern, clean building, much more ordinary than, say, the Corinthia Royal in an upscale district of the city center.
"Which is Ross Wilson's room?" I asked, as he nervously began to search the register.
"A 39-es," he replied.
"Is he alone?"
"I don't know. Guests don't have to be announced."
"Then how do you know if someone is freeloading?"
"They pay for a room" the receptionist blurted out.

Matt and I went toward the elevator, and two of them approached the room through the stairwell. The hallway was completely empty. Skylights illuminated the level; the corridor swam in a true flood of light.
"Police!" one of the patrolmen knocked on the door, but there was no answer from inside. He repeated it.

"Stand back!" said the other, kicking the door. The lock flew out of place as if it had never been there. I hummed appreciatively, though it was disconcerting to see how little it protected against break-in.

The two patrolmen went ahead and secured the suite, which consisted of only three rooms including the bath. Their effort, though undoubtedly professional, proved unnecessary, because a sizable chunk was missing from the skull of the lifeless body sitting by the table. Despite seeing a similar corpse in the middle of my living room two days ago, I almost threw up.
"Dead" remarked one of the patrolmen.
"Are you sure?" I was excited. "Blood" splattered up to the ceiling.
"Ő az?" I asked Matt, who was already calling Toby McRae for identification.
Twenty minutes later, forensics were already searching room 39.
"Ma'am!" one of them called. "We found the number of the man who attacked you on Wilson's phone."
"How?" I was surprised. "Did Wilson hire that man?"
"They spoke ten days ago, it's possible."
"Check all numbers! He had to talk to someone else. It couldn't have been his own idea."
"We're after it" reassured me.

A true storm of emotions raged inside me. An hour ago I was still fantasizing about how I would question Wilson about his dark dealings, yet now I brooded over why he had to hire the one who attempted on Anton's life. Now I didn't feel safe at all anymore; despite walking with patrolmen in my wake, the world turns a lot in a few hours. The whole case was beginning to resemble a mafia movie where one stumbles into corpses at every step. I hated the whole thing. Since the attempt, Anton slept with a pistol under his pillow, and the lights were constantly on in the living room. The image of Wilson's blown-apart skull was burned into my brain, and only one thing kept running through my mind: who could be capable of shooting a man in the head in cold blood, like some butcher.

chapters.title: Chapter Twenty-Three
paragraphs: March 25, 2022
"how did you sleep" I asked Anton around nine in the morning, when I got out of bed.

I woke up to the smell of mirrored eggs. Lately, the dividing line between the weekend and the weekday has slipped a bit. Once I was in the office on Sunday, other times at home at unexpected times. I didn't plan anything for this morning either, I just wanted to go to the prosecutor's office around noon.
"I didn't sleep" he answered, although it didn't seem so at first glance.
"I didn't even notice" I established. "Why not?"
"Jake Cooper was on my mind the whole time. Then around three I went to the office to write it."
"Was that when the inspiration hit you?" I was confused. "Are you crazy? Why didn't you wake me up, I could have done something."
"No-no" protested. "I was glad you were finally resting and I had to think everything through."
Anton looked uneasy. I began to worry that he was up to something terrible.
"I don't know if I'm right" he started. "And that's why you can't even take it as cash."
"I won't" I reassured him.

"I couldn't figure out why Cooper was important. We only know a few things about him. One is that he organized you, the other is that he was there when Mike Alioglu died. We also know that he lived in the Liberty neighborhood and where. But we no longer know with whom. Mrs. Alioglu did not know her parents."
"Yes, that's true" I answered.
"Since then, however, there has been no information about him."
"Yes" I agreed again.
"But then why did Jess give the detectives her name? If he was only interested in those two things, then why do we need to talk to him?"
"Excuse me, but isn't it enough that we got there because of him?" I was disrespectful.

"That's enough" answered. "but you said you never saw Cooper again after he took you guys to Neon Nights. He never became a gentleman. Absorbed. Then what could he know about the operation of the club? What could have helped? Why did Jess give her name to the detectives?"

"I don't know" I answered and it started to bother me. Anton was right. If you ask the question like that, it's really not clear why Jake Cooper was included in the page, which also featured Hermione and David King.
"So I had to put things in a new light" he explained. "Either Jessica was just hoping they'd find Jake and he'd know something, or she was sure we'd win with him if we did."
"Knowing Jessica, she is clearly the second" I answered. "He knew what he was doing."
"In that case, he also knew then why we needed Jake Cooper. What if Jake did a lot more than we've been led to believe?"
"What are you thinking?" I asked Anton.
"Let's say he dropped a threatening letter into Jessica's mailbox."
"Do you think Jess knew it was him?"
"You guessed it, yes. And I say, he could have killed Jessica" Anton asked.
"Maybe Jake showed up already in the fall? Is that why Jess decided to leave messages with the lawyer?"
"Why not? In fact. I think Jake Cooper is a member of Neon Nights and always was whether you saw him there or not."
"Is that what you remembered last night?"
"Yes" he answered firmly.
"You can really shuffle the cards," I said. "I'm just afraid that reality is not a book in which the details only fit into the known route. But even if he had, Cooper is still nowhere to be found."
"Yes, but I have another theory."
"What theory?" I asked, and I could see that Anton was afraid to share something with me. "Who's up Sherlock, what popped out of your pen?"

I felt good that he didn't dare to tell me, he himself didn't want to believe what he made up. A few minutes later I understood why. My world turned upside down. What he said seemed completely impossible, yet it was a reasonable argument in every detail. I thought it over and over again, tried to find a fault in it, but for some reason it didn't work.
"It's a little scary that you can't find anything to complain about" noted Anton. "I was hoping that you would point out what I didn't take into account."
"You didn't buy a lot, honey" I answered. "The only problem is that it made it all more factual."
Anton's idea really upset me. I felt like I was going crazy if I didn't follow up on things right away.
"will you come with me" I asked.
"I'll gladly take you, but you won't be of much use to me" freed himself. "My mind is completely blank."
"No matter, it will be good this way too."

Water stood in puddles in front of the one-story house in the Liberty district. It had been raining continuously for a week and the channel had fallen for days. The street was empty, only muddy dogs were running around with their wet fur. Even though it was noon, you could barely see anything without lights because of the fog. The old garden furniture was scattered among the ruins of the small garden, and except for one thing, everything was the same as when I was here with the detectives: There was a weak light shining in the window. Excitement flooded my whole body.
"He is home," I noted, and slid my gun from the glove compartment into my holster.
"Shouldn't I go too?" Anton asked.
"No, you stay in the car! Call Wilbur and Matt! Tell me Cooper's home!"

Stepping to the door of the house, my heart almost jumped out of its place. I'm used to going off-road, but I've never run into anyone by myself. For a moment I surprised myself by being here, and then my hand automatically pressed the doorknob.
"What the hell are you doing here?!" Jeff yelled at me from his laptop.
"I could ask the same of you," I threw the ball back. "Shouldn't you be mourning Jessica somewhere in Montaukville?"
"My father recovered, and I had work to do in the city" he answered. "What are you doing here?"
"As far as I know, he doesn't live here" I hesitated. "Or is the apartment in the Gemini apartment complex just a disguise?"
"Not that it has anything to do with it, but I sold it" he answered. "I couldn't live there alone."
"Already? You have a pretty good realtor, as a matter of fact."
"I'm asking for the last time, Miss Evans, what are you looking for here?"
"Jake Cooper. Or is this house not his?"

"Jake is not home now" he answered, a little embarrassed. "But this was already the third question that I didn't even want to answer, while you actually didn't answer one. How did you know I was here?"
"It's pretty clumsy that I could interrogate you like that against your will, isn't it? Anyway, that was his first meaningful question, Jefferson."
"And are you going to answer too, or did you just come to insult me?"

"I'm actually surprised. I expected him to be here, but his reaction is completely different from what I expected. You should know that I'm looking for Jake and that I'll find this house sooner or later. Why isn't it bothering you more that I wasn't surprised you were here?"
"Everything bothers me," he replied, as if he didn't want to understand what I said.
"By the way, where is Jake?" I provoked him.
"He will come."
"When did he leave?"
"Not so long ago."
"Do you think I should wait for him?"
"In your place, I wouldn't risk it."
"Why not?"
"Jake is not a very kind person, and especially won't be with you."
"Not that you, Jefferson, are one."
"I don't know why you are surprised by it."
"You misunderstand. I'm not surprised."

Ever since Mrs. Alioglu pressed Mike's picture into my hand, I kept it in my wallet. I didn't know if I was ever going to meet Jake, but I thought that if life brought me to it, I would grant the poor woman's request.
"There is a guy here who, I think, could have to do with Jake. But correct me if I'm wrong."
"Who is it?" he asked.
I gave him Mike's photo.
"Where did you get this?" he asked perhaps his second sensible question since I had been there.
"So you know him?"
"He is Mike Alioglu, Evans, not Jake."
"Of course he is," I replied contentedly. "But he was Jake's friend, if I'm not mistaken."
Jeff buried his curly head in his two hands. By the time he looked up, his gaze had completely changed.
"I didn't say you were wrong" approved. "It couldn't have been Jeff's. He hardly knew her. She was very young when Mike died" as strange as it sounded, I knew he was telling the truth.
"I'm glad we finally meet, Jake!" I welcomed him. "I see you came home."
"So, how does this work? Is it even fitting for me to ask such a thing?"
"You ask what you want," he replied coldly. "When did you realize?"
"Unfortunately it wasn't me who realized, but Anton."
"Her husband?!" he laughed. "Who did they come to me in Montaukville with?" He seemed far more foolish than to have such ideas.
"He is not my husband, but obviously that doesn't matter at all. Are there more of you?"
Jake looked rigidly into my eyes.
"Why? Aren't two personalities enough?"
"If you ask me, even too many."

"You say this as if you knew either of us. By the way, Jeff said it well: I'm not glad you are here, and you'd better make it short, because although it might be surprising, I am not the poster child of patience."
"Don't think I want to drag it out. You only have to answer a few questions," I reassured him, and meanwhile my mind was on when Wilbur and Matt would finally get here.
"What are you curious about?"
"Do you have a doctor?"
"Do you want to start from that far? I thought you needed information for the investigation."
"That too would belong to it, but if you don't want to answer, well, don't answer."
Jake nodded in agreement.
"Did you kill Jessica?" I asked something more current.
"I didn't think you had such a good technique. Do many break?" he bantered. "It would be too simple to answer no. Rather I say: I don't know."
"Yeah, I thought it wouldn't make any sense. I'd rather say what I think, okay?"
"As you wish, then clear out of here."
"Let's not jump ahead so fast," I answered.

Jake seemed very confident, but I didn't understand why. He had to know that since we finally have him, I won't satisfy myself with listening to his flippant answers. However, all signs indicated that this hadn't reached his consciousness.
"Don't think you're in the saddle, Jake" I started. "In fact. I want you to know that things are pretty bad for you. You are my number one suspect in Jessica's murder."
Jake stared blankly before him; it didn't seem he took what I said too much to heart.
"I thought we were attacked. But you obviously know better," he replied.

"Also, we know that the investigation into Mike's case ended prematurely thanks to Ross Wilson. The killer never became that way. Do you know who was suspected before?" he didn't answer. "You, Jake, and perhaps not unreasonably. What do you think about this?"
"I think nothing of it. And I think you forget a quite important thing."
"What would that be?" I asked back, because I was sure he only wanted to scare me.

"I'm quite surprised at how clueless you are about what I might know about you. He somehow starts from the fact that everyone sees him as a simple prosecutor. Of course I understand why you think that. He really managed to hide everything about his past life well. You can easily take this as a compliment, and you can even thank Jessica that her mouth didn't move for a moment. I tried with him, believe me."

His unexpected threat made me sick. I guessed where he wanted to go, but I didn't understand why he confessed that he didn't have anything. If he hadn't, all I could think about was when he would pull something out of the hat to corner me.

"But that doesn't mean I don't know your name, Alice Bucks. I know exactly who you are. In fact, I remember you. I've followed your entire career in whore-training, don't worry. Unlike you, I remember everything, and I can say one thing. No matter how much you tried to avoid it, the world will know."

I tried to remain calm, but only managed to freeze my face expressionless. Inside, it affected me just as he intended. Let him rot, I thought to myself. I knew Jess kept silent about me; she swore to this back when I presented to her what I would do with my past, but I wasn't prepared for a person whom I believed had permanently disappeared to still address me as Alice today. I hated the knowledge that he knows me; I felt transparent, as if not only was I naked, but my soul was completely vulnerable too. Of course, it would have been too early to despair. My incognito rested on rock-solid foundations, and Jake couldn't possess evidence against me. Regardless, I knew he would release the truth, and I must be such an actress as only Jess could be. Seriously. How could she hide Jake from me?
I didn't answer Cooper's threats, I just smiled when I heard Wilbur's heavy footsteps in the puddles. Take him in, boys, I ordered as soon as the door opened, and tell him the ropes about his rights.
Anton sitting in the car didn't even dare budge; he kept staring at the window of the house, as if anything essential could be seen from outside.
"So, how was it?" he asked.
"It's quite terrifying" I answered. "I have no idea how Jessica could live with him."
"It seems she loved him."
"And this I understand even less," for a moment I wondered what kind of relationship these lived in in this strange threesome.
"Did anything come out?"
"Besides the dissociative identity disorder?"
"Yeah."
"Not about the murders."
"And about anything else?"
"Everything about Alice Bucks that shouldn't have."

chapters.title: Chapter Twenty-Four
paragraphs: March 25, 2022

On the way to the guardhouse, I racked my brains over how we were going to get Jake to talk. I wasn't really prepared for the meeting in the morning, so I didn't know what to ask him and I didn't manage to ask the right questions. He would have gone weeks earlier. The confrontation came too suddenly today. Regardless, I was satisfied with this result. We finally found Jake Cooper, and he couldn't have been more involved. Jeff and Jake are the same person. This development sent chills down my spine and every muscle spasmed. I was shivering in the seat like I do on cold winter evenings without a jacket on the terrace of a bistro where they think that a thin blanket will solve everything.

But now, no matter how many blankets I could have spread over myself, the tremors came from within, uncontrollably. I tried to get through the questions, like did he kill Jessica? Did he drop the threats in the mailbox? Did he send the assassin and kill Wilson? What did you do after you recruited us? And of course, what really happened to Mike Alioglu? I knew he wouldn't answer if I asked. I couldn't get Jeff to talk, let alone Jake. Of course, I didn't even have to question him. There were people who were suitable for this, who did their work excellently.

Other than that, I was genuinely interested in Jake and Jeff's mental state, although I didn't know if I could say that at all. Which one is dominant? Which one existed first? Based on the old stories, it was Jake. Perhaps Jeff was born out of his love for Jessica, which inevitably reminded me of the origin story of some Greek deity. Of course, I wasn't motivated by sympathy or concern, I didn't want to understand his problems, I didn't think that his illness could excuse him from his actions, only my prosecutor self wanted to see more into his head. I was interested as a case.

Anton followed the police without saying a word, I could tell he didn't want to bother them. As soon as he heard that Alice Bucks was down, he got upset, but I stopped him before he went all in. I didn't want to deal with Alice just yet. I knew exactly what Jake had about him: Nothing.

The cars stopped in the police yard and Jake was driven to the interrogation room in handcuffs. He didn't say anything, he marched silently through the ring of policemen. In the car, he called his lawyer, who we had to wait for. He was a well-dressed, tall man with a stern look. I haven't seen anything quite like it before. His whole appearance demanded respect, created a frosty atmosphere.

Where did Jeff get it, I wondered. As a prosecutor, one is aware of the city's best-known defense attorneys, yet he was not familiar. Even though it was clearly visible, he could have been one of the stars of his profession.
"They have to release me today" he stated with a straight face. The announcer mode gave even more momentum to what you said.
"We won't," I replied, and tried to remain as calm as I could.

I was deeply outraged by it. It is not enough that the suspect does not want to talk, even this lawyer came only to obstruct the work. Protect him from me all you want, but without an interrogation, it's completely useless to have brought him here.
"We can't let him go until he's spoken."
"He is under medical treatment," he replied.
"What was he looking for in the Liberty district then?"
"Since when is it a problem for the police if someone stays in their home?"
"Do you think you don't need constant medical supervision?" I asked, although I didn't think for a second that I was cornering him.

"It is now" he stated again. "He won't talk. In the sanatorium, they can interrogate you in the presence of nurses, but not under these conditions. I'm not trying to impede the investigation, I'm just protecting Jake's rights."
"Of course..." I muttered the reply under my breath, but understood what he wanted.
"Then we'll take it over. And we'll hear it tomorrow" I closed the discussion briefly.

I tried to take a constructive approach to his offer and not deal with the first thing that came to my mind, how someone with influence can manipulate the justice system. However, I have yet to find a satisfactory explanation as to how Jeff managed to get himself a defender of this caliber.
March 26, 2022

The next morning, we were already sitting in the private section of the Rosehill sanatorium in the room of Jefferson Morriset, who suffers from dissociative personality disorder. Contrary to the promise, there were no nurses present. The suspect allegedly insisted on this. In return, I also had to give up my escort. Thus, the plural of "we sat" actually meant just the two of us.
"Amanda, I think I owe you an apology" said Jefferson.
"How what?" I was surprised. "Why are you apologizing?"
"I know our relationship didn't get off to the best start" he continued.
"Really? I think it started very well. I would have even given you tea if I asked, remember?"
"But you didn't ask."
"Really not. But I have to admit to you, I don't really care what kind of relationship we have. He is believed to have killed Jessica Marsh, a Crown witness in the trial against the President."
"How what?" he asked back. "I don't understand Amanda. Why do you say I killed Jess?"
"Because that's what Jefferson did! He drove the car to the entrance of a tunnel. He applied the parking brake and slammed the car into the wall, and Jessica died from her injuries."
Jefferson's eyes filled with tears, and he looked at me trembling.
"Don't say that Amanda, I didn't kill Jessica! They pushed us off the road, remember? That's how it happened."

"No, Jefferson, it didn't happen that way!" I hesitated. "There was no one else there but you. You drove the car yourself, pulled the brakes yourself, and turned into the tunnel wall yourself. You killed Jessica."
"I couldn't do that, Amanda!" he defended himself, almost crying. "Why would I have killed Jessica? He was my everything. I loved it from the moment I saw it. No one kills the one they love."
"If you knew the truth..," I answered. "But I don't want to come up with crime statistics right now. Only those are really killed."
"I would never do that."
"And the other? The friend in your head, say? He wouldn't either?" I asked irritably, whereupon Jefferson answered with silence.
"But" he finally answered uncertainly. "I'm afraid he would have been able to."
"Then we're just local" I answered.
"But I don't know what he does and doesn't do. But he knows everything about me."
"And what do I do with this now?" I inquired. "Do I take it that you and Jake are not the same person? You can see that this is going to be very difficult."

"Yes, I know!" he snapped, then fell silent. He didn't cry anymore, he didn't want to excuse himself anymore. "I miss Jessica terribly" he then continued. "Every day without him is a curse, but I understand that he doesn't want to deal with it. It's none of your business."
"I am a prosecutor and not a psychiatrist," I answered with some arrogance.

"You know, I loved him. It started many years ago" he started. "Suddenly, I noticed that Jessica was constantly on my mind. It was a strange feeling because I had never experienced this before. I grew up in a tough neighborhood and not in the best of circumstances. Love was not a frequent guest there. He saw my house, I go back there to this day. There is something there that calms me down. But what is the need for it at all? It's good elsewhere too, my foster parents love me as if I were their own. Also, my foster father is a psychiatrist. I could stay with them forever. Of course, I learned to stand on my own two feet over time. There are places where the disease does not matter. If you understand finances, anyone will forgive you for anything."
"Is that what you do?"
"With taxes and accounting. It's not very exciting, but I can work from home."
"What does Jefferson want to talk to me about now?" I asked. I started to not understand the situation.

"Amanda, I'm not a bad person. One part of me definitely didn't, and that part of me adored Jessica. I don't know everything about the other one either. I started by saying that I have my first independent memory from many years ago. This memory is associated with Jessica. I saw him walking down the street one afternoon. She was carrying a small red bag, wearing high-heeled shoes, and walking in them turned the sidewalk into a catwalk. It caught my attention right away. At that time, I was on the street a lot, because everything else seemed so natural, as if I was always doing the same thing. Jessica, on the other hand, did not fit into this permanence. I don't know where it actually came from. I don't even know why she was wearing clothes, I would have preferred to throw everything off at thirty degrees," Hi, who are you?. "I asked," Why do you ask?. "half of it," Where have you been in a dress and high heels?. "I inquired," Over there. "he pointed in the direction of the foot of the hill, because he had to cut through a steep street to the children's home. He pulled his mouth into a wide smile," You're weird. "he said," Thank you very much!. "I replied, thinking he meant it as a compliment," I'm Jessica, what's your name?. "I remember thinking about it for a long time," Don't know your own name?. "he wondered, almost laughed," Jeff. "I finally blurted out," It's okay Jefferson. "he answered," If you come here another time, show me a place I don't know. "He continued to stand there. I sat down on the edge of a fence. There were a lot of houses with gardens around there and I started to eat the few cherries that I got from one of the trees earlier. I was wondering what I could show him that he doesn't know. Then in the evening, Jake came home and looked angry. Maybe he was scared by this feeling, which he had never felt before."
"Is this your first memory?" I asked back.
"Yes, the first one. That's why I think Jessica had something to do with me being born."
"And when did this happen?"
"A few years before Jake recruited Jessica and Alice."
Fuck you, I thought to myself. Has Jessica been hiding from me her whole life?
"Why did you tell me this memory?"
"Because somehow I have to make you understand that I'm not a bad person. We have to work together. Jake wants to hurt himself and I'm afraid I won't be able to stop him."

"If you don't know, then why am I going with you, Jefferson?" I inquired. "If you can't stop Jake, what good are you? Let's say I accept that he loved Jessica, but that still makes his significant other my prime suspect in this murder. Jake is dangerous and knows everything he does. I would make an alliance with the wrong person if I trusted myself. But so that you can see that it was not in vain that he confided in you, I have to say that he convinced you. I believe you that he loved Jessica. In fact, I'd like to believe that Jake killed her and you didn't know anything about it."
"Can you prove it already?" he inquired.

"I told you before that I can't trust you" I answered. "But don't worry Jake, for now the court won't believe me. In addition, his condition completely upsets the course of the trial. We need to talk to an expert who can form an independent opinion about you. Anyway, where is Jake now?"
"I don't know" Jeff replied. "We haven't spoken since yesterday."
"And you can't summon him either?"
"Only if he wants to. Otherwise it won't work."
"How did Jessica bear this?"
"Surprisingly well. Jess also got along with Jake."
"Didn't you hide from him?"
"Jake keeps hiding" Jeff replied. "Except for the last period, it has not appeared for a long time. But if Jessica wanted to see him, he always found a way to trick her somehow."
"What did he do?" I inquired.
"Unfortunately, I'm not allowed to say this. Because of Jake."
"But you say Jessica knew the way to bring Jake to the surface?"
"He knew something that made him do it, yes. Jake would take it pretty bad if he found out what it was."
"We'll just find out" I acknowledged, because really everything will be revealed one day.
"Aren't you afraid that Jake will hurt himself?" he asked as if he were asking a friend.
"No," I smiled, and stroked Jeff's shoulder.

It was strange. I made physical contact with him for the first time. I couldn't put my feelings about him anywhere. For the past few minutes, I've been acting like someone who doesn't hate her with every fiber of her being. It would have been a lie if I had behaved otherwise. I started to break down the walls between us, I really wanted to believe him. I wanted to see him as just Jeff, but I felt that was extremely difficult. The smile and pat on the back were meant for Jeff, but I needed it just as much. I had to step towards accepting him once. For a moment, I was able to turn to him with love, and it reminded me how deeply draining it is to hate someone. I was scared. What if he eats me before Mortenstein is shut down? Why are all emotions so complicated? Will Alice Bucks ever settle in me? And if not, how long should I be faithful to him? Because the time may come when I can no longer be myself.

chapters.title: Chapter Twenty-Five
paragraphs: March 28, 2022

I spent all day yesterday waiting for Jake to make good on his threat. Even though I told Jeff that I wasn't afraid of it, I knew the world well enough to know that the truth often fades into a false fact among rumors and lies, which cause a lot of suffering until the truth finally emerges. Of course, in this case the truth was pure morality, and the facts did show that I had been an impostor, but Jake couldn't know that, and he couldn't have any proof of it. I eagerly awaited his next move. As it turned out, it didn't last too long.
The TV was broadcasting extraordinary news at 11 o'clock. They reported live from the president's press conference, which was more like a campaign event.
"Why are we watching this?" Anton asked. Lately he's had enough of the media. They dealt too much with the president's case and thus with us.
"Maybe he'll finally say something that makes sense" I answered. "He talks everywhere as if he is innocent."
"And you expect it to happen now?"
"No. But Jake definitely came up with something."
"But Jake is in the hospital," Anton persisted.
"Don't worry about it" I answered. "You can't underestimate it at all. When we were in Liberty he was threatened. He didn't do it for nothing, and he had plenty of opportunities to talk to anyone."
We sat in front of the TV with tense attention. The national flag was a characteristic element of the decoration behind the pulpit.

"This kid" interrupted Anton. He couldn't stand the president even before we met, so I could never claim that he hated him because of me. "A real monkey. It's like the whole country is behind him. Ridiculous. He was holding a press conference as president when he was accused as a private person."

He was absolutely right. It was clear that Mr. Mortenstein broke all written and unwritten rules during his presidency. He established a strong power, and every influential person in the country wanted to be associated with him in some way. He carried out these changes systematically, unnoticed by the average person. No one had built such a centralized control before. He became an absolutely unavoidable figure.
He walked in slowly, then with a serious face, as if he wanted to look each viewer in the eye one by one, he turned to the camera and began to speak. He didn't hesitate, he cut right into the middle.
"Dear fellow citizens!

Yesterday, I came into possession of important information. This information was later confirmed by a reliable source, and I will share it with you shortly, but before that, I'd like to address the events of recent weeks. We've been through a difficult period. Not only the presidential office, which is currently led by...—here, due to a sudden audio error, the briefing unfortunately remained silent—following my stepping back, but you too surely felt that the country was in turmoil. Following my slander, the leaders of the opposition immediately tried to ride the waves of brief success, hoping to gain a few votes; from here too I send them the message that the joy was premature, I will return with renewed strength after the trial!—he was almost shouting, so much did he put himself into the threat.—Many of us were confused by the news that our country's president directed a criminal organization that committed the most terrible crimes against fallen women. I listened to these accusations with shock, and felt at that moment that I don't know who the president they are talking about is, since as far as I know I, Edgar Mortenstein, run this country."
"Drown in your own snot, you piece of garbage!" Anton bickered with the TV.
"Shh!" I snapped at him. I was very curious where Mortenstein wanted to get.

"It was hard for me to pull myself together. I know how quickly the public can stamp a mark of contempt on someone, even if they know them, even if they love them. In recent years, more than one big-name actor, director, etc., has been forced to face the unfair and stomach-turning evils of the cancel culture, and experienced how it destroys, at best, only their careers, the early judgment, which is not based on evidence, but on the It was based on the insanity of mass hypnosis. I decided, however, that I would not run away and face the public opinion, because I know that I am innocent, but for the sake of our country, I decided to step back and put this in the hands of the judiciary. that I can confidently trust in the independence of the third branch of power of our state and in the apparatus belonging to it.
As it turned out, this assumption of mine was based on illusions."
"What the fu...?" Anton bit off the end of the word.

The president raised his hand to his forehead, as if grave spiritual pains tormented him because the independence of justice had suffered a blemish, then before anyone could accuse him of excessive theatricality, he continued his speech with confident arrogance.

Yesterday it came to my knowledge that the prosecutor assigned to my case is none other than one of my former wards, who, although she received support from the Neon Nights talent-care foundation, ultimately left the program due to her behavior, which it seems caused an eternal grievance for her. Now she has sworn revenge against me and against all values represented by me. This woman is none other than Alice Bucks, whom you may know today as Amanda Evans. It became clear to me that this absurd slander is her work, so I strongly request the prosecution office, for the sake of an independent trial, to remove this person from the body's ranks, and assign a new prosecutor to conduct the investigation, and destroy all evidence acquired so far!
A silent smile froze on my face.
"Let's look further, or do we already have the gist?" Anton snapped, not that I wanted that at the moment.
I didn't answer. But the phone in my hand already started to ring.
"He says the number, but I don't know who it is" I said and stared hard until he stopped.

As soon as it ended, I was already called from another number, and so on. There were only one or two calls where I knew who was searching; the others perhaps didn't even get my number from me. It was frustrating. I held the mobile as if clutching a grenade. At the tenth call, Anton unexpectedly took it out of my hand and smashed it against the wall with all his force.

We sat silently next to each other. It is hard to find words for what we felt. Suddenly his phone rang too. When he saw he didn't know who it was, he handed it to me. It felt very good to hurl it after the other one.

April 10, 2022

After a week, I went on a short vacation. Anton and I watched all eight Harry Potter movies in three days, with only the pizza delivery boy in our vicinity. Then on Sunday afternoon, Matt knocked on the door. At first I felt strange, then I soon understood that time had stopped only for me, in reality, all-out fire was coming down on my team, who, as if they wanted to survive a carpet bombing, were looking for cover in a cascade of unpleasant questions.
"Wright said he would expect her in his office by 8 tomorrow" Matt began with his characteristic calmness.
"Thanks" I answered. "Can I invite you to something?"
"No-no" he complied.
"There is not only alcohol at home" I insisted.
"I don't want to disturb, Miss Evans. I only brought the attorney general's message," he replied with shining blue eyes.
That was when I noticed that I was wearing the thinnest top in which everything showed through; besides, since we lay on the couch all day, I hadn't bothered with trousers in recent days either.
"Have fun, prosecutor, enjoy the few hours you have left of the week. Unfortunately, a new time calculation starts tomorrow, but don't worry about that, we'll take care of it."
"What new time do you mean?" I asked. I knew it was a big problem, but I didn't think until now that they would actually come off.
"I can't talk about that for now. Miss Wright insisted that I do nothing to disturb her rest."
"Are you okay Matt? Will the case be taken away from me?" I guessed.
"Miss Evans, that will be tomorrow's question."
"Matt!" I snapped at him firmly. I was his boss in the given situation, after all.
"It won't be taken away, Miss Evans, we won't give in to the president's pressure."
"Good then!" I answered a little hastily. I didn't know how to respond, so I said whatever came to mind. "Thanks, Matt!"
"Nothing Amy, have a good rest!" he left with that.
Anton stood at the end of the hallway with folded arms. He tried to eavesdrop on everything without having to say hello to anyone.
"I was afraid that they would take me off" fell out of it.
"The president specifically wanted to fire him. Being taken off the case would be one of the smaller things."
"How would I call you prosecutor next?"
"You always find the point" I answered. "Something happened and it really bothers me that Matt didn't tell me."
"It's not that important," Anton tried.
"Or rather that it is. Didn't you hear Matt?"
"Come back!" Anton invited. "Wright also told you to rest."

"It's like your mind is on rest" I smiled, while trying to pull the many XL t-shirts as low as possible. It wasn't the cleanest, for that matter. "Now you could even say that it is not only our imagination that is dirty."
"That's true, but not that only our imagination is not."
"Let's say no one who knows him has ever claimed that" I teased him.
"And someone who doesn't know us would have even less chance to speak about us like that."
"Why are you wasting your time Anton?" I nailed the question to him.
"I'm not stalling time, I'm pulling you," he replied somewhat cheekily, but in the next moment he was already tearing the t-shirt off me.
"Just be careful, Mr," I told him. Let's take care of each other's lovely things.
"I take care of you," he reasoned, then sat me on the chest of drawers, and I wrapped my legs around his pelvis.
"You won't run away from here" I threatened, but he didn't seem scared.
"So what happens now?" he was falsely consecrated.
"For God's sake, Anton, come inside me already" grasping it with both hands, I pulled the heater off him and slid onto his erect penis.
"I love it when you're inside me. I love it!" I told him the bare fact, and he, excited, dictated the pace of our lovemaking with masculine firmness.
"This chest of drawers is really suitable for this activity, don't you think?" he established.
"Much better than storing shoes in it" I agreed, though my voice sounded a little breathless.
"I love being inside you too," he continued. "And I love your voice even more during sex."
"You really don't have to worry about that" I reassured her, then pulled her head closer and started moaning right into her ear.
"This is what I'm talking about" he answered.

Meanwhile, I put my feet back on the edge of the closet and supported myself on Anton's stomach wall, then with both hands I pulled his face to my lips as hard as I could to plunge my tongue deep into his mouth. Anton took the card, I didn't have to say twice that I wanted to smear, we pushed forward almost up to each other's throats. "Like two overheated teenagers," he said.
"You don't like it?" I asked back so he wouldn't take it as violence.
"I wouldn't like it if it wasn't."

We picked up more and more momentum. Suddenly, he turned on his stomach, crosswise on the cabinet, and in this position we continued to rest, which we had been actively enjoying for two days. He passionately kissed my back, then, bending my knees, grabbed my thighs and started pulling and pushing back and forth strongly. My gasping voice was replaced by a scream, which tried to synchronize my sky-high pleasure, before Anton let me know with loud moans that he understood my every word. When he accidentally slipped out of me during wild lovemaking, I took the opportunity to give him the coup de grace. I gently gripped his rock-hard manhood and adjusted it to my mouth. I excitedly stroked it with my tongue several times and took it into my mouth as gently as if it were some wonderful relic. But I didn't want to reach the end just yet, so I repeated the above sequence of movements several times, leaving him a little rest time before I used him for good. I wanted to let her know how much I enjoyed the whole game and I wanted her to know that I wanted and loved her more than anything. When she could already see that the long minutes of my caressing covered all her senses with complete beauty, I gave her the final help with a few firmer pulls, and we looked into each other's eyes with a smile, while I felt her warm passion trickle down my palm.
April 11, 2022

After several days of complete blockade, I went to the prosecutor's office on Monday. It might have seemed like a run, but I didn't even think of stepping back, I just needed some time until I was busy with nothing. The press did what they had to do. For a couple of days I felt like someone who lives on a tropical island where it rains day and night, so you don't want to leave the hut.

Matt's words still made me uneasy. All I could think about was that they were going to take the case away from me, despite Matt's vehement denials. What else could they do? Mortenstein spoke clearly. You have proof that Alice Bucks is me. I was sure that the material had already been sent to the prosecutor's office, and I could pull the curtain. Even if they didn't win the case, they would still take me off the field. If possible, I hated them even more than before. Then it soon became clear that there was no such thing.

The president obviously wanted to give the impression that he had caught on and that he was basing his every word on evidence, but the reality was different. They didn't send me anything about their statement that could be incriminating for me, and I was surprised by this, because I would have expected from them that they would at least falsify some document about me. I was a little disappointed to note that I had overestimated them.

It couldn't have been a coincidence that they found me. I knew Jake and his excellent intelligence were behind it all, but it was reassuring that they didn't seem to have any tangible evidence against me.

It might have been strange to others why I was chosen as a target. If there hadn't been an active relationship between Jake and the president, it's unlikely that the news would have reached him so quickly, and it was illogical to bring me out. Anyone could take the case, it was all a matter of prestige for me alone, so apart from a little embarrassment, they didn't really gain anything from it. They spotted me, but this did not jeopardize the trial. It wouldn't even have been a disaster if we had to destroy all the evidence so far, as the president envisioned. Of course, this was pure bullshit. Again, it was just a good-sounding rhetorical trick, but legally there was no basis for it all. The president inadvertently helped me. If I looked at the closed chain of evidence, it even included Jake's manipulation.

I had time to find out by the time I got to Wright's office, or rather, by the time I got to the Attorney General, quite a few people found me with the information. However, my knock was answered only half a minute later by a basaltic deep voice.
"Come on!"
In Wright's place sat a tall bald man. I recognized him right away, even though I had only met him a few times, even then in the company of others. It was the chief prosecutor, Murphy Wittmann.
"I came to see Attorney General Wright" I said.
"I thought, seeing as he knocked on her office" he replied contemptuously.
"Don't know where it is?"
"I suspended it."
I didn't even know what to say suddenly. I pulled my head back and stared at him blankly.
"What did he do?"
"How do you say it?" he growled at me.
Suddenly I realized that it was precisely my manner that bothered this man.
"Sorry. It just surprised me. I was at home for several days and no one informed me that Miss Athena Wright was being replaced."
"I think rest."
"To tell you the truth, yes" I answered. "The last week has not been very easy for me."
"Obviously, since the entire media attacked him."

I couldn't answer that. I stood face to face with the table, behind which he was puffing, and I felt more and more uncomfortable. My thoughts were completely confused. I couldn't place Wright's suspension, I didn't even realize it happened at all.
"It's a pretty big mess" he continued. "He ran a little into the slapstick."
"Sorry, I don't understand that" I answered as respectfully as I could.

"They pulled the wet sheet over him. Then everyone has to explain themselves. Of course, nobody knows anything about reality. Pumpkin knows what is true from all this. But the story is completely believable."
My previous freezing came back.
"I mean, it's pretty much in the deck that you really are Alice Bucks" he continued. "Why not? After all, who are you?"
I was still silent, even though I should have said something a long time ago. The last few minutes caught me completely by surprise, I didn't even want to believe what I was hearing.
"Are you Alice Bucks?" he asked the question again.
"No... I'm not" I answered uncertainly.
"No, because I don't even know what you're talking about, or no, because I'm not telling you anything anyway?"
The Attorney General completely terrified me.
"The first one" I finally blurted out.
"You can be extremely persuasive" sharpened. "Does he perform everything so believably?"
"I'm sorry" a repressed personality I had seen a long time ago came out of me for a moment, "I just completely blocked him from his teasing style."
"Contentious?"
"It would be difficult for me to say otherwise, sir, and I confess I cannot decipher the reason for it."
"Look Amanda, or Alice, or who the hell doesn't know. I don't like sitting here, so I can offer you to shut up and then listen. Is that okay?"
I've heard too many similar speeches to not be able to ignore one more inside myself, while outwardly showing that I understand what they're saying. I nodded silently.
"As you can see, I have suspended your attorney general. Don't think that it would be harder for you."
"What do you expect from me?" I asked the question, to which I could not give myself an answer at all.
"First he said he would shut up. It wasn't even half a minute... What else would I expect, but that he fails the case?"
Suddenly my ears started ringing. My temples were throbbing. I wasn't at all sure that what I heard was actually said.

"I see you are confused. I'm about to explain it to you because you don't know a damn thing about how real things work, but how would you know. He's only a few years old. First and foremost: all politics."
I obviously couldn't agree with this statement.
"And he slaps her again!" he snapped, but I found it more and more difficult to bear this educational style.
"Sir, democracy is based on the separation of powers, law is not a toy for politics."
"Are you really that stupid?" he was confused, and I couldn't decide whether he wanted to insult such a peasant or without any ulterior motives. "Aren't politicians sitting in the legislature?"
"And I was talking about the law."
