"Do you want to teach me? You'd probably know what to do by looking at me. Another profession would suit you better. Perhaps it was fitting, if the news is to be believed."
"Why did you suspend Wright?" I asked, having to close my eyes to keep from attacking him. "If he looks like a whore, why did he suspend Wright?"

"Because there is nothing against you!" he yelled at me, which in terms of its content filled me with relief. "By the way, I watched for quite some time as Wright supported all kinds of concussion cases in a completely unprofessional way, the victims of which were of course all women, and he brought down and ruined even successful careers, just because he is gay and hates men. Do you know what it's called these days? Penis envy."
"The Freud" I interrupted.

"We have obviously passed that. Today, many people use this to explain why women try to take over men's place in public life, politics, and power. She doesn't envy men's tails, but their power. Like the proles the wealth of the bourgeoisie. However, just as the damned Komcs couldn't do anything about the situation, the women also keep failing. Wright was completely blind to men. Now, with the President's honor at stake, how do I leave a man like Wright in office? Totally untrustworthy. A lot depends on this negotiation. Faith in power could be shaken. The Russians are at war, the Arab world is seething. They attack the president themselves. Doesn't the country need peace and security?"
Wittmann made a real speech. He genuinely believed that what he had to say had a great impact on me.
"You don't believe in the president's guilt?" I asked. "But we have clear evidence against him."

"Evidence" he said the word regretfully. "What could not be proven in today's world? They will also assume that you are Alice Bucks. I assure you that the moment this is proven, he will fly from the prosecutor's office without his feet touching the ground. But if for some reason it would not be true, then you have to see for yourself what kind of filth we live in a world where everyone who whistles differently than they want is discredited."
"Who wants it?" I asked. "Who are they talking about?"
To this Attorney General Murphy Wittmann answered with a mock smile, and I still didn't understand whom he meant by this.
"Until the trial is over, I'm taking Wright's place. He will report to me and I will give instructions. You implement these yourself, is that understandable?"
I didn't want to think at length about the offer, since I knew whatever he said, I would go my own way as long as I could.
"Understandable" I answered.
"Personally, I'm sure Wright used his position to bully you into his views. I'm not mad at you" freed himself. "you're just an impressionable girl," I just want the president to get a fair trial.
"I understand, sir" I answered. "Then, if nothing else, I..," and I closed the door of Wright's office from the outside.

chapters.title: Chapter Twenty-Six
paragraphs: April 11, 2022

Several journalists were still hanging around in front of the prosecutor's office. Like weasels, they hunted for some exciting information about the case. Our journalists tried to answer the most important questions, but obviously many remained unanswered.

Nausea eluded me. It wasn't because of the journalists waiting outside, but rather because of Murphy Wittmann's blood-curdling despicable move that got me hooked. Murphy Wittmann. How familiar this name must have sounded among the students of the high school where Edgar Mortenstein and Wittmann attended decades ago. I knew that the Attorney General owed his position to this connection, but for some reason I lived in the dream world that the person of Athena Wright commanded authority and provided security against such obvious abuses. If for some reason, perhaps because of this naivety, Wittmann could have rightly corrected him, but that was not why he did it.

I stood at a loss in the corridor. I didn't know what to do. Then, guided by a sudden idea, I caught myself and went out to the prosecutor's office, straight into the arms of the publicists who wanted me. I didn't have time to think about the consequences. I didn't even want to. At first I saw complete confusion on the faces. They didn't know anything about me for days. I came by bus and even entered the building with sunglasses and a scarf covering myself. But now I was standing in front of them from head to toe, because I thought that from now on it would be better to do everything in front of as much public as possible.
"Are you really a whore?" asked a young guy who thought it was cool to start like that.
"Just like your mother" answered an older journalist. Those standing there applauded his answer.
"Are you Alice Bucks?" another inquired.
"No, I am Amanda Evans, and I have nothing to do with the one they are talking about."
"Then why does the president claim this?"
"Of course, you should ask him about that. But I know exactly why you say I'm Alice."
"And why?" several asked immediately.

Excitement vibrated at the location of the impromptu press conference. When they saw that I was talking to the journalists from the building, several people rushed to stop me at the same time, but I did not bother them. I wanted to take control of the case.

"It's because Alice Bucks was indeed held captive in the Neon Nights, then after they sold her to Marlo Martinez, she too fell victim during the Martinez settlement. And the president wants to blur me with her because he thinks he can remove me from the way by this."
"Did you know Alice Bucks?"
Before the law it might have felt a lie what I say, but my conscience was nevertheless clean when I answered.
"Yes, I knew her, but I am not Alice Bucks."
"And from where did you know her?"
"I got to know her at Marlo Martinez's."
"Were you also a member of the Martinez clan?"
"No, I was not a member."
"Then?"
To this, a shocked silence followed.
"I grew up in an orphanage, just like Alice" I continued. "Then, at the age of fifteen, I was surrounded by Marlo Martinez."
"Then you are indeed a whore?" asked the guy again. I didn't know from which paper he arrived, but for some reason he tried very hard to remain memorable.
"I was in Martinez's employ as a maid."
"And doesn't this count as a disqualifying reason to be a prosecutor in this case?" another journalist put the question.
"That I knew Alice Bucks?" I asked back.
"Yes. Maybe he has a vested interest in putting the president in jail?"

"I think it's hard for anyone to stay emotionally apart from such vile things. In this form, you could even say this. But there isn't. I have no personal interest in it. I just want to bring justice to those who have turned to the state for their grievances."
"Don't you think that in the current situation it is dangerous to weaken presidential power?"

"I would like it to be clear to everyone that Edgar Mortenstein does not equal presidential power. The fact that he holds this position does not affect confidence in the presidency. And the judiciary strengthens this power when it helps to remove those who abuse it or those who are unworthy of this power from this position. Of course, the trial will determine whether he and Edgar Mortenstein are unworthy of this seat."
"Did your boyfriend really get disappointed in you?" chimed in another excellent professional.

"What kind of question is this? Do you feel disappointed by me fighting for the truth or by standing alone in front of the press? What does my friend even have to do with this? Did you really feel the need to ask this question?"
"When will you hold an official press conference?" several asked, to which I replied only after a few seconds.

"More official than that? I won't. We'd better get used to keeping the press informed about the status of the case. Up until now, we may have chosen our words too much and worried that information would get out that would hinder the investigation. However, the reality is that this matter belongs to everyone equally. We will not hide any more, and please, whatever questions you have, ask them to my colleagues, who I instruct to answer everything they can, keeping in mind the interests of the investigation."
"Did something happen due to which you suddenly changed the strategy?" one of them inquired.
"Yes," I replied immediately.
"And what was it?"
"I just found out that Attorney General Wright was removed from her position by Attorney General Murphy Wittmann."
"When did this happen?"
"Probably in the course of yesterday."
"And what justified this change?"
"To this, I answer you with the greatest pleasure."
Suddenly a window was flung open, from where the Attorney General's shouting was heard.
"Evans! What did I tell you? He does what I say!"
At this, the journalists began to grow curious.
"Did Murphy Wittmann ban himself from commenting?"

"We didn't talk about that. All he said was that Wright was a man-hating gay who went on a rampage against male leaders, and cited several cases from the recent past where, thanks to Wright, men in senior positions, mainly accused of sexual abuse, went to prison. You may recall that the President also spoke about similar matters in his last speech, in which he wanted to do the same with Alice Bucks."
"By this do you want to say that Wittmann replaced Wright at the president's instruction?"
"You can obviously notice the connection yourself if you want" I answered.
"Can you prove this?"
"Only indirectly. However, it is well known to everyone that they were not only schoolmates, but are still good friends to this day. Based on these, who could not come to a similar conclusion?"

At that moment, Wittmann stormed out of the prosecutor's office. He started running towards me in a frenzy, but by the time he got there, I slipped out of the ring of journalists, who, recognizing who was running between them, immediately threw themselves at him and didn't let him move.
What happened after this, I don't fully know, but I was sure that I took control over my case.

chapters.title: Chapter Twenty-Seven
paragraphs: Weeks passed. At first I struggled with Wright's removal. My every nerve was straining to speak even a word to Murphy Wittman, who could not do anything against me because of the great publicity. He was forced to accept the idea that I was going after my own head, and although he looked at me with hatred every time, he still insisted that I report to him from time to time. At times like this, like when dogs sense that they are dealing with the wrong person, red flags immediately started flying in me. Every time he called me to his office, I felt like I wasn't actually answering him, but giving information directly to Mortenstein. It was as if Voldemort was living in his head and his voice sounded like Mortenstein's.

However, I didn't have sleepless nights because of this. Thanks to my new strategy, I seemed to be able to turn my luck around. They invited me to talk on shows, and after a few sessions I was able to enjoy the situation. Many people recognized me on the street. It was strange.

When I was young, like many girls, I dreamed of becoming an actress or a dancer or both. Thanks to Disney, so many people have already traveled this path, why couldn't it have happened to me? Of course, the chance of a ten-year-old dreaming in a children's home was zero. Now I got to know a country, and somewhere in me, a little girl felt happiness.

However, my other half, the adult Amanda, knew very well that all miracles last for three days, and that those who love me today may be shouting snakes and frogs about me tomorrow. I didn't believe in the kindness of people and I avoided the comment section under my videos by far.

There was one place I couldn't rule out. Spots appeared on YouTube and TV in unjustified situations, in which she was portrayed as a vile liar and a last-ditch whore. I didn't know what to do with that. At first I was crushed by this, I felt that they were succeeding in their intimidation, but every time I came to the conclusion that, despite all their efforts, the truth was on my side. After a while, I wanted to know what they were saying about me. I was filled with pride that they should use such tools against me.

The trial was scheduled for July 21. Until then, there was still a month and a half, and I vowed that when this was all over, no matter what the verdict was, I would travel with Anton to some ocean island for several months and not take notice of the world. For some reason I was extremely attracted to this idea. Not knowing anything but enjoying one of the paradises on earth.

However, I still had one big problem. I wanted to punish Jessica's killer. And even though I knew exactly what Jake did, he refused to talk to me ever since I lured him to Liberty with Mike's picture. I didn't know where he went. Physically, of course, but who knows where Jake 'went', what he did without Jeff having any idea.
So the Jake case didn't go well. There was nothing I could concretely link to Jess's murder, even though I knew from the moment the car was found with the handbrake on.
In addition, the investigation progressed promisingly. I was sure that we would manage to catch the president, but we still had to find answers to a few things.

For example, it wasn't at all clear why Jake had to kill Jessica, and we couldn't find out who Wilson got the tasks from. He had the assassin's number on his phone, but he couldn't get through to the president. Past unsolved cases, such as Mike Alioglu's or Katherine's death, of course assumed this connection, but did not sufficiently prove that Wilson would have been more to Neon Nights than a well-oiled corrupt detective.

The successful neon nightsos girls also caused confusion. Their testimonies were well documented and supported by many others. You couldn't be tied into them. We had to accept that the Neon Nights painted by Jess and the Neon Nights of Lisa Bennet were not in a relationship of thanks either. This did not mean that the two were mutually exclusive. Rather, it supported the president's social insensitivity. However, we have not yet found what connects the two places, although it seemed like something very tangible and simple that we do not notice.

I discussed the problematic details with Anton many times at home, but we did not get ahead. We always got stuck somewhere. He obviously couldn't track down Wilson. The Wilburs tried to decipher that thread with the help of Patrolman McRae, but I didn't expect that from him. I wanted him to notice some detail that I couldn't see with my own eyes.

We were talking about this today as well, and no matter how hard we tried, we couldn't think of anything new. I wanted to go to the prosecutor's office in the afternoon, but now I was still trying to drag out the morning routine as long as possible, so I had been in the kitchen for about half an hour to meticulously prepare breakfast, a simple fried egg.
Anton was sitting at the table reading the news while sipping his coffee. It annoyed me.
"Please don't do it" I told him.
"I was just drinking" he answered.
"You don't drink, you sip, you can drink without it."
"I'm sorry" he answered, and went out into the living room.
I didn't want him to go out, but didn't know what to do.
"Don't you get bothered by some sounds?" I asked aloud, not wanting it to be my fault that he had to go out. He didn't answer, so I went to the living room. "Are you not bothered by sounds?"
"But yes."
"And what is it?"
"A few words" he answered. "But those are annoying words."
"What, for example?" I asked him.
"Let's say the child. This annoys me to death."
"I understand, so if I started saying that the toddler did this, the toddler did this, the toddler did this and that, the toddler played with the toddler and the babysitter..."
"Okay, stop it! Yes, of course it annoys me. But this voice doesn't come out of me directly, I understand that it bothers me, I pay attention."

"How stupid the brain is sometimes" I thought about it. "Why do some noises or words upset you? Some people really like these words, while others hate them. There are quite a few of them, like the thumb for example. What does this even mean? How did you measure something that was an inch with that? Or is that exactly why it became inch by inch? But then what could it originally mean? It makes no sense."
"Or the tummy-dweller, and the bro."
"Do you hate bro?"
"Is that a question? How irritating, Amy. When a stranger walks up to you and says, bro! I'm sure I'm not your brother."

"It really annoys me when they say something grammatically wrong. I would rather rip off the face of the one who says: because of this or that he is smaller than me. I have it! At me! From me means something completely different."
"You are right. And then there are the stupid songs or music that creeps into your ears and when you're at your deepest and worst, they start echoing in your brain."
"Like Baby Shark."
"And the Chicken Song."
"The Pen Pineapple Apple Pen..."

"Or that old song about cuckoos. I can't remember a word of it, except that it's a cuckoo, and that's what he says at the end of every line, and when you brought up what the lawyer, Jess's lawyer, said last time, that song was still in my head for days, and I'm sure it will be for another two days, now that I've remembered it again."
"You are a genius Anton!" I exclaimed. "You are a genius! The cuckoo! That's the crazy word we've been looking for!"
"Who was looking for it?"
"So what! It wasn't for nothing that Jessica messaged Bill. It's not just you who can't stand Anton, Jake can't either! This is the key to it, I put my neck on it."
"And what do you want to do now?"
"Interrogate."
"Don't you want to test it before him?"
"I know I'm right. There is no other way for this message to make sense. I'll set the scene, then we'll debrief Jake by mentioning him."
"Maybe you shouldn't go there alone."
"I won't be alone, and I won't interrogate either. There are much better people at the board for that."
"And if he says nothing?"
"All we have to do is confront him again to know exactly what he did, and one way or another, we're going to put him in jail."

chapters.title: Chapter Twenty-Eight
paragraphs: Only two of us went to the sanatorium. Me and a detective named Derek, who was a lot trickier than you'd think at first glance. He was a short, skinny man, his hair was frayed here and there in the worst places, his appearance almost made people dislike him. However, his voice exuded a calmness as if he were a therapist. This quality made him a really pleasant company, which was reinforced by his unmistakable perfume scent.

Jeff was not alone. In addition to his lawyer, a nurse and a doctor were also in the room. The latter considered it absolutely important to point out that Jefferson had not been in contact with the outside world since he was brought in, so he emphatically asked us not to bother him with things that have since become public. Of course, I had a hard time believing this. I was sure he found a way to talk to the president. True, he could have done this even before he was brought here.
We were seated around a larger table, even though we didn't come to negotiate, we just wanted to learn the truth from someone who had been living a lie for some time.
"What's your name?" Derek asked as a warm-up.
"Jefferson Morriset," Jeff replied.
"What do you do for a living?"
"I am an accountant."
"What was your relationship with Jessica Marsh?"
"We lived together."
"How long?"
"Until February 12, 2022. Until Jess died."
"And what was the cause of death?"
"A car accident, actually" replied Jefferson, summarizing the well-known story.
"Can you explain how it happened?"
"We left Montaukville for an evening performance, but on the way we hit a tunnel entrance."
"Why?"
"Because a foreign car, a Ford Mustang GT, pushed us off the road."
"What did that car look like?"
"It was black."
"Haven't you seen it before?"
"No."
"Have you seen this before?" Derek asked.
"A few times. But only like everyone else."
"Was its sound not familiar?"
"No," Jeff replied. "Why would it be? I cannot recognize a Mustang just by its sound. I like cars, but I'm not that obsessed."
"Do you remember how the accident happened?"
"We were followed, but I don't remember the concrete impact."
"Was Jessica afraid?"
"No. I remember I was the one who told her that I think we are followed. She didn't care. She said I was imagining things."
"Yet it is pretty obvious if one is followed, isn't it?"

"I don't know. I found it strange, but I can't say exactly when it started to become suspicious for me. There is only one way to Riverdale from Montaukville. Anyone who wants to go there has to follow others for a long time, because there are few straight road sections and it is impossible to overtake."
"Remember the squeeze?"
"Not completely."
"So it's not possible that it was just an accident?"
"I don't know," Jeff replied.
"Why are you uncertain about this?" Derek asked back.

"Because Jessica was threatened before she withdrew her complaint against the president. I was very worried about him before, I thought he might be in danger. But after that, what reason would there be to hurt him?"
"We don't know, that's what we want to find out" replied the detective. "How long did they follow you with the Mustang?"
"Almost from the beginning of the road."
"And was there a section before that where they could have overtaken?"
"At first, yes" replied Jeff. "but I didn't think they wanted to leave us. I was moving pretty fast too."
"Did you see how many people were in the car?"
"No. It was completely dark. On a public road at such a time, it is impossible to say who is sitting in the car."
"And you didn't know them?"
"Not!" Jeff replied a little more nervously. "How would I have known them if I hadn't even seen who was sitting in the car?"
"That makes perfect sense" Derek stated. "Indeed, when it is dark it is impossible to determine how many people are in a car. Yet you speak of them in the plural every time. Why?"
Jeff fell silent. He had no idea why he speaks in the plural. I saw on him as he tries to think the matter through.
"You don't have to answer the questions Jefferson" interjected the lawyer.
"Yes, I know" acknowledged Jeff, "but i want to" with that he turned to Derek. "I don't know why" he finally answered. "For some reason I always thought there were more."
"I understand" the detective acknowledged. "Do you remember who called the ambulance?"
"No."
"A married couple who came from the opposite direction."
"I didn't know that. I didn't remember them."
"Do you know what the rest of the road looks like from the tunnel to Riverdale?"
"It's still winding. Nowhere in the middle of a village or another city."
"And what about intersections?"
"Not much of that either."
"Yes, there really isn't. Not so much that the oncoming car had already left the last intersection, which is towards Riverdale, by the time they got to the tunnel, and they didn't see anyone coming."
"This is not so surprising" Jeff replied. "Those who followed us could have turned around immediately after the accident."

"That's true. But a few minutes after the emergency services were notified, another car arrived at the scene of the accident from Montaukville, and they also did not encounter oncoming traffic. How is this possible?"

"What do you mean by that?" Jefferson was puzzled. "I am aware of what I saw. I know there was a car there. Just because I'm in a sanatorium, don't look like a fool!" he snapped. It was hard for him to bear being squeezed.
"I don't see you as a fool, take it easy, Jefferson. I'm just stating the facts. That car could have disappeared from our sight in countless other ways, say on a side road in the forest."
"Jefferson, please listen to me and don't answer the detective's questions" interjected the lawyer again. "I want us to end this."
"We won't" I interrupted. "We are treating Jefferson as a suspect and are currently working to clear himself."
"Really?" the lawyer asked back. "They work in a very interesting way."
"Sir!" Derek continued. "We still have some questions to ask, but really, Jefferson is not primarily the one we look to for answers."
"If they want to talk to Jake Cooper, I'm afraid they're going to run into walls. It's been several weeks since he last communicated with us. He is extremely self-righteous."

I was obviously aware of this too, but I hadn't come here for information. Jefferson fidgeted nervously in his chair, and I felt he is still hiding something from us that it is more comfortable not to talk about.
"Is there anything Jefferson wants to talk about?" I asked.
"No," he replied quickly and shortly.
"Then perhaps let's switch conversation partners," I suggested, and pompously, like one who is completely sure of her case, leaned back in my chair.
"What do you want to do?" asked Jeff's lawyer.
"Miss Evans has a theory about how to seduce Jake Cooper" replied the detective.
"This is fantastic news!" the lawyer quipped. "And yet what does it take?"
"Nothing special" I answered. "Just a keyword, say, like: cuckoo."
Jefferson's face suddenly changed.
"Are you thinking that, like some idiot in a spy movie, he'll use a key word to trick a sleeper agent into Jefferson's alter ego?"
"Yes, to that" I answered. "I mean specifically this word: cuckoo."
"Don't say it again!" Jeff interrupted.
"And why not?" I inquired. "Because Jake Cooper can't stand the word cuckoo?"
At this moment, not only did the facial expression transform on Jeff's face, but his gaze did too.
"Fuck Alice Bucks!" Jake blurted out. "Fuck you, you stinky bitch!"
"How are you talking to the prosecutor?!" Detective Derek snapped. "Don't provoke the accusation with rumours!"

"Jake, please don't say anything!" interjected the lawyer. "And don't insult the prosecutor either! Now they will forgive you, as this was an extremely insidious move on the part of the investigative body, but don't lose your cool!"
"Same" I calmed my temper. "I'm not really offensive, especially not so petty as to take what a suspect takes personally in a situation like this."
Jake felt a little at a loss. He suddenly didn't understand why everyone was interrupting.

"I don't say anything" he stated finally. "They can leave. But this woman is really Alice Bucks, Jessica's old good friend, so she has no right to be here because she is involved in the case. You will see for yourself. Did they come for justice? Well, they got it."
It was the first time I saw the lawyer restless, almost angry. On the other hand, an insignificant yet noticeable smile appeared on Derek's mouth.

"No problem" I interrupted before anyone could distract them from the point. "We got it. According to you, my name is Alice Bucks and I was Jessica's friend. Will you tell me who you heard this from?"
"From nobody. I haven't spoken to anyone since I was brought here."
"Nor with his lawyer?" I inquired.
"Jake, don't answer!" the lawyer spoke again in a commanding voice, but Jake still didn't pay attention to him.
"From no one. What does it matter?" he poked in huffily. He didn't even notice that he had already told us what we came for.

"How do you know Alice Bucks anyway?" Derek asked. "Jake only knows him from Neon Nights! Does this rumor about the prosecutor come from you?" The lawyer wanted to end the questioning in exasperation.

"Thank you very much for addressing it," I said to Jake calmly. "You see, we didn't even have to cross-examine him to admit that he was a member of Neon Night and that he was related to Edgar Mortenstein."

chapters.title: Chapter Twenty-Nine
paragraphs: It worked! I thought to myself. They finally managed to get some sort of confession out of Jake. I was satisfied with the interrogation, Derek did a good job. A negotiation, no matter how precisely one prepares for it, does not always go exactly as planned. The two sides try to hide their most important trump cards from each other until the point when they have to deliver the first blows to the other, and if we're lucky, nothing comes up that we didn't expect before.

Although we willingly answered the questions of the press, we could not give them everything. This was partly due to legal obstacles and partly strategic. Because of the publicity, it may have seemed to many that we were conducting a completely transparent investigation, which of course we tried to confuse the opponent as well.

It was noticeably successful. The Mortensteins were getting nervous. They didn't expect us to fight back. Unpleasant articles began to appear about the relationship between Wittmann and Mortenstein, which was never a secret, but now it seemed much more interesting than before. Clumsy answers were received from the president to questions about this. The position of chief prosecutor was obviously not due to his professional merits, but to political decisions.

In addition, there was the Jake thread too, which in itself couldn't yet overthrow Mortenstein's power—since anyone can be an acquaintance of a suspected killer—but the knowledge that Jake might still be working for him fired everyone up.

At noon that day, I was waiting for Anton, who was being held up by his editor, in our usual restaurant, when suddenly five men in black suits appeared and sat down in different parts of the guest area. I thought the scene strange until Edgar Mortenstein stood in front of me in full life size and without question took the place reserved for Anton. This is when someone is late, I thought annoyed. A president is already stepping down.

I was shaking. I didn't even have time to react to it, it came so unexpectedly, it completely upset me. I felt like my face was turning into a winter landscape, while drops of water from a hot storm dripped down my forehead. I've never seen this man like that before. His narrow eyes and thin mouth gave off an austerity, his scent was masculine yet fresh, and his jacket lapel was decorated with a jeweled pin. A well-known yet unknown figure sat in front of me.
"Busy" I snapped at him immediately, I didn't even know how, because I hadn't felt so uncomfortable in a long time.
The president reacted to my attempt with silent laughter.
"Good morning, Miss Evans!" spoke up.
"Well, do you know my name?" I asked with irony in my voice, still chalking up my outspoken style to embarrassment rather than courage.
"In politics, you know, the rules are quite flexible. Sometimes you can do something, sometimes you can't. I'm an experimental type."
"I hope you know that what you are doing now is completely illegal."
"And do you know that you won't make an objection because of it?"
"Why wouldn't I?"

"Because you like fair games, don't you?" he inquired, but the sentence carried too much meaning for me to be sure that I understood correctly. "I just wanted to meet you in person" reassured. "Please don't take it as an intrusion..," then he finished the sentence while looking into my eyes. "Miss Evans, prosecutor."
"Why are you repeating my name?" I asked.
"They say we have met already, but I think I would remember if that had been so."
"It would probably have stayed with me too if that had been so," I repeated.
"I owe you an apology. The media has obviously gone beyond all limits. I didn't consider what I could do to you, I'm sorry."
Specifically, I felt nauseous. It may seem strange, but I was happy about it. I finally felt something that was simple and clear.
"It's too early to apologize" I answered. "Besides, there is no need for it. He who puts his hand on the plow's horn should not look back."
"You are versed in classical education; I like that," Mortenstein noted.
"I would like to draw your attention to the fact that it is increasingly difficult for me to consider this encounter as non-violating influence."

"Then I really need to leave" he laughed. "I just felt like I needed to see you" freed himself. "Believe it or not, we're on the same page. We both want the truth to prevail. I trust you, Amanda!" and he emphasized what he was saying by gently taking my hand.
He stood up and left. As quickly as he came, he was gone. I didn't wake up from the shock until Anton got there.
"Mortenstein was here" I started.
"The president?" he was surprised.
"No, the actor... why are you asking stupid questions?"
"Did you threaten me?"

"Why would he threaten me? I mean...yeah, sure...but I didn't understand how he did it. He just showed up and sat in your seat. Why the hell do you have to be late when this man is setting me up? I don't know what he did, I'm still shaking."
"Did he say something?" he too seemed upset by this unexpected visit.
"Of course he said, he said: sorry, and that he was Miss Evans, and that he didn't know me."
"I don't fully understand."

"I don't understand either..," I answered at a loss. "He came here to the restaurant with five suits and then sat down. I said it was busy. He said Miss Evans, and I said, oh, do you know my name? To the fact that all means are allowed in politics. Me: It's illegal to influence the prosecutor before the trial, he says it's obvious, but he wanted to see it because he doesn't even know him. Don't say it! He said, I'm sorry, you missed the target, and I'm fine with the influence of the prosecutor's office, and then he left as soon as he came. That's how it happened, except that none of the words were pronounced like that."
"What the hell was this?" Anton put the question that I too had been racking my brain on since Mortenstein left.
"Let's tell him to make his presence felt, to make his power felt, that he can do it at any time, that he just appears. These are very big animals Anton, these polar bears..."
"There is also a brown one among them..." he continued the well-known meme, then we fell silent.

I didn't lose my appetite. I felt the situation was much more depressing. A multi-climbed weight weighed on every square centimeter of my body. This is how pressure under water is used to be perceived, although it is quite possible that this is not a mere illustration, it is the same in reality, even if there is no one here who can tell you what it feels like, because the body immediately crumbles under such a weight.

But the weight of what? What appeared that invisibly rushed at me with all its force? There was only one person here, barely one hundred and eighty centimeters tall, maybe not even that much, ninety kilos at the most. It doesn't seem that hard. It was true that there was an aura of astonishment that I couldn't describe what it actually was, I just felt, with every square centimeter of my being, that it was already something, that it was already able to weigh on a person. But maybe it wasn't even justified by the fact that I had to resist him with all my strength. And that sound, yes, I can't take it lightly either, because even sitting in front of it is a very taxing, almost sweaty challenge, and the smell, the smell, the silence of shock, all of this is a weight that is placed on that body, attached as ballast and producing the heavy newtons, then the five hundred kilos of the suits, and the invisible pride of power, which I should be able to resist, and already I knew that's why he came, to show himself exactly, to let me know how damn hard it was all going to be, which as much as I wanted to imagine, I never knew.

I looked at Anton. Into Anton's sky-blue eyes in which a glinting tear loomed, and he was afraid or rather feared for me, but his mouth nevertheless smiled excitedly because he sensed the uniqueness of the situation, long before I did, and I had to realize that this tear is not only of fear, but this idiot is now unconcealably proud, because the country's greatest power dreads his love like a little child who knows they were caught, and there is no way back from punishment.

Mortenstein never testified. He would have been a fool. Every unspoken statement was waiting for us to prove its truth ourselves. He was not so forthcoming as to respond in detail to the allegations. He fought against us with much more sophisticated means. The TV proclamations that he addressed to the country were not addressed to us, they were merely political tricks. He did not respect the prosecutor's office by allowing us to ask him anything. He had every right to do so. As a defense attorney, I would have advised him to do the same, but regardless, I thought this strategy was petty and pathetic.

The president spoke when and to whom he wanted. I accepted it from the beginning, I knew it would be like this, and it was good because I never had to look him in the eye. To me, for years Mortenstein was just a celebrity who occasionally appeared on certain channels, and whom I tried to enclose in the plastic house of the TV, like some evil genie. It was good just to think about him, it was good to ignore him, it was good to hide from him, but he still felt that he could influence me even if he saw me, if we faced each other, that it was necessary for him to meet me, because I am the one who has his fate in his hands, I lead the investigation, and I alone can put him in prison. He had to be confused.
"You are terrible" I said. "Can't you see the situation? Take what Mortenstein did as a threat, don't be proud of me."
"It's hard for me to separate the two" answered Anton. "Fear. From you. How could you not like that idea?"
"We already knew that he was afraid of me, how is this different?"
"That he went so far as to tell you in person. He packs it in intimidation, but he can't succeed."
"And do you like this?" I inquired provocatively.
"That Mortenstein is afraid?" he smiled. "Very!"
"I told you you were terrible" I caressed Anton's arm and left my hand on it.
I could feel the hair on his arms stand up and he was thinking about something ridiculously superficial. He smiled tellingly.
"If I didn't know you, I'd be in trouble" I poked my head towards the bathroom with that. "Come on then!" I ordered him.
Fortunately, there weren't many people, it was already lunchtime, and the place was empty. I took Anton's hand and led him to the toilet.
"By the way, everything was recently renovated" I noted. "We don't have to worry about hygiene either."

Although the men's and women's sections were opened from two separate doors, we boldly entered with the sign "gentlemen" and pushed our way into one of the cabins, now kissing. It wasn't built up to the ceiling. Anyone could have heard anything.

We've always talked about it. After the bathroom cabin incident, I knew that it would happen one day, even if it is very difficult to foresee it, after all, when do people get into such a mood in a restaurant that they fall for each other right then and there? But now that Mortenstein had set up and stirred up the stagnant water, he had created a special occasion, and this was the one of many occasions when it finally happened.

Anton lifted my top in a few seconds and kissed my neck. I felt that we were attuned to each other very quickly. I unbuttoned his pants, then pushed them down below his knees, and finally he did the same with mine. We stood there facing each other with our pants down and I laughed to myself.
"Like some messed up western" I noted. "Who shoots first?"

He shot. He grabbed my arms tightly and held them together above my head. He turned me around on my axis, then propped my hands against the wall and tilted me forward. My bottom was almost exposed to him, and I imagined he was mesmerized by the mysterious sight of my labia. Either way, I could feel him start caressing them with his fingers, finally breaking in between them and trying to get them wet, making it easy from there. Even though every minute was stolen, he let us both enjoy the moment. I loved the way he touched me, I loved the way he caressed me, and I screamed at the caress. He finally saw the opportunity to do the same with his manhood and slid into me like a skate. He made a few strong movements, then hurriedly pulled himself out of me, I felt the sticky thick juice start to trickle over my bottom.
"The fuck..." he hissed.
"It's okay, my love" I said soothingly. "It's okay, we're not done yet, don't worry, just wipe it off my ass please."
He cleaned it carefully. I turned around, seeing shame and disappointment in his eyes.

"You know I like it when that happens, right?" I asked him because I really liked him. I loved the whole lead-up, and the fast finish didn't bother me either, I thought it was just because he couldn't resist me.
"I'm sorry," he whispered.

"Pay attention!" I looked at him a little. "Sit down here" I pushed him onto the seat, while pulling up my pants and buttoning my top. "I'll be right back, don't worry!" then I leaned over and gently breathed into his ear. "When I came back, you gave me such a kiss as if you were in high school again" I finally swirled my tongue around in his mouth.
Anton went to a boys' high school, but that's obviously not what I meant by my promise. He swore that they never did anything like that there, and I believed him.
I went to the counter to ask for a Martini.
"My friend is not feeling well" I said as my cocktail was made.
"Was the president here?" the bartender asked back.
I didn't know the guy's name, but lately we came here so much that we definitely knew each other by sight.
"Yes, he is" I answered. "I'm Amanda Evans" I introduced myself. "I'm the prosecutor in the case against the president."
"I know who you are" he answered. "I saw you on TV. I don't think there's a person in Love City who doesn't know you. I'm Frank."
"So you know me" I was excited. It was still pretty cool when someone knew who I was without me telling them. In this case, of course I told you, but...
"And why was the president here?"
"I'd like to know that too, but I don't."
"Are you okay with it?" asked the perfectly logical question.
"Jesus, no" I rejected the assumption. "In fact, maybe he shouldn't even be here. But only he can tell why he came."
"Did you want to negotiate?" he asked further.
"You know, I can't really answer questions about the case..."

"Sorry!" he blurted out, almost scared. "You're right, I have nothing to do with it, it's just that there's so much talk about it lately that you feel like you're a part of it. My girlfriend is a big fan of yours. He's studying law, he talks about you all the time."
"This is very honourable" I answered. "I didn't think I had fans" I laughed. "If it really is, we can send him a picture."
After successfully riding the waves of celebrity, I downed my drink as if it were just a shot.
"If you think, I can lock the men's washroom until you finish," he offered.
"No need, thanks!" I smiled to myself. "It's more exciting that way."
Because of the situation, I happily slipped a ten on the counter.
"Thanks Frank! This has become my new home."
When I got back, Anton was still reading his phone, but I immediately took it out of his hands.
"This will not be necessary now" I stated firmly and stepped on the gas.

After the previous one, the little soldier has not yet fully recovered. I wasn't worried about that. I switched places with Anton and found myself in an almost too comfortable position. He stood in front of me as if he had come for some examination.
"Now let's see what's wrong..," I flirted with him and blew a kiss on the little patient.

It was like a set table. I didn't even understand why we hadn't been in such a position at home, when it was so obvious. Anton was stroking my hair and I thought maybe he wanted to see how I did it. Life began to return to him. He was already standing in front of me before I could touch him with my hand, and I took great pleasure in handling his tool staring at me. I felt more and more like I was at a fair, where people are being lured with a huge lollipop. I could not and did not want to resist the temptation. I took his hard hat between my lips and lingered with him for a few minutes. I passionately licked his rock-hard, swollen glans, then took it all deep into my mouth, and then covered it with my tongue and licked it all the way in the middle of a powerful suction. He crawled over my neck with his hand, but he didn't want to dictate the pace, he just enjoyed the situation. He hiccupped as he breathed, and as usual let me know how much he adored me with long low moans. I continued to pamper her with pleasure, adding an increased tempo to the big show, while I grabbed her bottom with my hands, perhaps taking my oral promise too strictly. Of course, why would she have complained about this, when I gave her a pampering with my tongue that she could only dream of before me, and has been dreaming of since the first time I played with her. My kisses were strong, in my fantasy I sucked the juice from him almost to the surface, and I could see in his closed eyes how he could feel the magma gathering every centimeter that wanted to erupt. I moved steadily forward with my rhythmic movements. This is what it looks like in reality when someone is being led by the tail. I was an excellent guide on the way to beauty. I couldn't decide what I wanted more, to bring him to the top like this and show him that I was the reason for all his haste, or to hand him the reins and accept the cocked member inside me. Fortunately, before I could engage in philosophical debates with myself, he sensed what I was thinking and grabbed my shoes in a moment to pull everything that could be in his way down to my hot throbbing crotch with a few quick movements. He took it upon himself and pushed him further towards deeper pleasures at the pace he dictated.
"You are an animal" I whispered to him, during my moans, but he didn't answer, he just emphasized with his actions: he understands exactly what I mean.
Suddenly I noticed the opening of the toilet door.
"Don't stop!" I shouted, chasing away the person who was trying to come in, while Anton didn't bother himself at all, he continued to do as he should.

The door slammed shut, and the person did well to step down. There was also a room for women for the traditional things, she didn't have to disturb us with her lower body needs, but I couldn't think much about it, because Anton pushed all other thoughts out of my head. He was flying faster and faster in his lap, I slowly felt like the whole stall was falling apart, while we were all the more tense to each other, and I knew it would be best for both of us if he didn't pull himself out of me now. I wanted to feel him enjoying me. I gently leaned into his ear to whisper:
"Don't ask anything, just do it please..."

I didn't have to wait long. I felt all the life being torn out of her during a long moan, which I wanted right then and there to reach its goal and fulfill the role that was assigned to her from the moment of her creation, because I was seized by the irresistible desire to want a child from her and a family, and I felt ready to change the world we live in. The erupting lava covered everything inside me like a hot volcano, then I sat tightly in Anton's lap for minutes, all of us locked together, as if we never wanted to let go of the other.

chapters.title: Chapter Thirty
paragraphs: Anton was preparing the morning bread in the kitchen, when angry and disappointed indignity was heard from the toilet.
"How the hell would life screw this shit up!"
"what's wrong" he asked, as if the same curse hadn't burst from my throat at indeterminate intervals.
"What would it be? When would it happen if not now? This unlucky one came, just today, that he had to come here just today."
We both listened. I heard how Anton searched among the medicines and pulled out a painkiller, but my stomach didn't even hurt, I just fiddled with the tampon packaging when he opened it.
"Tell me, are you normal?" I looked at him. "Don't you think this can wait another five minutes?"
"It's true, I'm stupid" he stated and backed out of the door with a berry and a glass of water in hand.
As soon as I pulled myself together, I wanted to lash out, but it was much better to turn behind Anton and hug his waist from behind. He was just standing at the stove turning the loaves.
"It hurt that he came" I started to explain. "I was hoping that once we got down to it, it would come together right away."
"How much medicine and energy goes into the trash" ironized. "If we knew in advance that not every love affair would result in a child, we could have really taken the matter more lightly."
"Although it would be possible to know which one it will be from" I answered. "I really enjoy trying though, let's not stop..."
"I don't think you should worry about that," he answered and kissed the crown of my head.
Since the stunt in the restaurant, we've made love even more than before, and at the same time, Anton became so attentive that he sometimes started to get on my mind.
"I will help!" started. "don't lift too much!"
"Anthony!" I said. "Even if I had something in me, it would be the size of a pea, what do you think will happen if I touch a pot, a vacuum cleaner or a washing machine?"
"I know, I know," he replied usually.
"Then don't annoy me!"

But there were also occasions when his kindness was especially good. He regularly sent me home with small gifts. One time she brought flowers, another time Scrub Mommy, another time again; chocolates. The love burning in his eyes filled me with a very good feeling, which only made my desire to give him a baby even stronger. Anton didn't change, he just paid more attention to me, because that's what it brings out of a person when he and his love decide to start a family.

What I liked most about him was how much he immersed himself in his role. I knew that no matter what, he would take care of us, and to the mom-to-be in me, that made him extremely manly. We talked many times before about what will happen to us when we have a family, what will happen to us if for some reason we don't have one. At the time, I wasn't sure what the future would bring, because I didn't dare to know. All my life, Anton was the only man I could trust, even though I could never accept the peace of certainty even with him, not even after he repeatedly risked his life for me. The future is distant and the threads from which it was woven are so inextricable, I always thought.
"And if it doesn't work out?" I brought up.
"Then we go to Disneyland alone" he answered. "With you, I'll always have a princess to take there."
"You're drooling, but good" I left it to him.

Anton always played down unpleasant thoughts. It didn't fit in his life to deal with inconveniences, although I would have liked him to say something more soothing. Of course, it could easily be that he didn't mean it as a joke, but this solution was so far from the real problems that I couldn't take it seriously. Now I had to let it go, I simply didn't have time to deal with my fears about starting a family, the trial of the Mortenstein case started, and I had to go through so many documents that a week wouldn't have been enough.
"Where is Hermione's testimony?" Anton asked.
After a short search, I showed him the bundle of witness testimonies.
"Where is the expert report on Jeff's BMW?"
I immediately pointed to another block.
"And where are the materials on Jake?"
"Next to the desk to the right" I answered.
"So what?" he asked. "You know everything, you know everything, what are you nervous about?"
I hated it when someone tried to tell me if I could be nervous. If I wanted to, I was nervous, if I didn't want to, I wasn't. But none of them followed orders.

"I don't know what you planned for today, Anton, but I will deal with these until 23:59, so whatever you feel like doing today or have felt like in recent years, go ahead! I swear to you, I don't even care if you go to a strip club."
"At half past eight in the morning?"
"Sorry Mr. Maybe also write to Google when they are open."
"I picked you up there, honey, you danced there."
"Mhmm... Wow!" I threw him out of the apartment in a few minutes.
(Anton Martin)

Amy has been working a lot this past month. He carried home by the kilo the materials related to the case, filling almost the entire living room with them. I was also surprised, because in the last two weeks he even left the usual weekly cleanings, which I know required a lot of mental strength on his part, but he had to realize that he won't be able to read everything intensively if he is killing his time with cleaning things. He finally gave up when, in the last case, he packed so much that it took him three days to put everything back where he found it logical. He preferred not to experiment more.
"Good, good! Then it will never be cleaned again!" he argued with himself.

I headed downtown. It was hideously hot. I didn't want to go by car, I felt that if I was already out of the house, I would rather choose public transport, it promised to be more exciting. My plan was to sit on the beach and people watch. I wanted to finish a book, for which I needed a couple of characters from the street, and the squares were swarming with people like ants on the hot summer asphalt sometime back when I was a child. In common sense, I bought a bottle of cola and a bag of peanuts. I could feel my brain slowly turning over. Even though I pulled myself up on a shadier bench, the air burned like an oven. I couldn't pay attention to anything else.

I wanted cool. I was afraid to ask myself the questions about global warming. I've gotten comfortable in recent years. I knew I did it too, so I couldn't enjoy a damn day in the middle of July anymore, and I was angry with myself for it. Of course, also for politicians and public space planners, who want to solve every call for tenders with indecent paving and baby-sized trees. It wasn't good to sit there. I thought of the library, where I was sure that I would be able to spend some of my time pleasantly, the rest we will see.

There, however, I could not resist the inviting aroma of the cafe. When I arrived, the wind of redemption hit me, my body suddenly cooled down from the pleasant 25 degrees that were shot into the building through high-performance nozzles. I bought melange and cream. It was the most commercial menu I could choose from, but I wanted to be sure. I was disappointed in both the bench and the river bank, unfortunately I couldn't fit several side dishes. Two older women were talking at the table next to me. Lately, if we turned around somewhere, after a while it became apparent that people often talked about the president and the ongoing investigation against him, several people would recognize Amy, sometimes me too, but luckily the latter happened rarely. Slowly, I instinctively paid attention to what they were talking about at the tables. I became a gossip, or I just liked to interrogate others, I don't know, but now these two ladies were telling each other about the grandchildren, and as they showed the pictures, an uneasiness spread inside me. It was as if he was being addressed by another image among the many millions held captive in the library. Did he really need that? Did the boasting of two grandmothers unleash some secret that wanted to find me? Which image did you want to show me? Who did I want to see something about that I had never seen before?

I ran my fork through the cream. The foamy cream slowly split in two. I had already stuffed the bottom and the top into me, while the warm coffee flowed pleasantly down my throat. I played on the plate. I added more and more pieces of cake to my mouth in small bites, until finally only a few crumbs remained. I folded up the remaining melange and headed in the direction of magazines and newspapers. I could feel the trail heating up.

I didn't know what I was looking for, I didn't know where to look and when. The year 2008 came to mind almost imperceptibly. But what happened then, I asked myself. Olympiad? But what would I want from the Beijing Olympics? Choice?! Yes, it will be, although I would rather forget it than remember it. I calmed down. Then this could be it, this is what wanted to disturb my peaceful coffee. But what should I research? Just grab a couple of newspapers and we'll be fine? Like a man possessed, I found myself slurring words out of my mouth. I looked around, but no one paid attention to me, according to them they didn't see me or didn't want to see me. I went to a machine and placed an order from the first five daily newspapers that suddenly came to my mind, numbers dated around the election. I'm an idiot. I felt clean. I can't be normal. There are silent ghosts whispering in my brain that won't let me go until they get what they want.

I was looking through a Formula 1 magazine before the requested pages arrived. I have always held librarians in high esteem, no matter where they work in the institution. I knew nothing about the background of the interrogations. I haven't been on a library tour yet, but I would be very interested. I imagined that the old issues were lined up in huge warehouses, where you could only enter with a permit. I don't know why, maybe so that no one can mix them up, so that in the end a fool like me can no longer see into the past. Then I thought of online newspapers. What if all I had to do was type the date into the search engine and it would immediately output the archived articles? No, no, I pushed the thought away, I did the right thing by asking them out. I wanted to trust the print media today. What was printed on paper cannot be lost, I deluded myself. Or maybe it was just because of the atmosphere of the library that I didn't think of researching online?

With the newspapers in hand, I looked for a separate table for myself, where I could comfortably flip through them while sitting in an armchair. First I reviewed the Daily City. "The election ended with the victory of Edgar Mortenstein! The previous government lies in ruins..." the newspaper wrote, and it was not biased in the slightest. The previous set failed miserably, and even then the flames of hope flickered in me. I was amazed at how much younger Mortenstein looked. It's as if it's not him, just someone who sounds like him, but that's exactly the point of the pictures. They lock in a date that will never come again. There was also a group photo. They celebrated the victory, who stood alone or with their families on the podium in front of an amorphous crowd. I took out another sheet. Love City News: "Mortenstein went big yesterday, taking two-thirds of the constituencies!" That got me excited. We had a lot to thank for that. The picture showed the people on stage from a similar angle, in a slightly different division. Mortenstein was preparing to speak. Then the third and fourth, and finally the last newspaper showed the moment in a similar way. Nobody wanted to stand out. They wanted to illustrate the great success with pictures of the celebration. An overwhelming excitement ran through me. I didn't know what I hadn't noticed yet, but I already knew that what was calling from within was in front of me. I put the pictures next to each other. Everything about them really looked almost the same. I tried to recognize those on it. The third row could hardly be seen, the second just as well. In the front row, however, everyone looked good. Mortenstein, the woman who now replaces him, and something always happens when her name is mentioned, the finance minister, the interior guy, party functionaries and some kids. Among them is a curly-haired, barely 12-year-old guy in the front row. Then in the second. Finally, he is seen from the side just leaving the podium. There were other family members. For example, the vice president held his little daughter in his arms, and the insider guy shook the hand of one of his teenage sons. The communications director smiled between his two daughters and all three applauded. It was almost as if the next moment, like some actor, he would raise their arms in the air to bow with them. But where did the other child go? Why did he leave and how did he get there? Who did it belong to? Didn't he find anyone to be happy with? No one was after his hand? Who was he who spoke to me from the pages of the newspapers during creaming? I took out my phone. I looked up some archive articles. I also found earlier moments of the same image. In these, Mortenstein was not yet standing at the pulpit, but was further back with a curly-haired boy by his side.

chapters.title: Chapter Thirty-One
paragraphs: Who could that boy be? I asked myself this morning while lying in bed. Amy was already getting ready, she got up at six, even though the trial didn't start until noon. He obviously still had a lot to do. I didn't dare say anything to him. I didn't want to mess it up. His focus was completely elsewhere, and this nothingness would only have served to distract him from his precisely planned agenda.
"How old was Jess?" I asked Amy.
"Doesn't it matter?"
"It doesn't matter" I answered.
"It was 27" he answered briefly. "He was born in 95. What is this for you?"
"I just wanted to know how many years older you were."
"You already knew, but with two" Amy replied. "It really doesn't seem like much, does it?"
"The two years or the 27?"
"None of them."
"Because none of them are many" I established.
"Jessica Marsh lived 27 years" he said, as the minister had said at his funeral, and then he was silent.
"Sorry, I didn't mean to disturb you" I spoke again. "Get ready, I was just asking."
"I don't think it would be a problem if you reminded me how little Jess lived. Damn little. He lived a very short life. It shouldn't have taken that much..."

The courtroom is full of people. I dressed elegantly, I wanted anyone who recognized me to see that Amy was getting all the support I could. He wore a dark blue suit. With perfect elegance, with matching high heels. Her blonde hair fell like gold on her shoulders, she even went to the nail salon at 8 in the morning. He stuck a small judge's gavel on his ring finger, as if he was betrothed to justice. He choreographed everything he could think of. I really liked this care, it represented well how he prepared for this trial. I was proud of him.

Mortenstein wasn't in the room yet, but I knew he might arrive at any moment. I was waiting for the press staff to get excited when he arrived. Amy stood up and read the indictment she had written months earlier. I knew he knew it from the outside. He said it 99X without paper, and in the end he didn't make a single mistake in it. There was no need for this, yet he wanted it that way. I thought I discovered Athena Wright in the gallery. He liked Amy and I knew how much he wanted the president behind bars because of her.

The journalists started clicking wildly with their cameras. The president appeared in the hall like a celebrated celebrity, with bodyguards following him, a true police escort. Although Mortenstein was a defendant, there was no outward indication that he was under arrest. It wasn't. He defended himself on the loose, he only had to keep certain limits. Anno showed up at the restaurant illegally, but the attorney general did everything he could to make Mortenstein feel as comfortable as possible amid the charges. That said, there was no danger involved. If he had run away, he would have confessed his guilt, anyway, he was too big a man to be able to hide. Whatever he believed about the truth, he thought he would swim, and I was repulsed by this arrogance.

Amy looked behind her in confusion. During Mortenstein's entrance, he was looking at him the whole time, he wanted to make him uncertain, but I knew that all his efforts were unnecessary. Amy was long past doubting herself. The discrediting in the media only made it stronger.

Along with Mortenstein, Jake also arrived, although he was followed by nurses instead of bodyguards. Too comfortable for him, I thought. He will swim. It was hard for me to see him as Jeff, even if Jess and Amy probably wanted him that way.
Their lawyers were like two peas in a pod.
After a few minutes, the judge finally appeared and the room rose to welcome him.
"Dear Court!" spoke up...
And with this it began...

The judge must have been around sixty years old. Amy had told me about him before, but I hadn't memorized his name, and of course I'd never seen him before. He was considered a professional authority among judges, taught at university, wrote many books, and did not seem like someone who could be influenced by power. I looked at this as if Amy was born under a lucky constellation after all. There was nothing unusual about that. That's how things should work. The independence of the court is fundamental, and only conspirators should have concerns about it. Of course, there are exceptions everywhere, and in the last decade it was easy to believe that Mortenstein had succeeded in dismantling the system of checks and balances, but the reality seemed different, and I honestly wanted to believe that.

Now it was Amy who made the accusation. The water broke, I was overwhelmed with heat, I was sweating under my jacket. It was cool in the room, but I would have felt the same way in a Finnish ice bath. He said things like: forced labor, human trafficking, child prostitution, and I thought how could this even happen? How could these things happen to people in a country that outwardly radiates the appearance of perfection, a country where perfection has become an export, and I was disgusted by it. What kind of world is it where this is happening right in front of our eyes, yet we don't know about it, or even worse, we don't even want to be aware of it.

I remembered how Amy and I met. My editor said he would introduce me to the director, because he is a big and serious man and has a lot of experience in publishing books. In any case, they wanted to publish my novel, which at first I thought was the level I could take on in front of the public. They thought it would be an exciting, good crime story, and it was, and in their great happiness that I had chosen them, they said that the business should be celebrated in a place that was worthy of the career I was about to pursue. They trusted me, recognized the possibility of success, but they didn't know me well enough. Neon Nights, they thought, was just right for the celebration. The director has been there several times. I've never been to a similar club before, I laughed at him, but they said they'd never seen a crime writer who was so defenseless while writing about murders, criminals and sex. I had to partially prove them right. If nothing else, it will be good for gaining experience, I thought, because in life we ​​have to experience things that we don't want, but we have to know about them so that we don't live in ignorance.

I was blown away by Neon Nights. I couldn't fathom that what I was seeing really existed. I was surrounded by a mixture of naked women and repulsive men who looked vulnerable, while the smell of alcohol and drugs mingled with each other. Even though the equipment was impeccably clean, I still felt that the whole place was swimming in filth. Such is life, I thought, such is the life that many people fantasize about, that the powerful dream about. There are no compromises in this world.

Unsettling thoughts swirled in me, but I still didn't know what the real face of this place was. I didn't know that the girls who sit here stripped naked on the lap of a member of the club in a baggy suit, are not driven by calculating games, but by survival, and that the club mocked as a paradise of pleasure is actually a prison, a graveyard for the souls of young girls and women. But how did I know this later? Why did I return to this place a hundred times, when I was dizzy from the first moment?

Because a blonde girl suddenly appeared on the stage, whose body moved like a wave on the surface, and I saw in her that, despite the sumptuous spectacle, deep down there were a million unspoken words, and I wanted my ears to hear them. He noticed something about this, because after the dance he came up to me and asked what I was doing here, and in my confusion I could only tell him: You. Then he laughed, sat down next to me and started talking.

Mortenstein looked at Amy with a calm smile during the plea. Sensing this, he tried to avoid the president's gaze. I don't think she was afraid of him, she just didn't want to deal with Mortenstein sitting there.

The other defendant, Jake, appeared more tense. When Amy talked about Jess' death and accusing Jake of murder, Jeff's eyes welled up with tears. He was honestly afraid that Jake had actually done it, but he was afraid to admit it. Jake was nowhere to be found, and Jeff had to endure the demanding atmosphere of the trial alone. Every minute was killing him.

Although the crimes against the girls were not committed only by the president, in addition to the above, no other accused was present in the room. As much as Amy wanted to go after her captors, she trusted Jessica's decision and didn't bother with the contents of the locked folders. He knew he could take them to court later, but for now he had to deal with Mortenstein, who couldn't hide from him because of his power, and Jake was held captive by his feelings for Jessica.

After Amy's speech, the judge wanted to question Mortenstein, but he declined the opportunity. He could think what he wanted, he had already said it, and in the trial he would do better to sit back and listen. He had a good lawyer, he could trust him, even his breath stopped when he spoke.

"Dear court!" he started. "I am requesting the immediate removal of Prosecutor Amanda Evans as we have evidence that she is the same Alice Bucks who failed Neon Nights training. I think this exhausts the concept of conflict of interest, as it can be driven by a desire for revenge against the president."
The courtroom was noticeably disturbed by the defense attorney's request. However, I knew they were on the wrong side of things because Amy had proof on her side.
"How can you justify this statement?" the judge asked back. "The lawsuit was withdrawn a month ago."
"Since then, new evidence has come into our possession" answered the lawyer.
"And when did this take place?"
"Yesterday, Your Honor" he answered.
"Well, then they would have had a day to administer it."
After that, there was silence in the room again.

"No problem, judge" Amy spoke up. We have the death certificate of Alice Bucks, which we did not make available only to the court for the reason that this assumption was previously withdrawn by the defense.
"So do you have proof that you can't be Alice Bucks?" asked the judge.
"Yes" Amy replied. "the expert examined it and found the document authentic."

"So you're not Alice Bucks" the judge stated. "Unless, of course, we assume that Alice Bucks rose from the dead, graduated from law school, and then applied to the DA under a different name to get revenge on the president."
There was a general cheerfulness in the room, a smile even appeared on Mortenstein's face.
"Of course, I reject this possibility" the judge declared. "As for the trial, if no further objections are made against the persons present, I recommend that we continue. I reject the request."

chapters.title: Chapter Thirty-Two
paragraphs: (Amanda Evans)

I don't know what the Mortensteins were thinking bringing this thing up again. That was the most likely thing to do, but that's why I made sure to have Alice's death certificate well in advance. I was surprised that they only got something about it yesterday, although I had no idea what it might have been. I expected that if it turned out that way, it would definitely be brought up at another, more suitable time, but until then I was more interested in other things.

The hearing of the witnesses followed. I built the strategy well. First I wanted to prove Mortenstein's guilt, then that Jake killed Jessica, and finally that the two things were connected, namely because Jess' death happened because of the start of the case against the president. We were pretty good with the first and last, but I didn't have direct evidence against Jake yet.

Hermione was the first to be ushered into the meeting room. He seemed quite confident. Her beautiful long brown hair reached down to her waist. He was dressed elegantly, and even the women were giving him approving glances, as far as I could tell from the front of the room. Provocatively, he pointed his white cane at the witness stand, signaling Mortenstein, who knew exactly how he had gone blind. Then they swore that he would only tell the truth.
"Did you know Jessica Marsh?" I asked him.
"Yes, I knew him" he answered. "I was very good with it."
"And where did you get to know each other?"
"In Neon Nights."
"And do you know Edgar Mortenstein too?"
"Yes," she replied.
"And from where?"
"I was a prostitute for eight years in the club he created, the name of which I mentioned earlier."
"Could you tell us from when to when you were there?"
"From 2009 to 2016" Hermione replied.

"We've all heard of this club" I continued. "However, according to some, this club was nothing more than an exclusive modeling school, where the children of wealthy families were taught and started on the path to a career in fashion."
"I don't know who the ones claiming this are," she said, "but what I knew as the Neon Nights had nothing to do with fashion."
"Then what did it have to do with?" I asked back.
"As I said, I worked as a prostitute, and countless other girls..."
"I understand" I acknowledged Hermione's answer. "Please tell me more about what life was like at Neon Nights."

"Before I report on this, I would like to say a few words about who I am, that is, who I was before Neon Nights" Hermione didn't know who she was asking, but she instinctively looked at the judge, who she considered the most authority in the room.
"We are listening," the judge reassured her.

"I come from a poor southern family," she began. "My father died early; my mother raised me and my other three brothers with difficulty. I think this is important to tell because I want you to understand how I could get there. When I turned 16, a recruiter found me and made an offer for a server job in a club that had recently opened then. My mother wasn't happy with the idea, but we were so badly off financially that I had to accept it. In the first months, thus, I was a bartender in a completely ordinary-looking bar of the Neon Nights that was separated from everything. The work didn't seem hard, yet it was relatively tiring for me, since I had never worked before. I felt, however, that I would soon get into it. I was very happy when I got my first salary and could give it to my family. My mother gave a part of it back to me so that I would better feel the fruit of my work and not lose my spirit. She was very grateful for the help; I will never forget her face when I gave her the first 300 dollars. The payouts were weekly, and I felt I made a very good decision to start working."
"And when did things change?" I asked Hermione.

"About two months later, I was called into the office once, and they told me they are very satisfied with my work and receive much praise from the guests too, then asked if I don't want to make even more money. I asked what they mean. They said that among the guests there are those who are lonely and desire company, and since I receive much praise regarding my appearance, it would be obvious if in exchange for extra income I didn't skip this opportunity. I had a bad feeling about this, but I thought the more money I can make, the easier it will be later to move with the family to some better neighborhood, and with my bartender experience I could even find a place in a more serious restaurant. We didn't really like that district; there was barely any work opportunity, and the boys were slowly growing up."
"Did they tell you what you would have to do?"

"Actually, they beat around the bush. They didn't say concretely. They asked me to talk with whoever they call me to, but I didn't really understand what I could talk about with older people. I wasn't really interested in their world."
"And what happened after that?" I went on questioning.

"With the first client, the trick was revealed immediately. I had to go into a separate room where a very attractive man waited. I was sixteen; before that I had only had to do with young guys; I hadn't slept with anyone. Then barely a minute went by, this man already placed his hand on my face and wanted to kiss me. I resisted, whereupon one of the bosses called me out and asked me to allow what the man wants; unfortunately some misunderstanding happened, and the one who should have come to him is busy now, but the person is very rich and will pay me, don't worry, they are watching over me. They also said that I shouldn't think I have to sleep with him; I reassured them that I won't. They laughed at this, but indeed I didn't sleep with that man."
"What did you have to do?"
"Everything else" Hermione replied. "In fact, I was forced to do everything other than what he asked."

"And what did that man ask of you?" I questioned him because I knew that now would be the time to come up with the specifics. Hermione wasn't scared by the question, she had a lot of experience with similar things, she continued talking with an unflappable face.
"I had to suck it off twice" summed it up briefly.
"And did the man do something to you?"

"Since I was still a virgin, they wouldn't let him touch me down there, as I found out because there was a lot more money in taking my virginity. We live in a sick world..., what did that mean in that situation? Obviously, they were not thinking about protecting my virginity, but that they would be able to satisfy another fetish with it. They were all animals, so they didn't care if I was still a virgin."
"So that person didn't touch you?"
"I didn't say that. He drew such circles on my breasts that they were purple for a week. When I went home, I was afraid that my mother would notice."
"So they were allowed home after that?" I asked in a somewhat surprised voice.
"It was the last time I could go home, yes."
The room was once again silent.
"What happened?" I asked again, but I could see in Hermione that she was getting over it.

"When we finished, they begged me not to say anything at home. I was ashamed too, and feared my mother would beat me, as well as that the money tap would dry up. It was a misunderstanding, I reassured myself, and they pressed a thousand dollars into my hand, which surely wasn't left there by that man, so I felt they really wanted to compensate me. Then the next day they didn't let me go home anymore, and the outside world darkened on me forever."
"Did you still have to work in the bar after this?"
"Not where before. However, in the inner club room countless times, regularly even naked."
"How did you lose your sight?"
"I got hit in the head with a wine bottle, but that's in my confession" he answered briefly.
"And do you blame the Neon Nights for this happening so?" I asked.
"I don't know much else. If I hadn't been forced to tease that bloated dick, the miniature dick, maybe he wouldn't care if I didn't suffocate when he stuck his cock in my mouth."
"Please put it more appropriately" interjected the judge. "Try to stick to the facts and answer what the prosecutor asked."

"Sorry, Judge, I meant your penis" I laughed at myself. "But I don't want to waste their time, he continued. I said countless things in my testimony, and I could probably list for years the many vile things that were committed against us at Neon Nights, but I feel it is unnecessary. It is also clear from this that I gained real experiences that I will never forget and that decisively influenced my later life. So I suggest we move on."
