The room's audience received Hermione's words with restrained laughter, but the judge was a more serious man than this.
"Don't worry miss" reassured him. "We stay as long as we need to stay to see things clearly. Miss Evans, please continue to question the witness."
"Well, you can really find a lot in his testimony" i started. "but I still want to ask you some questions in public. Have similar cases often occurred?"
"What is the prosecutor thinking? How did they beat us?"
"For example."

"Yes" snapped Hermione. They beat me, and not only those we were entrusted with taking care of. You probably don't know how many fetishes there are that people normally can't satisfy and think that if they pay someone for it, they will do anything for them, especially when it comes to girls like me back then.
"What kind of girl was she then?"
"Whom the dog is not looking for."
Hermione spoke from my heart. It was good to hear from him what happened to me. I looked at Anton, who already knew everything about me, but now he was ashamed that he had to hear all this.
"Did a fatality occur too?" I asked Hermione again.
"Yes" he answered. Katherine Mayers was killed.
"When?"
"It must have been around 2014, I think."
"And how did Katherine die?"
"He was shot in the head by his friend. Then days later, a gentleman was also killed."
"For revenge?"
"I wouldn't put it that way. Rather, with an educational purpose."
"Were you often brought up with such methods in Neon Nights?" I asked.
"Not" Hermione replied firmly. "It was just in the air that tempers would be unleashed between the gentlemen."
The judge growled once.
"Did gentlemen and girls often develop romantic relationships?"
"Don't call it love" Hermione clarified. "Let's call it a jealous relationship, and to answer the question; not. All kinds of acts between the gentlemen and the inmates were forbidden."
"According to them, there were exceptions" I interrupted.
"There are exceptions everywhere" Mary qualified my generalization.
"Did management cover up these murders?"
"There was a policeman outside who I saw several times. He was obviously a greased cop. I think he took care of what needed to be done, and then the matter was closed."
"What was his name?"
"I don't know that. I never spoke to him, and they obviously didn't tell us anything about who their built-in man was at the yard."
I took a photo of Ross Wilson from the folders on the table and showed it around the room.
"According to the indictment, this man is the one Mary Morales is talking about. Obviously, it would be better if you could identify it, but there will be someone else who will do it for you."
"If she has deep-set brown eyes, balding brown hair, and cauliflower ears, she's the one" Mary interjected and accurately described the face in the picture.
"This will do for half an identification" interjected the judge, almost with amusement in his voice. "Do we know anything else about it?" he inquired.

"He was found dead at the table in his hotel room a few months ago. Shot in the head. His identity will be even more important to us because they found the number of the assassin who attacked Anton Martin in our home. But now let's talk about Neon Nights" I turned to Hermione. "Mary would be interested in how everyday was at the club."
"It's best to think of it as a strict college. Only here, instead of going to school, you go to have sex, you train yourself in this and that."
"What is meant by this?"
"Let's say we learned to dance, mix cocktails, have sex."
"To have sex?" I asked back, because it might seem strange to others.

"In order to understand this, think about how limited our consciousness was at that time. Everyone was as alone as a finger, but at the same time there were thirty or forty other girls in the same shoes as him. We knew exactly who could feel what, what state they might be in, what they were going through. I was there almost from the beginning, but new girls kept coming. Some were fourteen years old when they got there, some were sixteen. There were always those who had experienced what others had experienced before, so the love that was completely absent from this cruel organization still reared its head among the girls. It was pointless to compete with each other. No one could get further than the other. The only chance was to help each other. Over time, we held sessions that made it easier to survive the humiliation we experienced every day. We taught the younger ones what and how to do, how not to hate themselves, and when it was necessary, we were kind to each other. It may seem strange, but it was so."
"Was there no other joy in their lives?" I asked.

"They took us to the mall once a month to buy clothes for ourselves. I remember many people staring at me because we walked through the best shops and the average people envied us. Perhaps many people thought that we were doing it of our own free will, out of a desire for money, because it showed who we were. Yet who would have known that it was only an appearance?"
"But if you were detained, weren't you afraid that they would run away while shopping? Or because they talk themselves out of it?"

"This occasion was a gift. In the beginning, someone else tried to step down, but what can a girl do against an entire organization? Two people escaped and went to the police, we never heard from them again. They were passed on. I loved life, even when it was all so shitty. That's my mentality. Of course, several people wanted to commit suicide, but they were dissuaded from it. We survived."
"Has anyone been fired?"
"What do you mean fired?"
"I mean, if someone didn't work to the expected standard, they were sent away?"

"There were people who avoided me from Neon Nights, yes, but I don't think there was a problem with their performance. Several were sold. One of them, for example, was Alice Bucks, who was recently in the news in connection with the prosecutor."
"Where did he go?"
"The Martinez family bought it. As far as I know, he also died during the Martinez showdown."
"Do you know of other girls who have been kicked out of the club?"

"Yes, I know of several other girlfriends who have avoided me there. I didn't know where then, since then I found out that they were taken to Thailand or Dubai. According to the videos on the dark web, some of them have had even worse things happen to them than we did."
"What happened in 2016? How did it all end?" I asked the last question.
"Jessica Marsh closed the place."
"Simply like that?" I asked with disbelief in my voice.
"I made it short, but it wasn't that simple."
"How did it happen?"
"I don't know. For me, it's like a dream that becomes reality after waking up."
"What do you mean by that?"

"The two of us lived in the rooms. One morning, however, the usual alarm didn't sound. I went out into the corridor where we had to march every morning, but not a single gentleman was there to see who was in what condition. Some of them were lying in their beds, taking the opportunity that no one would bother them, and some of us were waiting for someone who knew what was going on. We didn't understand what happened. Then Jessica appeared with fifty or so bank cards in her hand and said: Get out of here and go out into the world! All this shit is over. He handed out the cards and told us to forget all of this if we knew, but not to let the police even think about it. As he knew that there would certainly be some among us who would still try, he began to swear that everyone would take revenge for him, whoever needed it, let's not worry, just take care of ourselves, and if someone wants revenge, let them believe that he will make everyone pay what he has to pay. I believed him. My first trip with my roommate took me straight to an ATM, and then we saw that we had a million dollars on our card. I dropped the chain. I didn't even realize what happened. I contacted my family, but my mother died in the meantime. He basically got ten thousand dollars for me. The poor thing could never process being kidnapped."

Mary Morales stood up for herself. She told her experiences in such detail and with such empathy that the audience could feel how she experienced every moment of the horrors she told. The world stopped for a minute, I heard people's breaths. They wanted fresh air instead of oppressive stuffy Neon Nights, and I knew that this changed everything they wanted to think about their lives before.
"What is your name?" Mortenstein's lawyer asked Hermione.
"Mary Morales."
"Some still call themselves Hermione."
"Yes, as I usually use my stage name."
"What do you do that you took a stage name?" he inquired.
"With online porn" Mary replied firmly. A part of the audience was audibly surprised.
"I understand" the lawyer acknowledged, then asked another question.
"How long have you been doing this?"
"since 2019. Before that, I tried to get the neon nights years out of my head for three years."

"I'm sorry, but I don't understand" continued the lawyer. "He says he was held captive in a brothel for eight years, and after the aforementioned unexpected release, he started it all over again three years later."

"Not. Don't say that because it's not true" stuttered Mary. "What I started again is completely different. It is possible that it is similar for you, but there is nothing else that connects the two things apart from sex."
"You know, I can't imagine if every letter of the above was true, how he would have been able to throw himself back into the work he did before."
Of course, the lawyer could very well imagine, he knew exactly what Mary was talking about, he simply wanted Hermione to feel less comfortable in her own skin.

"I'm surprised you don't understand the difference between the two" he started. "Why would enjoying sex and showing it to others be the same as being sexually assaulted against their will? Do I have to explain this to a lawyer?" took a short break. "By the way, there is a much more practical explanation for this work. I guess they noticed that I couldn't see out of either eye. This includes the fact that I haven't been to school since I was sixteen, and when I got out of Neon Nights, I didn't want to enroll quickly at the age of twenty-four. I had money, as I mentioned. However, boredom kills you after a while, and the one million dollars slowly starts to dwindle. It is logical that I wanted to do something with my life."
"According to them, he saw the future in this" the lawyer concluded.
"Yes. By the way, they don't call me Hermione for nothing. I wasn't worried about myself. My followers named me that. I proudly admit that I am good at it."
Hermione quickly got bored of testifying, but now she was really into it and seemed to be relishing her previous wounds, as she happily reported on her current situation.
"Do you know Edgar Mortenstein?" asked the lawyer.
"Yes," Hermione replied.
"How do you know?"
"He was the leader of the club called Neon Nights, where I was detained."
"Yes. He said the same thing to the prosecutor. He, on the other hand, did not ask whether he had already met the president."
Hermione now held a pause.
"I don't understand your question" Hermione paused for a moment. "What do you mean by that?"
"I mean, have you ever met Edgar Mortenstein in your life?"
"I haven't met him in person yet, although I believe he is here in the room right now."
"Yes, he is here, and believe me, he doesn't remember ever seeing you."

A stunned silence fell over the room. I understood this shock, but the answer surprised me less. There were only a few of the girls who had seen Mortenstein in person at Neon Nights. I didn't know anyone other than Jess who had seen him. However, this did not change the fact that he was the head of Neon Nights.
"What makes you think that President Edgar Mortenstein ran the club where he was detained?"
"Everyone knew that. We didn't have to see him to know."

"Still, it would have been nice" the lawyer continued his train of thought. "You state with complete certainty that the president ran that brothel, as if you saw him there every day. However, the truth is that you never saw him, never met him, never heard his voice, so it is easily possible that the president was not even there, he had no idea that such a club existed, instead, it is much more likely that someone misused his name, if they said that Mr. President had something to do with all of this."
"Jessica knew him," Mary shot back.

"Yes, the dead Jessica Marsh, who was the lover of Jefferson Morris and who saved them from the alleged brothel called Neon Nights, she only saw Edgar Mortenstein. However, what evidence can they show for this?"

"We have given all the evidence to the court, sir" I interrupted. Hermione flinched slightly at the unexpected attack. He thought it so self-evident that Mortenstein was at the head of the organization that he didn't even want to believe that they would want to lie about this, even though I tried to point out in the preparation of the witnesses that we had nothing else to do in this matter except Jessica's testimony.
"Which are incomplete regarding the president's person," the lawyer cut in.
"There are indeed gaps in direct evidence" I noted. "but this trial is being held so that the prosecution can indirectly prove the fact that Edgar Mortenstein is behind the Neon Nights network."
"Continue the hearing of the witness!" the judge instructed the lawyer firmly.
"How do you know he was held at a club called Neon Nights?"
"You are also asking about a well-known fact" Hermione worried.
"You don't actually know that for sure, do you?" asked the lawyer mockingly.
"But yes" answered Mary. "In the lobby of the club was a huge Neon Nights sign, surprising as it was; from a neon lamp."
"And how did this sign look like?"
"It was dominated by purple and pink colors. It was written in written letters, the contour light was white."
"And do you know what the logo of the Neon Nights institution maintained by the president looked like?"
"I don't know" Hermione replied.
"In a pink circle, with white and blue NN merging typography. It doesn't look like the mentioned inscription at all."
"It doesn't mean anything" stuttered Mary.

"It is up to the court to decide this" retorted the lawyer. "One more thought at the end, then I'll let you go, I see you're tired. You say you've worked at that place since the beginning, so you should know. Was that place called Neon Nights from the beginning?"

"Not. At first, I admit, I didn't know what his name was. While I worked there as a bartender, it was not called by its name, and the sign Neon Nights was only added to the dance floor of the club years later."
"When?"
"I can't say that."
"Based on these, do you maintain your testimony that you were a prostitute in a club called Neon Nights?"
"Yes, where else would I be?" Mary finished, and I thought I could detect disappointment in her voice. Maybe he didn't think so, but he did everything right.

chapters.title: Chapter Thirty-Three
paragraphs: (Anton Martin)

The next witness would have been David King. However, after the judge's call, half a minute had already passed, but at the door next to the pulpit, we only saw the clerk going out and no one coming in. I looked at Amy. David flipped through King's testimony and jotted down new lines in his notes. About two minutes passed before he noticed that the witness was late. Although the judge inquired aloud, no one answered from beyond the door. Finally, the clerk returned alone.
"David King is gone" he remarked curtly.
"What is he gone?" the judge raised his voice. "Don't you know you have a duty to testify?"
"He was seen going out at the entrance."
"And why wasn't he stopped?"
"They didn't know that he was a witness, Mr. Judge..."

Amy's face was grim. I knew him. I knew he had thought of every eventuality, but he looked bewildered. King's departure came as a surprise, especially since the guy agreed to testify only a few weeks ago.
The room was in an uproar. The judge conferred nervously with the other court members for a minute, then continued.
"Call Lisa Bennet!" issued the order.

In a short time, a well-dressed young girl entered the room with an expensive bag and glittering jewelry on her arm. Her outfit was worth more than Amy's salary for a couple of months combined. The difference between the two witnesses was very striking. Although Hermione was a beautiful woman, her radiance was nowhere near as charismatic as that of this model, who carried in her every move the fact that she had been polished to perfection with meticulous work. It was like a painting that suddenly found itself in a courtroom.

Amy was not like any of the girls. Naturally, the subtle gestures were born in him, which captured the attention of a person without even trying to do so. I have never met anyone like him in my life, he was unrepeatable among all. I saw in him right away that he did not fit in at all in the environment where we met. I think this tension made it really interesting for me when we first met.
Lisa Bennet took her seat on the witness stand somewhat theatrically. His perfectly buttoned attire gave the impression of watching a performance. He looked into Amy's eyes with a curious look.
"What's his name?" Amy asked distantly.
"I'm Lisa Bennet, an international model" she answered.
"How do you know Edgar Mortenstein?"
"I've known Uncle Eddie almost since I was born" he started. "He and Dad have always been good friends."
"Who is your father?"
"James Bennet, the owner of the Bennet & Bennet real estate holding, but I don't think my father had anything to do with this matter" the Bennet girl stammered.
"What is your connection to the Neon Nights?" Amanda put the next question.
"Please specify which Neon Nights you are talking about."
"Talk about what you were involved with."
"I knew about Neon Nights a while ago, which, to put it simply, worked as a model training center in the city center on Avenue Brillante."
"And do you remember from when the Neon Nights functioned in that form you knew?"
"I can't tell you the exact date, but maybe it started around 2010. I've never been that good in history..," smiled Lisa, she felt that she could really afford this much humor.
"Please present the place in more detail, and the classes in which you participated."
Lisa barely visibly rolled her eyes, which Amy couldn't ignore either. I could imagine what was going through his mind that he couldn't say.

"I got into Neon Nights as a high school student. The sessions were during the day after normal school hours. It was like attending evening training. They taught the knowledge related to modeling and other similar fields to a very high standard."
"Could you list some of these areas?" Amy asked.

"Obviously I can" Lisa continued with sarcasm in her voice. "For example, we studied media. This later came in handy for everyone who entered public life, then dance, etiquette, fashion history, style... Everything that wasn't taught in high school, is nevertheless essential for those who wanted to find a job in one of the similar fields. Everyone loved the dance lessons, but we also learned the drier etiquette with pleasure, because they wanted to make real gentlemen out of us, and we wanted to become one too."
"We understand" Amy summarized what Lisa had to say, then continued. "How long have you been attending Neon Nights classes since then?"
"For four years. I don't know exactly, it should be calculated. Maybe from 2013 to 17."
"He has said this before, so presumably the interval is correct" Amy reassured him. "How would you describe the girls at Neon Nights?"
"I don't want to offend anyone" began Lisa Bennet, "but whoever got in there was most likely a real A-category young girl."
"What does category A young girl mean?"

"Girls who had the right background to take advantage of Neon Nights, and I don't just mean financially. A neon nightsos was definitely dedicated enough to make the most of every moment, and his upbringing was impeccable, so to speak. Many of us attended private schools, we received the best education, everything was given to us to succeed."
"What do you mean by success?" Amy inquired further.

"Several became actors, singers, dancers, and well-known models. But I am sure that even those who did not step into the limelight still benefit from what they learned there. You can tell from afar if you've been to Neon Nights."
"Were there girls who grew up in, say, worse conditions?"

"We didn't know where everyone was from. My girlfriends mostly came from better-off families. Obviously we all knew each other. There weren't enough of us to walk past each other on the street as strangers, but I didn't know how everyone got there. I heard there was a scholarship program for those whose families weren't wealthy, but it wasn't clear which girl was receiving the grant. Everyone was treated the same."
"Did someone not complete the training?"

"Oh, how!" snapped Lisa. "Of course. Not everyone could handle the pace dictated by the course leaders. I remember more than once we walked five kilometers in ten-centimeter high heels. Can you imagine how that felt? By the end, everyone's feet were covered in blood. Obviously, this was not always the case. Anyway, it turned out to be a useful experience, they pushed our limits."
"Did a lot of people complain about things being taken so strictly in Neon Nights?"
"We were schoolgirls, many people complained, but now everyone realizes how much we owed to this method."
"And what happened to those girls who left the training?"
"I understand they had to pay back the scholarship."
"And if they weren't on scholarship?"

"Some of them too. I don't know exactly, but there was a commitment from the department of families. The parents of the girls who came from wealthy families must have contracted differently than those who could not afford the training at all."
"How should this be understood?" Amy asked. "He seemed to smell blood."

"I don't know exactly. I did not write these contracts, nor do I know which girl was there with such support. But the news spread that they had signed a ten thousand dollar scholarship with them for one year."
"Not many people left the training then, I think" Amy acknowledged.

"Not a few. You would think that ten thousand dollars is still a visible amount, wouldn't you think. For someone whose family is not rich, it can be a real challenge to extract it, of course it had to be seen as an investment. Despite this, quite a few left Neon Nights during the year, regardless of whether they were on scholarship or not."
"Why did Neon Nights offer scholarships to less fashionable girls?"

"From investment. It's a deal, I guess. Presumably, it would have been repaid from later works. There were quite a few talents among them. I support this attitude, and I think it was a very nice idea on the part of the Edgars. Uncle Eddie is sensitive to social issues, probably because he's a politician."

I suddenly burst out laughing loudly, which I immediately bit back, of course I thought I could detect signs of disgust on Amy's face. Edgar Mortenstein and social sensitivity was a veritable oxymoron to everyone, yet this girl talked about it as if she were at least testifying for Martin Luther King.
"How did the participants recoup this investment after they left the training?" Amy asked Lisa.

"I don't even know how the scholarship was awarded exactly" she objected. "Why are you asking me such a thing? I have no idea about it" looked at the president. "Uncle Eddie can certainly help you with this matter."
"I think so too" Amy replied calmly. "but the President has refused to testify, and I cannot compel him to speak of it."
"I understand" Lisa replied. "Well then there is nothing to do, you have to find someone else who knows this" he acknowledged enthusiastically.
"Do you think it is conceivable that there were girls who worked this repayment in, say, another place?" Amy tried to steer the conversation towards the other Neon Nights.
"What are you thinking?"
"Say, as sex workers."
"Jesus!" Lisa Bennet sighed. "How do you come up with such a thing? We studied in a professional system, don't make me laugh with such theories."
"I think I raised a realistic possibility. Why are you so aloof from him?"
"I don't even respond to that" she scorned. "I find this unimaginable."
"Why do you consider it so?" Amanda went on prying.
"I have 100% trust in those who provided us with these opportunities. That's all I can add to that."

Lisa Bennet was genuinely outraged by Amy's idea, even though it was a perfectly realistic suggestion that insolvent girls were being used as sex workers. Although at first we all protested against accepting that an inverse Neon Nights had to exist, in the end we had to realize that it was nothing more than a devilishly designed trap, which was no less than an investment. In fact, it was the only real one. Those who were successful and rich could, thanks to their background, pump huge amounts of money into Neon Nights training, and they did so with pleasure. They thought they were exclusive, and in the form they experienced, the place really gave them what it promised. Well-to-do families presumably easily paid off the debt for their back-dancing daughters, and were perhaps more ashamed that they didn't make it to the end than outraged by the harshness of the training. The girls from less fortunate backgrounds, but persistent and talented, could pay off their debts from their later jobs, while those who stepped back from them could find themselves in the Neon Nights of Mordor. The idea was brilliant. And Amy had to connect the two places somehow.
"Lisa, I have one more question for you" Amy said. "Have you ever experienced any signs of sexual abuse at Neon Nights?"

"I haven't experienced anything like that. We had professional teachers, most of them women. They were tough, but they didn't touch anyone with a new one. By the way, special emphasis was also placed on sexual education. Sessions were held by sexologists and intimacy coordinators. Believe me, I'm telling the truth when I say Neon Nights was decent in every way."
"Thanks!" Amy replied and sat down at her desk.

Now it was the lawyer's turn. I haven't quite figured out their strategy yet. Thinking with the head of a crime writer, I tried to see the most unexpected things in the background, but Mortenstein's side, as if he didn't want to deny anything, rather tried to make the Neon Nights phenomenon look like a series of misunderstandings. It was clear that they also accepted it, there was a place where girls were sexually exploited, but they skillfully covered up that they had anything to do with it. One could almost feel that they too had become victims of some sophisticated abuse.
"How are you?" the lawyer asked with attentiveness in his voice, but Lisa seemed caught unexpectedly by this kindness.
"Thank you, quite well" he answered curtly, narrowing his eyes.
"Please describe what the Neon Nights where you held your trainings looked like."

"Like a palace" she exaggerated. "It was a beautiful building in the city center. Three floors, snow-white walls, marble columns. Neon Nights itself was not intrusive. I mentioned earlier during the hearing that we were obliged to maintain confidentiality when signing the contract, and this approach was also reflected in his placement. Only on the intercom was a barely noticeable inscription. It wasn't advertised anywhere. Incidentally, Hermés was also in the same building on Avenue Brillante, with Cartier next to it. It was really motivating to enter the building even as a teenager. But now I don't know what's been working there since it stopped, which I'm really sorry for anyway..."

Lisa chattered. Although he did not answer what the lawyer asked, he seemed to want to cut it. This made me believe him even more sincerely when he talked about how special he felt in the environment that his rich family and circle of friends created for him. She was treated like a real gentleman, and I knew then that Lisa Bennet's testimony was going to be a dead end for Amy. He was obviously telling the truth. Its impeccable manners and appearance showed from afar that it was the result of the careful work of professionals. I envied him because of Amy. I knew for a fact that Neon Nights of You was Amy's dream, but instead she was banished to the Brimstone Hell. I was angry at the world. Then I looked at Amanda and I was filled with infinite pride. I remembered how Mortenstein had treated her in the bistro. He was afraid of her. Amy's life journey was a middle finger up to this world, and I couldn't look at it without admiration.

"The interior was created based on carefully compiled plans. In its mood, it matched the style outside. It was furnished with quality furniture and was definitely not over the top or intrusive. I loved going there."
"And was there any reference to the Neon Nights inside the building?" the lawyer asked.

"Neon Nights operated on the entire third floor, which was taken up by a private elevator, and of course there was a sign that it was there. At the entrance, a sign indicated what was on the other side of the door, while in the lobby, there was a neon, yet discreet sign."
"What did this inscription look like?"
"I'd rather call it a logo. In a pink circle, white and blue NN slipped into each other. Neon Nights was signed in small letters in a semicircle. I can still see it before my eyes."
"And what did the name Neon Nights mean? What did it want to refer to?" the lawyer went on inquiring.

"It may seem corny to today's eyes, but in the mid-2010s there was a renaissance of neon lights and it hinted at what we wanted to achieve. We wanted to be the light at the galas, at the big events of social life."

The lawyer, it seemed, did not want to keep Lisa up for long. He asked him a few more things, for example, what kind of person he knew Mortenstein to be, and he said that the president was one of the best people in the world, which, let's face it, could not be considered an unbiased statement on his part, but nobody expected him to say anything other than what he thought. Lisa Bennet was honest. After the girl's appearance, the police carefully looked into who she really was, and in every detail the reality matched what she said. She was an international model who was active mainly in Europe, and more recently in the Far East, as an ambassador for countless long-established brands. He was a celebrated celebrity in his profession, and most importantly, he was as pure as snow. He really owed everything to Neon Nights, and it was impossible to expect him to say even one bad word, or even the president who made it possible for him. If they wanted credibility for Neon Nights, they got it. They could hardly have found a better person for this purpose than Lisa Bennet. She shone in the Neon Nights sky as promised upon her entry. He said something else, then left the room, and I felt uneasy. Not because of Lisa. His confession didn't shake Amy's plans, but rather the weight of the trial began to twist her arms. Out of curiosity, I looked at my watch, barely an hour had passed since the trial of the century.

chapters.title: Chapter Thirty-Four
paragraphs: Several people were smoking in front of the building. I stared at the billowing smoke as it ran towards the sky in the summer heat. I just snagged a few pieces of them with my nose, but even those few puffs had such a strong effect on me as if I were a smoker again, even though it had been many years since I quit. I pondered my morning restlessness. I had been perplexed for a day since I saw that boy in the pictures in the newspaper. Who could he be?

In the courtroom, Amy had heard who knows how many witnesses from both sides. Several girls came forward to testify about their experiences in the darker Neon Nights, but as if they just wanted to bide their time, none of them could say anything new. Neither of them had seen Mortenstein face to face, so it was difficult to connect him to the place they were talking about. What a sophisticated system they have built... I morphed. I wanted to help Amy somehow, and I knew I had the key in my hand, even if I didn't know what kind of lock it opened yet.
"Matt!" I shouted. He came out of the courthouse just at that moment. "You have to help" I waved to him, almost begging, but I was a little lacking in specifics.
Matt was open to me, I showed him the pictures I took yesterday and asked him to say anything that could explain the boy I thought might be important.
"So it's all just a feeling?" he asked, dislodging the fragile pillars of my theory in seconds.
"I would rather call it intuition" I answered. "I can't put it into words, but I'm very uneasy about it. I know it means something, and the more I think about it, the more I feel it is important."
"It can be easy. Just look at the picture. Everyone is there with their children. This little guy is approx. twelve years old. It must belong to someone."
"To Mortenstein?" I asked excitedly.
"That seems logical if he stands next to him," he replied.
"Does Mortenstein have children?" I was surprised.
"There isn't."
"I know that too. But he could be his nephew."
"Does this matter?" Matt asked, getting to the point. "If I were you, I would continue on this path. If he says it's important, then it's worth putting time into it."
"It sure is" I answered. "When is the break?"
"Maybe in ten minutes. Would you like anything until then?" he inquired.
"I don't know. I'll figure it out. I'd rather go back to Amy. If I think of something, I'll let you know."

Not that I had any right to command a detective, but Matt and Wilbur were slowly becoming a part of my everyday life. I hoped that a divine spark would soon arrive and everything that had been in darkness until then would be illuminated.
(Amanda Evans)

The trial progressed, even if terrible fatigue began to plague me after a few hours. Anton seemed very excited during the break, but he didn't say anything specific, except that it was important and not. He said he prefers to keep his nonsense to himself. Regardless, he still approached Matt and had a brief discussion with him about something. I knew he was investigating, I couldn't get him to wean me off this, I thought, I'll talk to him at home about what he was up to today.

Anyway, I had another problem. After listening to the girls, a wave of doubt hit me. I was almost ashamed of what I thought. I was scared of him. What if I can't prove the president had anything to do with Neon Nights. Not to the shop window, which according to them the whole upper class knew about, but to ours, which was the reality for us.

None of us saw the president there but Jess, and I couldn't be sure that his testimony alone would be enough to prove it. Even though everyone knew who Edgar Mortenstein was, the defense cleverly twisted things to make the whole thing look like a misuse of the president's name. They didn't empathize with the girls' suffering, they didn't want to minimize the meanness, they simply pretended that Mortenstein had nothing to do with it all.

Jessica's death was no accident. I just had to connect all the clues together. I was sure that if I succeeded, the picture put together would be strong enough to put the president behind bars, yet I danced with the evidence on a razor's edge.

As much as I wanted to push the thought away, the fact that only Jess had the key to the truth stirred something in me that I had never felt before. I began to doubt that he was telling the truth. Bullshit! I was tired, maybe that's why the thought came to me, but I couldn't help but think about this possibility. What if he lied all along? After all, she never talked about Jake. Not even once. And when she got together with Jeff, she hid everything I should have known about him, and then continued to lie even when the investigation was well underway. I knew he was threatened, but did Jake really write that letter? And why didn't he ever talk about Bill the lawyer? So many questions lay unanswered in front of me.
After the break, the hearing of Timothy Jones followed. The little guy with the cape sat on the witness stand excitedly, yet with a palpable affront.
"You Mr. Jones is the LC. Editor-in-Chief of Rumors?"
"Yes, I am" he answered.
"How long have you been running the paper?"
"Since its inception. from 2000."
"How would you describe LC. Rumors' profile and role in the current press?"

"We are basically a tabloid. But in the highest possible quality. Many people look down on the boulevard, even though it is an important area, especially in today's world. People have always been curious about what is happening in the world above them, which they cannot otherwise see. The world of stars has occupied people since the press was created."
Jones's speech clearly revealed his sociological sensibility regarding the functioning of society.
"Do I understand correctly that the boulevard is not just a job for you?"
"I'm used to saying that the boulevard is my life, although this may seem ridiculous in the eyes of many. It's okay, I've already accepted that they look down on my work."
"What do you think is most important in the boulevard?"

"Definitely the rumours. Did you know that most of the rumors are true? I'm not saying they're all true, but some of them are. And if someone comes across as many rumors in the course of their work as I do, after a while they will see the trends and the true structure of the tabloid world. All I mean by that is that I have it all in my little finger, so if I haven't heard of something, it doesn't exist."
"With that, he already hinted at why we called him here" I explained the reasons for Timothy Jones' presence.
"Yes, well the Neon Nights article, surely."
Suddenly, it was as if the roles were reversed and the witness started asking questions. It was a smiling situation, Jones really got into it.
"A typical case of making people believe of something that it exists, whereas in reality it doesn't."
"Are you saying Neon Nights didn't exist?"
"There might have been something with that name, but not in the form it was in that article."
"I'd like you to explain exactly which article you are speaking of."
"About the issue of July 8, 2010 and the article that I should have rushed into in the afternoon so that I could take it out after sending it to the printer."
"Could you tell me how the incident happened?"

"In the afternoon I received a call from a woman named Jane Smith. Signed in on behalf of the President's Office. He asked me to publish the article in the newspaper that he was going to send soon. At first, I objected that I could not include any articles in that day's issue, since it was completely full. The newspaper has a precise structure, it is easy to follow, which I am particularly proud of."
"And what happened after that?" I inquired.

"The woman kept insisting, she even threatened me so that I would be aware that you can't say no to them, so I went along with it. He sent the article, I took out another one, and finally that day's issue was ready. Then around midnight, when I was already sitting in the car in the parking garage, he called me again to say that there was no way the article would be published, that I should take action immediately. I hesitated again, but quickly realized that it was unnecessary. I called the printer, replaced it with an advertisement and the matter was resolved. It really bothered me, but I tried to forget it as soon as possible. It was an unpleasant, bad feeling. Never before or since has anyone forced me to put something in my sheet with a mandatory argument."
"How did they threaten you?" I asked.
"It doesn't matter, I don't really remember it. It wasn't even that important."
"And what was the article about?"

"It's all bullshit. Basically nothing. That's why I brought up that what I don't know about probably doesn't exist. They wrote about a ball that was attended by the top ten thousand in order to make deals with each other. The event was compared to the balls of the 1700s, and it was written that it was the vision of President Edgar Mortenstein that the social elite would once again organize themselves at events like the evenings of the time. I treated the content of the article with reservations, because I had not even heard of anything like it before, and if the social elite of Love City had really participated in the event, the news would have definitely reached me. I've been trying to find out more about him for weeks, to no avail. It became certain to me that this ball never existed. At least not in the form in which it was discussed."
"And what was the name of this evening?"
"I would rather not say anything about it" Jones replied. "Neon Nights. How can such an event be called that?"
"Do you have any idea why it was necessary for the article to be published in LC. Rumors?"

"I didn't understand it then, and I don't understand it now. I would think of it as propaganda, but I have no idea what the fuss was about. Not least because if I had known about it, I would have volunteered to cover the event myself."

Timothy Jones talked a lot. Incomprehension and offense were felt in his testimony. A small part of him, perhaps to this day, thought he missed out on one of Love City's biggest social events, which understandably hurt him.
One thing was certain. The name Neon Nights came up again and again in different contexts and this fact could not be ignored.

"Dear Court!" I continued. "We followed the contents of the mentioned article. We have come to the conclusion that there is nothing else to report on the event presented above. On the contrary, we found many letters that referred to Neon Nights as a model trainer. This inconsistency allows the assumption that the events and organizations associated with Neon Nights are actually different manifestations of this name, which were connected to the same circle, Edgar Mortenstein's circle."
"Thank you, prosecutor!" answered the judge, then turned to the lawyer. "Does the defense want to question the witness?"
"No, Your Honor! We don't want to deal with articles about Neon Nights."
"It's okay. I'll end the session then" said the judge. "The trial will resume tomorrow at 10 a.m."

chapters.title: Chapter Thirty-Five
paragraphs: Evening of July 21, 2022
I knew that Anton was limping in something all day, so when we got home, I wanted to ask him the question right away, but he got ahead of me.
"I found something yesterday" he said. "Photos from the 2008 elections."
"And what was on them?" I inquired.
"At the time of the election victory, a little boy flanged on the platform. It belonged to someone, but we don't know who."
"What is this plural?"
"I also showed Matt the pictures."
"Was that the nonsense you were talking about during the day?" I asked.
"Ez."
"And why are these pictures so interesting?"
Anton took out his phone and showed them.
"do you see In the first one, he is standing in the front row next to Mortenstein, then in the other pictures he is further and further back, finally disappearing on the edge."
"Nice curly kid," I noted.
"The" answered Anton, and then we sat facing each other in silence for a minute.
"Amy, you noticed too, didn't you?"
"Is it that there are too many curly haired people in this case? I was just wondering how you came up with that" I answered. "How did you come up with this?"
"Intuition."
"So that's why you talked to Matt."
"Yes. He is on to finding out."
"When will he be done with it?"
"Tomorrow during the day."
"Then let's hope, because I feel like today didn't go very well."
I've been waiting all day for a negotiation. I was hoping Anton would calm me down.
"There is not enough evidence" I started.
"No, there isn't" insisted Anton. "The flash drive is full of it."

"And I don't even have access to half of it. It doesn't matter what's on it anyway. Obviously, there were several types of Neon Nights. And the president is only tied to the place by Jess's testimony, which won't be enough. Well thought out. Nothing has been denied, so it is up to me to prove beyond a doubt that Mortenstein was the leader. But how if no one saw it?"
We listened again. I was tired and had a gloomy vision of tomorrow.
"I thought I was doing well with Neon Nights at least" I complained. "instead, it is now that someone misused the president's brand. As if anyone else could have known that name."

"But that's Amy!" Anton threw himself into hope. "What are the chances that Neon Nights will come to mind? And the president's worldview can be seen in everything. Everything you associate with this name stands out from the crowd. If you are a model? Be the best. If you had a ball? Give me the way" then stopped for a moment. "If you want to fuck..."
"Then fuck without limits..." I completed his sentence. I knew exactly what he was speaking of.

He was right. At the same time, I also knew how much pressure there is in court in a case against a president. They cannot judge based on assumptions. Even if everyone has the picture together, how can it be proven that he was the head of Neon Nights? No one but Jess saw her there. And Jessica's testimony was in vain detailed if no one else could support her most important claims.

"Don't forget Jake either" Anton spoke up. "You can prove that he was a Neon Nights man and only a blind man can't see that he's connected to the president. The house of cards collapses when you knock out a card. Start with the weakest, Jak!"

Toby McRae came to court in a police uniform. He had been a member of the board for eight years, yet he seemed excited. His testimony could help establish a connection between Ross Wilson and the assassin who was after Anton, and his information could be used to link Wilson to Neon Nights. They were all important pieces of the puzzle.
"Do you recognize who is in the picture?" I asked McRae.
"Ross Wilson" he answered.
"When did you first meet?"
"Probably January 1st 2014. That's when I went to the police."
"How was your relationship with the detective?"
"At first we had no relationship with each other. We didn't talk."
"Did this change over time?"
"We had to exchange a few words regarding some matters."
"What were these cases?" I inquired.

"Ironically, the first such case was Neon Nights. We received a call that a girl had died, Wilson took the case immediately and told me to go with him. According to the rules, the police can never go to the crime scene alone."
"Do I understand correctly that you accidentally got involved?"
"Actually, yes."
"Did you experience any strangeness regarding the case?" I pried.

"More than that, yes. Right at the beginning. I found it unusual that Wilson threw himself into the case right away, because after a report, patrolmen go to the scene first, and then they notify the investigators. Here, however, the call came directly from the club."
"Then, if I understand correctly, they called Wilson specifically?"
"Actually yes" replied Toby McRae.
"Did you report this to anyone?"
"I was there a few weeks ago. Nothing good could have come of reporting Detective Wilson."
"What did you experience at the club?"
"When we got there, Wilson went in alone. I was waiting in the car. After a few minutes, two police officers left Neon Nights."
"According to them, they were ordered there via the emergency call" I supplemented the testimony.
"Yes, in all probability."
"Why do you think the club called Wilson when the police had already been called on the emergency line?"
"I can think of someone calling Wilson and someone calling the police."
"What happened next?"
"After ten minutes Wilson came out and we went back to the guard. In the car, I asked him what happened. He said one of the girls died in the bathroom. He suspected an overdose."
"Did you believe him?"
"I had no reason not to believe him, but I was surprised that it had come to that."
"Do you know her name?" I inquired.

"Yes, to Katherine Mayers. Wilson compiled a very short material about her, in which he surprisingly wrote that the girl had been shot in the head. He called me an overdose in the car. He was obviously trying to mislead me."
"How long were you colleagues with Wilson?"
"He left the police in 2015. In principle, he went to the Bahamas because he got a bigger sum. Even then, I thought that he might have received a bribe, just because of cases like the Mayers case."
"This year, however, thanks to you, we found out he returned."

"After hearing about Wilson, I started investigating him myself. I found out that he has been to Love City several times since then, the last time obviously when we found his body in the hotel in the Nouveau Palais."
"Has it since been revealed who killed him?"

"Not really" McRae replied. "But we found a few numbers in his phone, including the number of the assassin who attacked you a few days earlier. The calls were also traced and they actually spoke to each other."
"Does this mean that the assassin was ordered by Ross Wilson to kill us?"
"All signs point to that, Prosecutor Evans."
"Did they find anything else about him?"
"Yes. A few days before his death, according to cell information, he was in the Liberty neighborhood, close to where Jake Cooper lived."
"Close to it or right there?"

"We are talking about cell information, we only know that it is connected to the transmission tower that broadcasts in that area. But another interesting thing about him came to light. On the day Jessica Marsh was killed, she was in Montaukville in the early hours of the morning, according to the same information."
"So that's when the Black Mustang Jefferson Morriset testified drove into a car?" I inquired.
"Yes, right then."
"And was he still there in the evening when the assassination took place?"
"Not" replied the patrolman. "Ross Wilson was already in Love City by then."

"Let's wrap things up Mr. McRae" I took the initiative. "Do you think it's possible that Ross Wilson was a Neon Nights man who was used to cover up sensitive matters for the organization and that this relationship continued after Wilson's Bahamian detour?"
"Yes, I think that Wilson was the Neon Nights' man, and had to do with the premature closing of numerous cases. Besides, it seems he set to work again in Love City before his death."
"Thank you, Patrolman McRae!"

Mortenstein sat through the entire testimony with a frozen smile on his face. This face irritated me. He was arrogant and overconfident. I didn't believe this charade for a minute. I was sure he was grinding inside. It had to be even if he honestly believed he could get away with it. There was no reason to believe that after the trial he would be the same president as he was before. After fourteen years in power, a case like this tarnishes one's reputation even if one eventually leaves the courtroom victorious, I thought. But his smile spoke to me and said: you can't catch me!
The president's lawyer put only a few questions to Patrolman McRae.
"When you and Ross Wilson arrived at Neon Nights, did you see the President there?"
Toby obviously answered no, because if he had seen it he would have mentioned it much earlier.
"In your investigation of Ross Wilson, did you find any leads to suggest that President Edgar Mortenstein had anything to do with the Neon Nights club?"
"I couldn't find one, lawyer" he answered briefly.
"I understand. The defense has no more questions for the witness!"

chapters.title: Chapter Thirty-Six
paragraphs: The other part of the case revolved around Jake. I wanted to hear Jake as a witness at the trial, but I didn't have the chance, and not just because Jeff was sitting in the room right now. His lawyer forbade him to say a word during the trial, as did the president, especially after the scandal at the sanatorium. By the way, according to the expert opinion, his illness did not make Jake punishable, and in the best case they could only have imposed compulsory medical treatment on him, but I didn't care how many years he got. I owed Jessiáca enough to expose her killer. I woke up this morning feeling like, despite all the weirdness, I'd rather believe him. I was also ashamed that I began to doubt him yesterday.

"Dear court!" I started. "Despite the defense's sole focus on keeping the president out of the Neon Nights network, there is circumstantial evidence to the contrary. One of them is the testimony of Jake Cooper, during which he consistently called me Alice Bucks, despite the fact that he was already in the sanitarium during the smear campaign against me. Since, according to her lawyer, there was no communication between them about Alice during her treatment, it can be proven with her testimony that Jake Cooper could have known Alice Bucks' name because she was a member of Neon Nights, according to Jessica Marsh's claims. I would like to add that Jake Cooper called me Alice already at our first meeting, and we cannot forget that immediately after this meeting, the presidential speech was given, in which I was identified with Alice Bucks. With the response to our questions to the president's office that they don't want to reveal their sources, we think they wanted to protect Jake Cooper."

Mrs. Alioglu definitely wanted to come. He wanted to clear Mike's name, and he felt the lawsuit against Neon Nights was his last chance to do so. Mike's death was as much the crime of Neon Nights as our slavery, so it could not go unpunished either. A broken lady in her fifties took a seat on the witness stand.
"Do you know Jake Cooper?" I asked him.
"I used to know him."
"And how well did you know him?"
"Mike was my son's best friend. He spent a lot of time with us as a teenager. It seemed to me that he had no parents."
"What kind of person would you describe Jake as?"
"He was a cheerful boy despite his ordinary, difficult fate. He ate with us many times. Mantim became one of his favorites."
A shy smile appeared on Jeff's face. It must have been a shared experience with Jake.
"Of course, I didn't know him well. I saw as much of him as you can see of a teenager."
"Were you aware of Jake's condition?"
"No, but then there wasn't anything, or Mike would have reported it."
"How do you look back on the period before your son's death?"
Fatma Alioglu sighed.

"It was as if an anthill had been disturbed. That's when people in the neighborhood started talking about a new club in the Dionysos district. Jobs were offered to boys. Mike said they are in great danger because the place deals with prostitution. Of course, the recruiters were talking about something else. But Mike knew what was behind it."
"Did they not approach girls?"

"Certainly not towards us. If they were really engaged in prostitution, they could have been caught elsewhere. But it was sacrilege anyway. In a neighborhood like Liberty, there were many Muslims and Christians. None of them tolerates this."
"Despite this, was there willingness on the part of the boys for the work?"
"To some extent, but Mike did his best to distract that mob from the area."
"What did he do?"
"First, he talked to everyone he spoke to. He told them the truth."
"How did Mike know what could be going on there?"
"He was a smart kid and informed. He could read between the lines. He knew that there was no easy money, especially when one heard the salaries they were told."
"How much did they offer the boys for the work?"
"A thousand dollars. For teenagers. I was disappointed that they were limping in the wrong way."
"Was Jake Cooper interested in the opportunity?"
"Unfortunately, yes. He gave it serious consideration. Mike couldn't sleep because of it. I'm not saying this to make Jake feel bad, but it's the truth."
"How did Mike die?"

"If he set his mind to something, he did it all the way through, and he was determined that no one in the neighborhood would be dragged into this dirty business. I don't know what was going through his mind, but I think he wanted to save Jake the night the Neon Nights people killed him. Jake was also nearby. He heard Mike say..."
Mrs. Alioglu fell silent. He couldn't finish the sentence, because he would have had to imagine what he never dared: the bone-piercing scream of his son falling into flames.
"The police report describes this evening a little differently" I hesitated. "There, according to the testimony, Mike wanted to enter Neon Nights and was killed because..."
The lady interrupted me.

"Anything could fit in that report. I don't know how you wanted to finish the sentence, but would it have made sense? Did Mike get killed for trying to get in? But that's what those people wanted. Or how should this be imagined? That detective just wanted to get this over with quickly. He knew that if Neon Nights was involved, he'd better close the case as soon as possible. To his credit, at least he protected Cooper. The poor thing was very scared."
"How do you mean, protected?"
"It wasn't for nothing that he wrote that in the report. He didn't want anyone asking Jake about it later. It doesn't say that they wanted to recruit Jake."
"Didn't you feel that this is unfair toward Mike?"
"We are a close-knit people, Miss Evans. At least Jake had to be protected."
"Was this the police officer who wrote the report?" I showed Mrs. Alioglu a picture of Ross Wilson. He nodded with closed eyes.
"Are you aware that according to a testimony, Jake Cooper ultimately did end up at the Neon Nights?"
He looked at me confused. He didn't seem to understand exactly what that meant.
"I feel sorry for the poor guy" he answered. "No one should have entered them."

I didn't want to completely crush Fatma Alioglu with my reconstructed vision of Mike's death. In this one, Jake got a lot more roles, and it didn't have the people from Neon Nights in it. Of course, from the perspective of so many years, it was impossible to prove who did it. So, for my part, I finished the witness interview, and the defense did not ask to speak. This surprised me. Mike's mother could have portrayed Jake in a better light, although with the latest information, not sure.

"Dear Court!" I continued. "The complaint against Edgar Mortenstein was filed by Jessica Marsh, but soon after she received threatening messages, she withdrew it. Taking this circumstance into account, the office itself filed a complaint against the Neon Nights network. Shortly after, Jessica Marsh was killed in a car accident during an attack. The circumstances of the case are unclear to this day, and taking into account the history and the traces recorded at the crime scene, Jefferson Morris cannot be ruled out, nor can the responsibility of Jake Cooper. For the sake of clarity, I call forensic vehicle expert Raymond Mason as a witness."
Raymond was a veteran biker in the trade. He spent his youth in NASCAR as a mechanic. He knew everything about cars. He was in the service of the police for more than ten years.
"Please summarize briefly what is worth knowing about Jefferson Morriset and Jessica Marsh's accident."

"After braking, the car is about 70 km/h" crashed into the side of the tunnel entrance. Although there were side airbags, they did not deploy, although a collision at this speed at this angle would be fatal anyway. The right front seat has almost completely disappeared, and the wall of the tunnel has pushed in almost to the gearbox. It can be considered a miracle that Jefferson Morris survived.
"Does the wreck's condition support the defendant's claim that they were forced off the road?"
"We found signs of a crash on the body, which came from a black Ford Mustang."
"According to this, the defendant's claim is correct when he says they were pursued with such a car."
"There must have been contact between the defendant's BMW and the aforementioned Mustang."
"So the defendant's testimony can be considered authentic?" I pried.
"Not completely" the expert hesitated. "Some of the circumstances are inconsistent with what Jefferson Morris claimed."
The monotony of the people sitting in the room suddenly turned into interest.

"Let's perhaps start with how difficult it is in reality to push someone off the road so that they crash into the entrance of a tunnel. Mainly because the defending party will do everything to avoid it. Also, in order for a car to hit the wall in the known position, the Mustang must push the rear of the BMW. The damage is on the left front door of the car, so if that impact had caused the collision, the car would have hit the tunnel wall with its right front."
Raymond Mason's report showed that he really understands what happens between two cars when they collide. Jefferson's story began to unfold with his confidence.

"Furthermore, the handbrake was engaged in the car after the impact" he continued. "Jefferson Morris had to use this before they hit the wall. In many accidents, the driver does it, but in this case it was only good for the side of the car to hit the wall."
"Do you think he chose this maneuver on purpose?" I asked back. "or was it just a mistake on his part?"
"I don't want to answer this question because I don't know."
"Is there another trace too that you cannot explain reassuringly?"
"There is yes" he snapped right away. "The shape of the indentation on the left front door."
"How do you mean? You said earlier that it originates from a black Mustang."
"Yes, but it's as if the BMW sustained the damage while stationary."
I looked at Jefferson. He seemed confused. The lawyer was firm with him, but he was beginning to lose his temper. And Raymond continued to speak.

"When two cars meet each other while driving, an elongated, less deep deformation occurs on the door, because the impact has less energy due to the parallel forces. However, there is a deeper damage on the door of the BMW. For such damage to occur while driving, the Mustang would have had to be traveling at a significantly higher speed than the BMW was traveling. However, I don't think this is likely, because the road turns left all the way before the tunnel. It is impossible that the other car could have gained a speed advantage of almost 30 km/h, especially since, according to Jefferson Morriset's testimony, he was also going fast."

I was sure the lawyer would have liked to end Raymond Mason's hearing by now, but he couldn't really cite anything, so he could only hope it would end soon. However, Jeff couldn't take it anymore and cried out loudly.

"But it happened as I said!" he shouted. "I don't know where you have seen cars collide with each other, on the race track or where, but the reality is different! Don't listen to him, he wasn't there, he doesn't know what happened. Why would I want to kill my fiancee?!"

The judge ordered Jeff to order, but he could no longer restrain himself and began to beat anyone who touched him. The nurses tried to overcome him. With great difficulty, they were able to give him an injection that knocked him out. Due to the sudden confusion, there was a break, so I had a chance to talk to Matt. He said the results would be out soon. I was hoping that Anton would be right and that we could move the pieces into the right place on the trial chessboard. I was much more optimistic than last night.
A rather large crowd had gathered in the corridor of the court. I was still talking to Matt when suddenly someone grabbed my shoulder. It was Mrs. Morriset, Jeff's foster mother.
"Miss Evans!" he started. "I would like to testify."
I was amazed.
"Why don't you apply to Jefferson's lawyer?" I puzzled.
"I feel like I should tell you how Jefferson behaved after the accident and..," he fell silent.
"Is there something you haven't spoken of so far?" I asked.

"I've known him for a long time. Both personalities" he answered. "When it came to what happened in the park today, I remembered something. Another personality can only be created by a large permanent shock that shatters the mind. And it was during that period that Jake's personality split."
"Isn't it shock enough that they set his friend on fire?" I asked cautiously.
"But even greater, if he does it himself..."

chapters.title: Chapter Thirty-Seven
paragraphs: Mrs. Morriset wringed her hands nervously. He was afraid to look at Jefferson, who was staring at him bewildered through a fog of drugs. He was like a little child. Jeff considered the Morris couple to be his real parents, which she certainly knew, so I couldn't understand why she wanted to testify against him anyway.
"Do you swear to tell only the truth?" asked the judge.
"Yes" answered the witness very softly.
"He just called me with that" i started. "that you would like to tell the Honorable Court how Jefferson Morris behaved after the assassination."
"Yes," she replied again.
"Why?"
Jefferson's mother drew her eyebrows together.
"Excuse me, prosecutor, could you repeat your question?" he was confused.
"Why do you want to tell us?"
Margaret Morriset felt uncomfortable. She thought for a moment.
"Because Jefferson... because Jefferson" meanwhile, he gathered all his courage and looked at his son, who returned it with a pitifully blank look. "Because Jefferson really loved that girl."
"Did you get that from the way Jefferson behaved after the accident?"
"Yes" he answered.
"What did it manifest itself in?"
"He was crying. For days. He was almost delirious with pain. She screamed with Jake."
"What did you say to Jake?"
"How he misses Jessica" gave the obvious answer.
"What was Jake's relationship with Jessica?" I asked.
"They loved each other, I think."
"Don't you know?"
"It's enough that Jefferson loved Jessica, they don't always have to be separated from each other, after all, that's how they lived together. Jake has rarely been seen in recent years anyway."
"What Mrs. Morriset has just told you is irrelevant information to the prosecution. You did not appear as a witness for the defense. Why not?"
"Because I thought about it when you talked about Mike Alioglu's death."
"Do you think Mike's death had anything to do with Jefferson Morris's split personality?"
"Yes."
"Do you think that the loss of his friend was such a strong stimulus for Jake Cooper that it led to this state?"
"Y-yes" answered Margaret Morris uncertainly.

I knew what he wanted to say because he had already said it in the hallway. But I didn't want him to testify against his son. It's not for mothers. I wasn't a mother, but I figured no matter how good a person Margaret Morris was, I couldn't let her do that.
"Is there anything else you would like to say?" I inquired with an urgent voice.
She understood why I did not want to question her further.
"Not" he answered. "That's all I wanted. I just wanted to say that he loved her," Quietly, almost insignificantly, he returned to his seat.
For a while no one spoke, as if the honorable court's attention had wandered.
"Are we waiting for something now, Miss Evans?" asked the judge.
"As a matter of fact, yes" I answered. "A new piece of evidence, to be exact."
"What kind of evidence is it?"
"Please give the honorable court a few minutes of patience, and it will turn out," I said confidently.
The judge did not press the matter. I sat at my desk in total calm, while a quiet bewilderment unfolded around Mortenstein in the dock, which slowly spread to the entire room.
"We would like the prosecutor to say something about this new evidence" Mortenstein's lawyer spoke up.
"Of course" I answered. "Does it suit you in three minutes? I don't happen to want to appear disrespectful by talking about something I don't yet have."
"Do you need a break, Miss Evans?" asked the judge.
"There isn't. It would be completely unnecessary for us to take a break. It really only takes a few minutes" I calmed their spirits, while with closed eyes I tried to become one myself.

The tension became palpable. People were uncomfortable with the silence that had been imposed on them, and I felt it was a good thing that Matt was a little late. A few moments later, we heard the hard heel of his shoe knocking in the courtroom corridor. All attention was on him. Entering the door, he immediately walked over to my desk and handed me an envelope. I picked up the letter knife, which was a surprising yet natural addition to the meeting room, tore open the letter and opened it with slow movements.
"Dear court!" I started with an undisguised smile on my face. "According to the DNA test carried out today, there is a father-son relationship between Jake Cooper and Edgar Mortenstein."

chapters.title: Chapter Thirty-Eight
paragraphs: Journal entry by Jessica Marsh (currently unknown)

Do they transport you in such cars? I was surprised because I had never seen a car where Jeff got out of. Who are you anyway? I asked him. But he just stared at me approvingly. You really are a strange guy, I remarked, and I could see that he liked me saying that, because he smiled. One of my father's men brought me and finally quelled my curiosity. But why? I asked him, are you so rich? He replied that it was his father, but they never lived together. Sometimes he takes her home, then he stays there for a few days, but then he goes home to Liberty, which is one of the worst neighborhoods in the city, but he still loves her. I didn't understand why her father wouldn't let her live with him, but I didn't really care. He promised that he would find a place to take me today, because he had wandered in many places around here. He is a kind of urban adventurer, he is comfortable here on the mountain, where many rich people live, and he likes to hang out in the big yards, sometimes it takes days before he is noticed. But we are not going to run into such a yard now, he said. There is a plot of land, quite high up, with a beautiful view. I will take you there because you can see the quarter where I live. I brought sandwiches and water, I answered, showing him my bag, and I was glad that he didn't want to take me to the rich courtyards. I felt it was more romantic what he came up with. We walked the streets. The road led between huge palaces. I thought how good it must be to live here. I envied everyone who was blessed to live in their own house, and even more so those who did it with their own parents. I'm glad I'm not alone in this. Jeff's every sentence and every move showed that he was not living in a good way, even though he has a father who sometimes shows him blinding luxury. He was just a street kid like me. Do you live there alone? I asked. He just nodded silently. He has friends, he said, but he prefers to be alone. He also said that he is very ashamed, but sometimes he does not remember everything that happens to him. It rarely happens, but it's quite strange, but I don't have to worry about it at all, because it's not sick, only sometimes strange things happen in life, like, say, someone's mother dies. I decided to believe him, I didn't want to push the matter anyway, if he's sick, that's enough trouble for him, he doesn't need a stranger to bother him with that. It's there! He pointed as he kicked a medium-sized pebble in the direction of the lot. Isn't it beautiful? He asked. It is full of trees, like a small forest. He was right about that too. Jeff was indeed a little strange, but I was extremely attracted to this strangeness in him, I felt that he was not for nothing proud of it. We were the same age, but maybe he was a bit more mature than his age. He was barely 14, yet he was a head taller than me, which was not common among teenagers at that age. You can catch fire, he said, as if there was nothing special about it, he took off his T-shirt and threw it over his shoulder. His young, muscular body was like a chessboard. I see you're out and about a lot. I praised it because it was baked as brown in the summer sun as if it were noon. When I can, he replied, then acknowledged with a confused smile that I liked what I saw. I liked his muscles. He didn't put it on in a gym, but life brought it on him. He jumped over a fence and then opened a gate. It's not like I couldn't jump, but it was considerate of him to graciously help me get in. No one has really opened the door for me yet, even though everyone knows it's appropriate for a girl. Dense vegetation flourished inside. We had to fight our way through bushes, while the terrain rose all the way. Finally, I saw a huge tree with branches as thick as the waist of my mysterious leader, who was running higher and higher like a gazelle. When I came home, I didn't know what to do. I wanted to please the strange boy, so I wore sandals, which didn't turn out to be a good choice. I suddenly tripped over a stone, but he, who knows how, jumped back a few meters in an instant to catch me, and after that, holding my hand, he led me firmly under the huge oak, only to pull me up to the first branches after a few moments, from where we climbed five meters up and rested on a comfortable wooden platform. I have a secret to tell you, he said. When I first saw you, I didn't know where I was taking you. Then I remembered this site and thought I could build you a beautiful leafy house on the tree. When you gave me your number last week, I was very happy for you, I wanted to see you again, and I couldn't wait for it to be finished, so I only have these few wooden pallets left. I couldn't hold it in. You can sit now, but it will be much nicer when it is ready. His eyes were sparkling, his messy hair made this serious offer completely frivolous. I almost cried. I met an unknown boy, with whom we exchanged a few words, but he immediately wanted to build me a tree house. I pulled her face to mine and gave her a kiss. Beautiful pallors, I praised the building. I burrowed myself between his arms so that we could comfortably hang our legs against the trunk of the tree. Even though it was thirty degrees, he didn't sweat during the long walk, his skin exuded a pleasant scent. I leaned my head on his shoulder while he held my waist tightly. Maybe he was afraid I would fall. The sight of the city was revealed before us, which was magical in the sparkling sunshine. But he was busy with something else. Jeff's pants were unmistakably bulging next to me, and even though he wasn't paying attention, I couldn't take my eyes off him. I felt it made everything even more romantic. I kissed his bare chest while placing my left hand over his lap. He looked at me with a cheerful smile. What do you want? He asked innocently. I could see that he had never experienced anything like this before. That made me smile too. We stared into each other's eyes for a while. I didn't want to tell him anything. I unbuttoned his pants. His groin was finally released from its strangling cuffs. She wasn't wearing underwear, which I could tell was the hot summer. Jeff just smiled. He slid his left hand to my bottom and slowly gripped it. I kissed all over his stomach, starting from his chest. We could have played a game of chess on it. I covered the tightly swollen sack with my left palm and began to gently stimulate it with slow movements. This made his masculinity rise even higher, which I tasted with tender kisses. He moved harder with every touch I made. Soft moans erupted from his hoarse throat. Between kisses, I let him go deeper into my mouth. We really enjoyed it. I was glad to give something in return for the arbor and for thinking of me. I was happy. I counted on him that much. I have tried to show you some of what you will soon have to know. It was good that I could introduce you to this new world. He was still a boy, I liked that he was like that. I treated him gently throughout. I didn't want her to think it could be any other way. I didn't want him to be ruined by the corrupt world. He said he couldn't take it anymore. I highlighted from the number. I felt that I would spoil her now if I let her inside me, so she enjoyed herself with the help of accelerated movements. Thank you for the house! I whispered in his ear and kissed his lips lovingly.

chapters.title: Chapter Thirty-Nine
paragraphs: It was over. I knew it was over the moment Mortenstein lost his impeccable confidence after reading the letter. Jake Cooper is the son of Edgar Mortenstein. My goodness, what news. The man who had ruled this country for a decade and a half was now on a straight path to prison. He knew there was something wrong.

Anton sat over the pictures for hours. He didn't say anything, he didn't bother me. He couldn't let go of the thought that everything had significance. He knew the boy shouldn't be in the picture, so he disappears. He knew he smelled something, and since he never gave up on a puzzle, he realized that the boy was Jake Cooper. Anton really acted like a detective. He wasn't a detective, he was more of an old-time detective. He is the main character in a book he wrote.

"Dear court!" I confidently began the closing speech. "In the next quarter of an hour, I will summarize how President Edgar Mortenstein ran the underworld network called Neon Nights, how he built the alternate world that served to cover it up, and how he used his son, Jake Cooper, to help run it."
(Amanda Evans' closing speech)

The story began sometime at the turn of the 2010s. Edgar Mortenstein came to politics from the business world, so he always had a keen eye for profitable opportunities. Upon his election, he acquired such influence that he was assured that whatever he embarked on in life, he would be crowned with success. In his mind, he formulated the structure of a brand with which he could express himself. The name Neon Nights reminds us of a bygone world where politics and business were still conducted according to the rules of modern patriarchy. He came from this world. He was young then. The idea of ​​Neon Nights was born in his mind, which took us back to the past.

As revealed in the police report compiled yesterday; Edgar Mortenstein had an affair with a prostitute in the nineties, while he was considered a successful businessman in the energy sector. I don't want to speculate on how many times they might have met at that time, but they must have met quite a few times, although over time not only for the pleasure of Edgar Mortenstein, because Jake Cooper, the son of Edgar Mortenstein and Elizabeth Cooper, was born from this relationship. Let's imagine how much Mortenstein at the time must have wanted this child. But what if Miss Cooper didn't want to abort the baby? However, in addition to his increasingly frequent political roles, the family, which he became a part of unwittingly, began to become really unpleasant for him. How did Elizabeth Cooper blackmail Edgar Mortenstein? Surely yes, because what else could have tied the millionaire entrepreneur to him? Miss Cooper certainly did not want to let go of the inexhaustible money bag, who eventually got tired of the constant harassment, so after her election, her first task was to clear the increasingly dangerous woman out of the way. These, although not closely related to the discussed accusations, are the facts themselves. In the past day and a half, we found the record of Elizabeth Cooper's death, in which case the investigation was also concluded with stormy speed. Is it just a surprising similarity, or a coincidence that fits the patterns of later cases? Based on the name of the police officer who conducted the investigation, we can confidently lean towards the latter, since Ross Wilson's handiwork was also praised in this report.

But why do we need to talk about Edgar Mortenstein and Elizabeth Cooper's relationship aside from Jake Cooper? Because I am convinced that this past was the indicator of the construction of a network of prostitutes, which the name of Neon Nights shrouded in an all-covering fog, even more so by the fact that it appeared again and again in different contexts.
